title: Summary
markdown:
[←5.6. The Complete Definitions](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Complete-Definitions/#complete-definitions "5.6. The Complete Definitions")[6. Monad Transformers→](Monad-Transformers/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers "6. Monad Transformers")
#  5.7. Summary[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=structure-applicative-monad-summary "Permalink")
##  5.7.1. Type Classes and Structures
Behind the scenes, type classes are represented by structures. Defining a class defines a structure, and additionally creates an empty table of instances. Defining an instance creates a value that either has the structure as its type or is a function that can return the structure, and additionally adds an entry to the table. Instance search consists of constructing an instance by consulting the instance tables. Both structures and classes may provide default values for fields (which are default implementations of methods).
##  5.7.2. Structures and Inheritance
Structures may inherit from other structures. Behind the scenes, a structure that inherits from another structure contains an instance of the original structure as a field. In other words, inheritance is implemented with composition. When multiple inheritance is used, only the unique fields from the additional parent structures are used to avoid a diamond problem, and the functions that would normally extract the parent value are instead organized to construct one. Record dot notation takes structure inheritance into account.
Because type classes are just structures with some additional automation applied, all of these features are available in type classes. Together with default methods, this can be used to create a fine-grained hierarchy of interfaces that nonetheless does not impose a large burden on clients, because the small classes that the large classes inherit from can be automatically implemented.
##  5.7.3. Applicative Functors
An applicative functor is a functor with two additional operations:
  * `pure`, which is the same operator as that for `Monad`
  * `seq`, which allows a function to be applied in the context of the functor.


While monads can represent arbitrary programs with control flow, applicative functors can only run function arguments from left to right. Because they are less powerful, they provide less control to programs written against the interface, while the implementor of the method has a greater degree of freedom. Some useful types can implement `Applicative` but not `Monad`.
In fact, the type classes `Functor`, `Applicative`, and `Monad` form a hierarchy of power. Moving up the hierarchy, from `Functor` towards `Monad`, allows more powerful programs to be written, but fewer types implement the more powerful classes. Polymorphic programs should be written to use as weak of an abstraction as possible, while datatypes should be given instances that are as powerful as possible. This maximizes code re-use. The more powerful type classes extend the less powerful ones, which means that an implementation of `Monad` provides implementations of `Functor` and `Applicative` for free.
Each class has a set of methods to be implemented and a corresponding contract that specifies additional rules for the methods. Programs that are written against these interfaces expect that the additional rules are followed, and may be buggy if they are not. The default implementations of `Functor`'s methods in terms of `Applicative`'s, and of `Applicative`'s in terms of `Monad`'s, will obey these rules.
##  5.7.4. Universes
To allow Lean to be used as both a programming language and a theorem prover, some restrictions on the language are necessary. This includes restrictions on recursive functions that ensure that they all either terminate or are marked as `partial` and written to return types that are not uninhabited. Additionally, it must be impossible to represent certain kinds of logical paradoxes as types.
One of the restrictions that rules out certain paradoxes is that every type is assigned to a _universe_. Universes are types such as `Prop`, `Type`, `Type 1`, `Type 2`, and so forth. These types describe other types—just as `0` and `17` are described by `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is itself described by `Type`, and `Type` is described by `Type 1`. The type of functions that take a type as an argument must be a larger universe than the argument's universe.
Because each declared datatype has a universe, writing code that uses types like data would quickly become annoying, requiring each polymorphic type to be copy-pasted to take arguments from `Type 1`. A feature called _universe polymorphism_ allows Lean programs and datatypes to take universe levels as arguments, just as ordinary polymorphism allows programs to take types as arguments. Generally speaking, Lean libraries should use universe polymorphism when implementing libraries of polymorphic operations. 
[←5.6. The Complete Definitions](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Complete-Definitions/#complete-definitions "5.6. The Complete Definitions")[6. Monad Transformers→](Monad-Transformers/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers "6. Monad Transformers")

title: The Applicative Contract
markdown:
[←5.2. Applicative Functors](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Applicative-Functors/#applicative "5.2. Applicative Functors")[5.4. Alternatives→](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Alternatives/#alternative "5.4. Alternatives")
#  5.3. The Applicative Contract[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=applicative-laws "Permalink")
Just like `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")`, and types that implement `[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Hashable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Hashable___mk "Documentation for Hashable in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` has a set of rules that all instances should adhere to.
There are four rules that an applicative functor should follow:
  1. It should respect identity, so `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") id <*> v = v`
  2. It should respect function composition, so `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> u <*> v <*> w = u <*> (v <*> w)`
  3. Sequencing pure operations should be a no-op, so `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x``=``[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f x)`
  4. The ordering of pure operations doesn't matter, so `u <*> [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x = [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (fun f => f x) <*> u`


To check these for the `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference") [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` instance, start by expanding `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` into `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")`.
The first rule states that `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") id <*> v = v`. The definition of `seq` for `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` states that this is the same as `id <$> v = v`, which is one of the `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` rules that have already been checked.
The second rule states that `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> u <*> v <*> w = u <*> (v <*> w)`. If any of `u`, `v`, or `w` is `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, then both sides are `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, so the property holds. Assuming that `u` is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f`, that `v` is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g`, and that `w` is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x`, then this is equivalent to saying that `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x = [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x)`. Evaluating the two sides yields the same result:
`[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f ∘ ·) <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f ∘ g) <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ((f ∘ g) x)``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f (g x))`
`[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") g <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x)``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (g x))``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f (g x))`
The third rule follows directly from the definition of `seq`:
`[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x``f <$> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f x)`
In the fourth case, assume that `u` is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f`, because if it's `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, both sides of the equation are `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`. `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x` evaluates directly to `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (f x)`, as does `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (fun g => g x) <*> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f`.
##  5.3.1. All Applicatives are Functors[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=applicatives-are-functors "Permalink")
The two operators for `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` are enough to define `map`:
`def map [[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference") f] (g : α → β) (x : f α) : f β :=   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") g <*> x`
This can only be used to implement `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` if the contract for `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` guarantees the contract for `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, however. The first rule of `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` is that `id <$> x = x`, which follows directly from the first rule for `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")`. The second rule of `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` is that `map (f ∘ g) x = map f (map g x)`. Unfolding the definition of `map` here results in `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f ∘ g) <*> x = [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f <*> ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") g <*> x)`. Using the rule that sequencing pure operations is a no-op, the left side can be rewritten to `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f <*> [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") g <*> x`. This is an instance of the rule that states that applicative functors respect function composition.
This justifies a definition of `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` that extends `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, with a default definition of `[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")` given in terms of `pure` and `seq`:
`class Applicative (f : Type → Type) extends [Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference") f where   pure : α → f α   seq : f (α → β) → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → f α) → f β   [map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") g x := seq (pure g) (fun () => x)`
##  5.3.2. All Monads are Applicative Functors[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-are-applicative "Permalink")
An instance of `Monad` already requires an implementation of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`. Together with `bind`, this is enough to define `seq`:
`def seq [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : m (α → β)) (x : [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → m α) : m β := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let g ← f   let y ← x ()   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`
Once again, checking that the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` contract implies the `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` contract will allow this to be used as a default definition for `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")` if `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` extends `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")`.
The rest of this section consists of an argument that this implementation of `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")` based on `bind` in fact satisfies the `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` contract. One of the beautiful things about functional programming is that this kind of argument can be worked out on a piece of paper with a pencil, using the kinds of evaluation rules from [the initial section on evaluating expressions](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Evaluating-Expressions/#evaluating). Thinking about the meanings of the operations while reading these arguments can sometimes help with understanding.
Replacing `do`-notation with explicit uses of `>>=` makes it easier to apply the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` rules:
`def seq [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : m (α → β)) (x : [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → m α) : m β := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   f >>= fun g =>   x () >>= fun y =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`
To check that this definition respects identity, check that `seq ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") id) (fun () => v) = v`. The left hand side is equivalent to `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") id >>= fun g => (fun () => v) () >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`. The unit function in the middle can be eliminated immediately, yielding `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") id >>= fun g => v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`. Using the fact that `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`, this is the same as `v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (id y)`, which is `v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") y`. Because `fun x => f x` is the same as `f`, this is the same as `v >>= [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`, and the fact that `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a right identity of `>>=` can be used to get `v`.
This kind of informal reasoning can be made easier to read with a bit of reformatting. In the following chart, read “`EXPR1 ={ REASON }= EXPR2`” as “`EXPR1` is the same as `EXPR2` because `REASON`”:
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") id >>= fun g => v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (id y)`
Reduce the call to `id`
`v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") y`
`fun x => f x` is the same as `f`
`v >>= [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a right identity of `>>=`
`v`
To check that it respects function composition, check that `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) <*> u <*> v <*> w = u <*> (v <*> w)`. The first step is to replace `<*>` with this definition of `seq`. After that, a (somewhat long) series of steps that use the identity and associativity rules from the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` contract is enough to get from one to the other:
`seq (seq (seq ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·)) (fun _ => u))       (fun _ => v))   (fun _ => w)`
Definition of `seq`
`(([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (· ∘ ·) >>= fun f =>    u >>= fun x =>    [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f x)) >>= fun g =>   v >>= fun y =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)) >>= fun h =>  w >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`((u >>= fun x =>    [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x ∘ ·)) >>= fun g =>    v >>= fun y =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)) >>= fun h =>  w >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
Insertion of parentheses for clarity
`((u >>= fun x =>    [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x ∘ ·)) >>= (fun g =>    v >>= fun y =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y))) >>= fun h =>  w >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
Associativity of `>>=`
`(u >>= fun x =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x ∘ ·) >>= fun g =>  v  >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)) >>= fun h =>  w >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`(u >>= fun x =>   v >>= fun y =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x ∘ y)) >>= fun h =>  w >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
Associativity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun x => v >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x ∘ y) >>= fun h => w >>= fun z => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h z)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun x => v >>= fun y => w >>= fun z => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ((x ∘ y) z)`
Definition of function composition
`u >>= fun x => v >>= fun y => w >>= fun z => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x (y z))`
Time to start moving backwards! `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun x => v >>= fun y => w >>= fun z => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (y z) >>= fun q => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x q)`
Associativity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun x => v >>= fun y =>  (w >>= fun p =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (y p)) >>= fun q =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x q)`
Associativity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun x =>  (v >>= fun y =>   w >>= fun q =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (y q)) >>= fun z =>  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x z)`
This includes the definition of `seq`
`u >>= fun x => seq v (fun () => w) >>= fun q => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x q)`
This also includes the definition of `seq`
`seq u (fun () => seq v (fun () => w))`
To check that sequencing pure operations is a no-op:
`seq ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f) (fun () => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x)`
Replacing `seq` with its definition
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f >>= fun g => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") f >>= fun g => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g x)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f x)`
And finally, to check that the ordering of pure operations doesn't matter:
`seq u (fun () => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x)`
Definition of `seq`
`u >>= fun f => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f y)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`u >>= fun f => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f x)`
Clever replacement of one expression by an equivalent one that makes the rule match
`u >>= fun f => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ((fun g => g x) f)`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a left identity of `>>=`
`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (fun g => g x) >>= fun h => u >>= fun f => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (h f)`
Definition of `seq`
`seq ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (fun f => f x)) (fun () => u)`
This justifies a definition of `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` that extends `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")`, with a default definition of `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")`:
`class Monad (m : Type → Type) extends [Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference") m where   bind : m α → (α → m β) → m β   [seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference") f x :=     bind f fun g =>     bind (x ()) fun y =>     pure (g y)`
`[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")`'s own default definition of `[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")` means that every `Monad` instance automatically generates `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` instances as well.
##  5.3.3. Additional Stipulations[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=additional-stipulations "Permalink")
In addition to adhering to the individual contracts associated with each type class, combined implementations `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` should work equivalently to these default implementations. In other words, a type that provides both `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instances should not have an implementation of `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")` that works differently from the version that the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance generates as a default implementation. This is important because polymorphic functions may be refactored to replace a use of `>>=` with an equivalent use of `<*>`, or a use of `<*>` with an equivalent use of `>>=`. This refactoring should not change the meaning of programs that use this code.
This rule explains why `Validate.andThen` should not be used to implement `bind` in a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance. On its own, it obeys the monad contract. However, when it is used to implement `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")`, the behavior is not equivalent to `[seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")` itself. To see where they differ, take the example of two computations, both of which return errors. Start with an example of a case where two errors should be returned, one from validating a function (which could have just as well resulted from a prior argument to the function), and one from validating an argument:
`def notFun : Validate [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) :=   .errors { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := "First error", [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := [] }  def notArg : Validate [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") :=   .errors { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := "Second error", [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := [] }`
Combining them with the version of `<*>` from `Validate`'s `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")` instance results in both errors being reported to the user:

"Definition of example") := "First error",   [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := ["Second error"] }`
Using the version of `seq` that was implemented with `>>=`, here rewritten to `andThen`, results in only the first error being available:
`seq [notFun](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notFun "Definition of example") (fun () => [notArg](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notArg "Definition of example"))``[notFun](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notFun "Definition of example").andThen fun g => [notArg](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notArg "Definition of example").andThen fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)``[match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [notFun](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notFun "Definition of example") [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") | .errors errs => .errors errs | [.ok](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Applicative-Functors/#Validate___ok "Definition of example") val =>   (fun g =>     [notArg](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Applicative-Contract/#SeqCounterexample___notArg "Definition of example").andThen fun y =>     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (g y)) val``.errors { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := "First error", [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := [] }`

title: The Complete Definitions
markdown:
[←5.5. Universes](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Universes/#universe-levels "5.5. Universes")[5.7. Summary→](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Summary/#structure-applicative-monad-summary "5.7. Summary")
#  5.6. The Complete Definitions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=complete-definitions "Permalink")
Now that all the relevant language features have been presented, this section describes the complete, honest definitions of `Functor`, `Applicative`, and `Monad` as they occur in the Lean standard library. For the sake of understanding, no details are omitted.
##  5.6.1. Functor[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=complete-functor-definition "Permalink")
The complete definition of the `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` class makes use of universe polymorphism and a default method implementation:
`class Functor (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   map : {α β : Type u} → (α → β) → f α → f β   mapConst : {α β : Type u} → α → f β → f α :=     [Function.comp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___comp "Documentation for Function.comp in Lean Language Reference") map ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _)`
In this definition, `[Function.comp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___comp "Documentation for Function.comp in Lean Language Reference")` is function composition, which is typically written with the `∘` operator. `[Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference")` is the _constant function_ , which is a two-argument function that ignores its second argument. Applying this function to only one argument produces a function that always returns the same value, which is useful when an API demands a function but a program doesn't need to compute different results for different arguments. A simple version of `[Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference")` can be written as follows:
`def simpleConst  (x : α) (_ : β) : α := x`
Using it with one argument as the function argument to `List.map` demonstrates its utility:
``[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"same"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") "same"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") "same"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [1, 2, 3].[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") ([simpleConst](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/The-Complete-Definitions/#simpleConst "Definition of example") "same")`
```
[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"same"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") "same"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") "same"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")
```

The actual function has the following signature:

```
[Function.const.{u, v}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") {α : Sort u} (β : Sort v) (a : α) : β → α
```

Here, the type argument `β` is an explicit argument, so the default definition of `mapConst` provides an `_` argument that instructs Lean to find a unique type to pass to `[Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference")` that would cause the program to type check. `[Function.comp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___comp "Documentation for Function.comp in Lean Language Reference") map ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _)` is equivalent to `fun (x : α) (y : f β) => map (fun _ => x) y`.
The `Functor` type class inhabits a universe that is the greater of `u+1` and `v`. Here, `u` is the level of universes accepted as arguments to `f`, while `v` is the universe returned by `f`. To see why the structure that implements the `Functor` type class must be in a universe that's larger than `u`, begin with a simplified definition of the class:
`class Functor (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   map : {α β : Type u} → (α → β) → f α → f β`
This type class's structure type is equivalent to the following inductive type:
`inductive Functor (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   | mk : ({α β : Type u} → (α → β) → f α → f β) → Functor f`
The implementation of the `map` method that is passed as an argument to `mk` contains a function that takes two types in `Type u` as arguments. This means that the type of the function itself is in `Type (u+1)`, so `Functor` must also be at a level that is at least `u+1`. Similarly, other arguments to the function have a type built by applying `f`, so it must also have a level that is at least `v`. All the type classes in this section share this property.
##  5.6.2. Applicative[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=complete-applicative-definition "Permalink")
The `Applicative` type class is actually built from a number of smaller classes that each contain some of the relevant methods. The first are `Pure` and `Seq`, which contain `pure` and `seq` respectively:
`class Pure (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   pure {α : Type u} : α → f α``class Seq (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   seq : {α β : Type u} → f (α → β) → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → f α) → f β`
In addition to these, `Applicative` also depends on `SeqRight` and an analogous `SeqLeft` class:
`class SeqRight (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   seqRight : {α β : Type u} → f α → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → f β) → f β``class SeqLeft (f : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v) where   seqLeft : {α β : Type u} → f α → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → f β) → f α`
The `seqRight` function, which was introduced in the [section about alternatives and validation](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Alternatives/#alternative), is easiest to understand from the perspective of effects. `E1 *> E2`, which desugars to `[SeqRight.seqRight](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#SeqRight___mk "Documentation for SeqRight.seqRight in Lean Language Reference") E1 (fun () => E2)`, can be understood as first executing `E1`, and then `E2`, resulting only in `E2`'s result. Effects from `E1` may result in `E2` not being run, or being run multiple times. Indeed, if `f` has a `Monad` instance, then `E1 *> E2` is equivalent to `do let _ ← E1; E2`, but `seqRight` can be used with types like `Validate` that are not monads.
Its cousin `seqLeft` is very similar, except the leftmost expression's value is returned. `E1 <* E2` desugars to `[SeqLeft.seqLeft](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#SeqLeft___mk "Documentation for SeqLeft.seqLeft in Lean Language Reference") E1 (fun () => E2)`. `[SeqLeft.seqLeft](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#SeqLeft___mk "Documentation for SeqLeft.seqLeft in Lean Language Reference")` has type `f α → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → f β) → f α`, which is identical to that of `seqRight` except for the fact that it returns `f α`. `E1 <* E2` can be understood as a program that first executes `E1`, and then `E2`, returning the original result for `E1`. If `f` has a `Monad` instance, then `E1 <* E2` is equivalent to `do let x ← E1; _ ← E2; pure x`. Generally speaking, `seqLeft` is useful for specifying extra conditions on a value in a validation or parser-like workflow without changing the value itself.
The definition of `Applicative` extends all these classes, along with `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`:
`class Applicative (f : Type u → Type v)     extends [Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference") f, Pure f, Seq f, SeqLeft f, SeqRight f where   [map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")      := fun x y => Seq.seq (pure x) fun _ => y   seqLeft  := fun a b => Seq.seq ([Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _) a) b   seqRight := fun a b => Seq.seq ([Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _ id) a) b`
A complete definition of `Applicative` requires only definitions for `pure` and `seq`. This is because there are default definitions for all of the methods from `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, `SeqLeft`, and `SeqRight`. The `mapConst` method of `Functor` has its own default implementation in terms of `[Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")`. These default implementations should only be overridden with new functions that are behaviorally equivalent, but more efficient. The default implementations should be seen as specifications for correctness as well as automatically-created code.
The default implementation for `seqLeft` is very compact. Replacing some of the names with their syntactic sugar or their definitions can provide another view on it, so:
`Seq.seq ([Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _) a) b`
becomes
`fun a b => Seq.seq ((fun x _ => x) <$> a) b`
How should `(fun x _ => x) <$> a` be understood? Here, `a` has type `f α`, and `f` is a functor. If `f` is `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`, then `(fun x _ => x) <$> [1, 2, 3]` evaluates to `[fun _ => 1, fun _ => 2, fun _ => 3`. If `f` is `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, then `(fun x _ => x) <$> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") "hello"` evaluates to `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (fun _ => "hello")`. In each case, the values in the functor are replaced by functions that return the original value, ignoring their argument. When combined with `seq`, this function discards the values from `seq`'s second argument.
The default implementation for `seqRight` is very similar, except `[Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference")` has an additional argument `id`. This definition can be understood similarly, by first introducing some standard syntactic sugar and then replacing some names with their definitions:
`fun a b => Seq.seq ([Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") ([Function.const](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___const "Documentation for Function.const in Lean Language Reference") _ id) a) b``fun a b => Seq.seq ((fun _ => id) <$> a) b``fun a b => Seq.seq ((fun _ => fun x => x) <$> a) b``fun a b => Seq.seq ((fun _ x => x) <$> a) b`
How should `(fun _ x => x) <$> a` be understood? Once again, examples are useful. `fun _ x => x) <$> [1, 2, 3]` is equivalent to `[fun x => x, fun x => x, fun x => x]`, and `(fun _ x => x) <$> [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") "hello"` is equivalent to `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (fun x => x)`. In other words, `(fun _ x => x) <$> a` preserves the overall shape of `a`, but each value is replaced by the identity function. From the perspective of effects, the side effects of `a` occur, but the values are thrown out when it is used with `seq`.
##  5.6.3. Monad[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=complete-monad-definition "Permalink")
Just as the constituent operations of `Applicative` are split into their own type classes, `Bind` has its own class as well:
`class Bind (m : Type u → Type v) where   bind : {α β : Type u} → m α → (α → m β) → m β`
`Monad` extends `Applicative` with `Bind`:
`class Monad (m : Type u → Type v) : Type (max (u+1) v)     extends [Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference") m, Bind m where   [map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")      f x := bind x ([Function.comp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/The-Type-System/Functions/#Function___comp "Documentation for Function.comp in Lean Language Reference") pure f)   [seq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Seq___mk "Documentation for Seq.seq in Lean Language Reference")      f x := bind f fun y => [Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference") y (x ())   [seqLeft](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#SeqLeft___mk "Documentation for SeqLeft.seqLeft in Lean Language Reference")  x y := bind x fun a => bind (y ()) (fun _ => pure a)   [seqRight](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#SeqRight___mk "Documentation for SeqRight.seqRight in Lean Language Reference") x y := bind x fun _ => y ()`
Tracing the collection of inherited methods and default methods from the entire hierarchy shows that a `Monad` instance requires only implementations of `bind` and `pure`. In other words, `Monad` instances automatically yield implementations of `seq`, `seqLeft`, `seqRight`, `map`, and `mapConst`. From the perspective of API boundaries, any type with a `Monad` instance gets instances for `Bind`, `Pure`, `Seq`, `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")`, `SeqLeft`, and `SeqRight`.
##  5.6.4. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=complete-functor-applicative-monad-exercises "Permalink")
  1. Understand the default implementations of `map`, `seq`, `seqLeft`, and `seqRight` in `Monad` by working through examples such as `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`. In other words, substitute their definitions for `bind` and `pure` into the default definitions, and simplify them to recover the versions `map`, `seq`, `seqLeft`, and `seqRight` that would be written by hand.
  2. On paper or in a text file, prove to yourself that the default implementations of `map` and `seq` satisfy the contracts for `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` and `Applicative`. In this argument, you're allowed to use the rules from the `Monad` contract as well as ordinary expression evaluation. 

[←5.5. Universes](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Universes/#universe-levels "5.5. Universes")[5.7. Summary→](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Summary/#structure-applicative-monad-summary "5.7. Summary")
