title: Running a Program
markdown:
[←2. Hello, World!](Hello___-World___/#hello-world "2. Hello, World!")[2.2. Step By Step→](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#step-by-step "2.2. Step By Step")
#  2.1. Running a Program[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=running-a-program "Permalink")
The simplest way to run a Lean program is to use the `--run` option to the Lean executable. Create a file called `Hello.lean` and enter the following contents:
`def main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Hello, world!" `
Then, from the command line, run:
``$ `lean --run Hello.lean`
The program displays `Hello, world!` and exits.
##  2.1.1. Anatomy of a Greeting[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=hello-world-parts "Permalink")
When Lean is invoked with the `--run` option, it invokes the program's `main` definition. In programs that do not take command-line arguments, `main` should have type `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`. This means that `main` is not a function, because there are no arrows (`→`) in its type. Instead of being a function that has side effects, `main` consists of a description of effects to be carried out.
As discussed in [the preceding chapter](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Polymorphism/#polymorphism), `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` is the simplest inductive type. It has a single constructor called `[unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit___unit "Documentation for Unit.unit in Lean Language Reference")` that takes no arguments. Languages in the C tradition have a notion of a `void` function that does not return any value at all. In Lean, all functions take an argument and return a value, and the lack of interesting arguments or return values can be signaled by using the `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` type instead. If `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` represents a single bit of information, `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` represents zero bits of information.
`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α` is the type of a program that, when executed, will either throw an exception or return a value of type `α`. During execution, this program may have side effects. These programs are referred to as `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` _actions_. Lean distinguishes between _evaluation_ of expressions, which strictly adheres to the mathematical model of substitution of values for variables and reduction of sub-expressions without side effects, and _execution_ of `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions, which rely on an external system to interact with the world. `[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference")` is a function from strings to `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that, when executed, write the given string to standard output. Because this action doesn't read any interesting information from the environment in the process of emitting the string, `[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference")` has type `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`. If it did return something interesting, then that would be indicated by the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action having a type other than `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`.
##  2.1.2. Functional Programming vs Effects[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=fp-effects "Permalink")
Lean's model of computation is based on the evaluation of mathematical expressions, in which variables are given exactly one value that does not change over time. The result of evaluating an expression does not change, and evaluating the same expression again will always yield the same result.
On the other hand, useful programs must interact with the world. A program that performs neither input nor output can't ask a user for data, create files on disk, or open network connections. Lean is written in itself, and the Lean compiler certainly reads files, creates files, and interacts with text editors. How can a language in which the same expression always yields the same result support programs that read files from disk, when the contents of these files might change over time?
This apparent contradiction can be resolved by thinking a bit differently about side effects. Imagine a café that sells coffee and sandwiches. This café has two employees: a cook who fulfills orders, and a worker at the counter who interacts with customers and places order slips. The cook is a surly person, who really prefers not to have any contact with the world outside, but who is very good at consistently delivering the food and drinks that the café is known for. In order to do this, however, the cook needs peace and quiet, and can't be disturbed with conversation. The counter worker is friendly, but completely incompetent in the kitchen. Customers interact with the counter worker, who delegates all actual cooking to the cook. If the cook has a question for a customer, such as clarifying an allergy, they send a little note to the counter worker, who interacts with the customer and passes a note back to the cook with the result.
In this analogy, the cook is the Lean language. When provided with an order, the cook faithfully and consistently delivers what is requested. The counter worker is the surrounding run-time system that interacts with the world and can accept payments, dispense food, and have conversations with customers. Working together, the two employees serve all the functions of the restaurant, but their responsibilities are divided, with each performing the tasks that they're best at. Just as keeping customers away allows the cook to focus on making truly excellent coffee and sandwiches, Lean's lack of side effects allows programs to be used as part of formal mathematical proofs. It also helps programmers understand the parts of the program in isolation from each other, because there are no hidden state changes that create subtle coupling between components. The cook's notes represent `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that are produced by evaluating Lean expressions, and the counter worker's replies are the values that are passed back from effects.
This model of side effects is quite similar to how the overall aggregate of the Lean language, its compiler, and its run-time system (RTS) work. Primitives in the run-time system, written in C, implement all the basic effects. When running a program, the RTS invokes the `main` action, which returns new `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions to the RTS for execution. The RTS executes these actions, delegating to the user's Lean code to carry out computations. From the internal perspective of Lean, programs are free of side effects, and `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions are just descriptions of tasks to be carried out. From the external perspective of the program's user, there is a layer of side effects that create an interface to the program's core logic.
##  2.1.3. Real-World Functional Programming[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=fp-world-passing "Permalink")
The other useful way to think about side effects in Lean is by considering `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions to be functions that take the entire world as an argument and return a value paired with a new world. In this case, reading a line of text from standard input _is_ a pure function, because a different world is provided as an argument each time. Writing a line of text to standard output is a pure function, because the world that the function returns is different from the one that it began with. Programs do need to be careful to never re-use the world, nor to fail to return a new world—this would amount to time travel or the end of the world, after all. Careful abstraction boundaries can make this style of programming safe. If every primitive `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action accepts one world and returns a new one, and they can only be combined with tools that preserve this invariant, then the problem cannot occur.
This model cannot be implemented. After all, the entire universe cannot be turned into a Lean value and placed into memory. However, it is possible to implement a variation of this model with an abstract token that stands for the world. When the program is started, it is provided with a world token. This token is then passed on to the IO primitives, and their returned tokens are similarly passed to the next step. At the end of the program, the token is returned to the operating system.
This model of side effects is a good description of how `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions as descriptions of tasks to be carried out by the RTS are represented internally in Lean. The actual functions that transform the real world are behind an abstraction barrier. But real programs typically consist of a sequence of effects, rather than just one. To enable programs to use multiple effects, there is a sub-language of Lean called `do` notation that allows these primitive `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions to be safely composed into a larger, useful program.
##  2.1.4. Combining `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` Actions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=combining-io-actions "Permalink")
Most useful programs accept input in addition to producing output. Furthermore, they may take decisions based on input, using the input data as part of a computation. The following program, called `HelloName.lean`, asks the user for their name and then greets them:
`def main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")   let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")    stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?"   let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")   let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference")    stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
In this program, the `main` action consists of a `do` block. This block contains a sequence of _statements_ , which can be both local variables (introduced using `let`) and actions that are to be executed. Just as SQL can be thought of as a special-purpose language for interacting with databases, the `do` syntax can be thought of as a special-purpose sub-language within Lean that is dedicated to modeling imperative programs. `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that are built with a `do` block are executed by executing the statements in order.
This program can be run in the same manner as the prior program:
``$ `lean --run HelloName.lean`
If the user responds with `David`, a session of interaction with the program reads:

```
How would you like to be addressed?
David
Hello, David!

```

The type signature line is just like the one for `Hello.lean`:
`def main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")`
The only difference is that it ends with the keyword `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")`, which initiates a sequence of commands. Each indented line following the keyword `do` is part of the same sequence of commands.
The first two lines, which read:
`let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference") let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")`
retrieve the `stdin` and `stdout` handles by executing the library actions `[IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")` and `[IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")`, respectively. In a `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block, `let` has a slightly different meaning than in an ordinary expression. Ordinarily, the local definition in a `let` can be used in just one expression, which immediately follows the local definition. In a `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block, local bindings introduced by `let` are available in all statements in the remainder of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block, rather than just the next one. Additionally, `let` typically connects the name being defined to its definition using `:=`, while some `let` bindings in `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` use a left arrow (`←` or `<-`) instead. Using an arrow means that the value of the expression is an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that should be executed, with the result of the action saved in the local variable. In other words, if the expression to the right of the arrow has type `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α`, then the variable has type `α` in the remainder of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block. `[IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")` and `[IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")` are `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions in order to allow `stdin` and `stdout` to be locally overridden in a program, which can be convenient. If they were global variables as in C, then there would be no meaningful way to override them, but `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions can return different values each time they are executed.
The next part of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block is responsible for asking the user for their name:
`stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?" let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference") let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference")`
The first line writes the question to `stdout`, the second line requests input from `stdin`, and the third line removes the trailing newline (plus any other trailing whitespace) from the input line. The definition of `name` uses `:=`, rather than `←`, because `[String.dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference")` is an ordinary function on strings, rather than an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action.
Finally, the last line in the program is:
`stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
It uses [string interpolation](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Additional-Conveniences/#string-interpolation) to insert the provided name into a greeting string, writing the result to `stdout`. 
[←2. Hello, World!](Hello___-World___/#hello-world "2. Hello, World!")[2.2. Step By Step→](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#step-by-step "2.2. Step By Step")

title: Starting a Project
markdown:
[←2.2. Step By Step](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#step-by-step "2.2. Step By Step")[2.4. Worked Example: cat→](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#example-cat "2.4. Worked Example: cat")
#  2.3. Starting a Project[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=starting-a-project "Permalink")
As a program written in Lean becomes more serious, an ahead-of-time compiler-based workflow that results in an executable becomes more attractive. Like other languages, Lean has tools for building multiple-file packages and managing dependencies. The standard Lean build tool is called Lake (short for “Lean Make”). Lake is typically configured using a TOML file that declaratively specifies dependencies and describes what is to be built. For advanced use cases, Lake can also be configured in Lean itself.
##  2.3.1. First steps[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=lake-new "Permalink")
To get started with a project that uses Lake, use the command `lake new greeting` in a directory that does not already contain a file or directory called `greeting`. This creates a directory called `greeting` that contains the following files:
  * `Main.lean` is the file in which the Lean compiler will look for the `main` action.
  * `Greeting.lean` and `Greeting/Basic.lean` are the scaffolding of a support library for the program.
  * `lakefile.toml` contains the configuration that `lake` needs to build the application.
  * `lean-toolchain` contains an identifier for the specific version of Lean that is used for the project.


Additionally, `lake new` initializes the project as a Git repository and configures its `.gitignore` file to ignore intermediate build products. Typically, the majority of the application logic will be in a collection of libraries for the program, while `Main.lean` will contain a small wrapper around these pieces that does things like parsing command lines and executing the central application logic. To create a project in an already-existing directory, run `lake init` instead of `lake new`.
By default, the library file `Greeting/Basic.lean` contains a single definition:
File: `Greeting/Basic.lean``def hello := "world"`
The library file `Greeting.lean` imports `Greeting/Basic.lean`:
File: `Greeting.lean``-- This module serves as the root of the `Greeting` library.``-- Import modules here that should be built as part of the library.``import Greeting.Basic`
This means that everything defined in `Greeting/Basic.lean` is also available to files that import `Greeting.lean`. In `import` statements, dots are interpreted as directories on disk.
The executable source `Main.lean` contains:
File: `Main.lean``import Greeting````def main : IO Unit :=``  IO.println s!"Hello, {hello}!"`
Because `Main.lean` imports `Greeting.lean` and `Greeting.lean` imports `Greeting/Basic.lean`, the definition of `hello` is available in `main`.
To build the package, run the command `lake build`. After a number of build commands scroll by, the resulting binary has been placed in `.lake/build/bin`. Running `./.lake/build/bin/greeting` results in `Hello, world!`. Instead of running the binary directly, the command `lake exe` can be used to build the binary if necessary and then run it. Running `lake exe greeting` also results in `Hello, world!`.
##  2.3.2. Lakefiles[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=lakefiles "Permalink")
A `lakefile.toml` describes a _package_ , which is a coherent collection of Lean code for distribution, analogous to an `npm` or `nuget` package or a Rust crate. A package may contain any number of libraries or executables. The [documentation for Lake](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/latest/find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=lake-config-toml) describes the available options in a Lake configuration. The generated `lakefile.toml` contains the following:
File: `lakefile.toml``name = "greeting"``version = "0.1.0"``defaultTargets = ["greeting"]````[[lean_lib]]``name = "Greeting"````[[lean_exe]]``name = "greeting"``root = "Main"`
This initial Lake configuration consists of three items:
  * _package_ settings, at the top of the file,
  * a _library_ declaration, named `Greeting`, and
  * an _executable_ , named `greeting`.


Each Lake configuration file will contain exactly one package, but any number of dependencies, libraries, or executables. By convention, package and executable names begin with a lowercase letter, while libraries begin with an uppercase letter. Dependencies are declarations of other Lean packages (either locally or from remote Git repositories) The items in the Lake configuration file allow things like source file locations, module hierarchies, and compiler flags to be configured. Generally speaking, however, the defaults are reasonable. Lake configuration files written in the Lean format may additionally contain _external libraries_ , which are libraries not written in Lean to be statically linked with the resulting executable, _custom targets_ , which are build targets that don't fit naturally into the library/executable taxonomy, and _scripts_ , which are essentially `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions (similar to `main`), but that additionally have access to metadata about the package configuration.
Libraries, executables, and custom targets are all called _targets_. By default, `lake build` builds those targets that are specified in the `defaultTargets` list. To build a target that is not a default target, specify the target's name as an argument after `lake build`.
##  2.3.3. Libraries and Imports[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=libraries-and-imports "Permalink")
A Lean library consists of a hierarchically organized collection of source files from which names can be imported, called _modules_. By default, a library has a single root file that matches its name. In this case, the root file for the library `Greeting` is `Greeting.lean`. The first line of `Main.lean`, which is `[import](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Source-Files-and-Modules/#Lean___Parser___Module___import-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Greeting`, makes the contents of `Greeting.lean` available in `Main.lean`.
Additional module files may be added to the library by creating a directory called `Greeting` and placing them inside. These names can be imported by replacing the directory separator with a dot. For instance, creating the file `Greeting/Smile.lean` with the contents:
File: `Greeting/Smile.lean``def Expression.happy : String := "a big smile"`
means that `Main.lean` can use the definition as follows:
File: `Main.lean``import Greeting``import Greeting.Smile````open Expression````def main : IO Unit :=``  IO.println s!"Hello, {hello}, with {happy}!"`
The module name hierarchy is decoupled from the namespace hierarchy. In Lean, modules are units of code distribution, while namespaces are units of code organization. That is, names defined in the module `Greeting.Smile` are not automatically in a corresponding namespace `Greeting.Smile`. In particular, `happy` is in the `Expression` namespace. Modules may place names into any namespace they like, and the code that imports them may `open` the namespace or not. `import` is used to make the contents of a source file available, while `open` makes names from a namespace available in the current context without prefixes.
The line `[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expression` makes the name `Expression.happy` accessible as `happy` in `main`. Namespaces may also be opened _selectively_ , making only some of their names available without explicit prefixes. This is done by writing the desired names in parentheses. For example, `[Nat.toFloat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___toFloat "Documentation for Nat.toFloat in Lean Language Reference")` converts a natural number to a `[Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")`. It can be made available as `[toFloat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___toFloat "Documentation for Nat.toFloat in Lean Language Reference")` using `[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Nat ([toFloat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___toFloat "Documentation for Nat.toFloat in Lean Language Reference"))`. 
[←2.2. Step By Step](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#step-by-step "2.2. Step By Step")[2.4. Worked Example: cat→](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#example-cat "2.4. Worked Example: cat")
code_blocks: -- This module serves as the root of the `Greeting` library.
-- Import modules here that should be built as part of the library.

title: Step By Step
markdown:
[←2.1. Running a Program](Hello___-World___/Running-a-Program/#running-a-program "2.1. Running a Program")[2.3. Starting a Project→](Hello___-World___/Starting-a-Project/#starting-a-project "2.3. Starting a Project")
#  2.2. Step By Step[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=step-by-step "Permalink")
A `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block can be executed one line at a time. Start with the program from the prior section:
`let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference") let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?" let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference") let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
##  2.2.1. Standard IO[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=stdio "Permalink")
The first line is `let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")`, while the remainder is:
`let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?" let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference") let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
To execute a `let` statement that uses a `←`, start by evaluating the expression to the right of the arrow (in this case, `[IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")`). Because this expression is just a variable, its value is looked up. The resulting value is a built-in primitive `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action. The next step is to execute this `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action, resulting in a value that represents the standard input stream, which has type `[IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")`. Standard input is then associated with the name to the left of the arrow (here `stdin`) for the remainder of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block.
Executing the second line, `let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")`, proceeds similarly. First, the expression `[IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")` is evaluated, yielding an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that will return the standard output. Next, this action is executed, actually returning the standard output. Finally, this value is associated with the name `stdout` for the remainder of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block.
##  2.2.2. Asking a Question[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=asking-a-question "Permalink")
Now that `stdin` and `stdout` have been found, the remainder of the block consists of a question and an answer:
`stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?" let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference") let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
The first statement in the block, `stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") "How would you like to be addressed?"`, consists of an expression. To execute an expression, it is first evaluated. In this case, `[IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference")` has type `[IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference") → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`. This means that it is a function that accepts a stream and a string, returning an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action. The expression uses [accessor notation](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Structures/#behind-the-scenes) for a function call. This function is applied to two arguments: the standard output stream and a string. The value of the expression is an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that will write the string and a newline character to the output stream. Having found this value, the next step is to execute it, which causes the string and newline to actually be written to `stdout`. Statements that consist only of expressions do not introduce any new variables.
The next statement in the block is `let input ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")`. `IO.FS.Stream.getLine` has type `[IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`, which means that it is a function from a stream to an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that will return a string. Once again, this is an example of accessor notation. This `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action is executed, and the program waits until the user has typed a complete line of input. Assume the user writes “`David`”. The resulting line (`"David\n"`) is associated with `input`, where the escape sequence `\n` denotes the newline character.
`let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference") stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
The next line, `let name := input.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") [Char.isWhitespace](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isWhitespace "Documentation for Char.isWhitespace in Lean Language Reference")`, is a `let` statement. Unlike the other `let` statements in this program, it uses `:=` instead of `←`. This means that the expression will be evaluated, but the resulting value need not be an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action and will not be executed. In this case, `[String.dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference")` takes a string and a predicate over characters and returns a new string from which all the characters at the end of the string that satisfy the predicate have been removed. For example,
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "Hello!!!".[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") (· == '!')`
yields

```
"Hello"
```

and
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "Hello...   ".[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") (fun c => [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") (c.[isAlphanum](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlphanum "Documentation for Char.isAlphanum in Lean Language Reference")))`
yields

```
"Hello"
```

in which all non-alphanumeric characters have been removed from the right side of the string. In the current line of the program, whitespace characters (including the newline) are removed from the right side of the input string, resulting in `"David"`, which is associated with `name` for the remainder of the block.
##  2.2.3. Greeting the User[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=greeting "Permalink")
All that remains to be executed in the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block is a single statement:
`stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello, {name}!"`
The string argument to `[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference")` is constructed via string interpolation, yielding the string `"Hello, David!"`. Because this statement is an expression, it is evaluated to yield an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that will print this string with a newline to standard output. Once the expression has been evaluated, the resulting `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action is executed, resulting in the greeting.
##  2.2.4. `IO` Actions as Values[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=actions-as-values "Permalink")
In the above description, it can be difficult to see why the distinction between evaluating expressions and executing `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions is necessary. After all, each action is executed immediately after it is produced. Why not simply carry out the effects during evaluation, as is done in other languages?
The answer is twofold. First off, separating evaluation from execution means that programs must be explicit about which functions can have side effects. Because the parts of the program that do not have effects are much more amenable to mathematical reasoning, whether in the heads of programmers or using Lean's facilities for formal proof, this separation can make it easier to avoid bugs. Secondly, not all `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions need be executed at the time that they come into existence. The ability to mention an action without carrying it out allows ordinary functions to be used as control structures.
For example, the function `twice` takes an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action as its argument, returning a new action that will execute the argument action twice.
`def twice (action : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   action   action`
Executing
`twice ([IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "shy")`
results in

```
shy
shy

```

being printed. This can be generalized to a version that runs the underlying action any number of times:
`def nTimes (action : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   | 0 => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | n + 1 => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     action     nTimes action n`
In the base case for `[Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")`, the result is `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()`. The function `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` creates an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that has no side effects, but returns `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`'s argument, which in this case is the constructor for `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`. As an action that does nothing and returns nothing interesting, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()` is at the same time utterly boring and very useful. In the recursive step, a `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block is used to create an action that first executes `action` and then executes the result of the recursive call. Executing ``Hello Hello Hello `[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") nTimes ([IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Hello") 3` causes the following output:

```
Hello
Hello
Hello

```

In addition to using functions as control structures, the fact that `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions are first-class values means that they can be saved in data structures for later execution. For instance, the function `countdown` takes a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` and returns a list of unexecuted `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions, one for each `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def countdown : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference"))   | 0 => [[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Blast off!"]   | n + 1 => [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") s!"{n + 1}" :: countdown n`
This function has no side effects, and does not print anything. For example, it can be applied to an argument, and the length of the resulting list of actions can be checked:
`def from5 : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")) := countdown 5`
This list contains six elements (one for each number, plus a `"Blast off!"` action for zero):
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [from5](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#from5 "Definition of example").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")`
```
6
```

The function `runActions` takes a list of actions and constructs a single action that runs them all in order:
`def runActions : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")) → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | act :: actions => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     act     runActions actions`
Its structure is essentially the same as that of `nTimes`, except instead of having one action that is executed for each `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")`, the action under each `[List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")` is to be executed. Similarly, `runActions` does not itself run the actions. It creates a new action that will run them, and that action must be placed in a position where it will be executed as a part of `main`:
`def main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := runActions [from5](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#from5 "Definition of example")`
Running this program results in the following output:
`countdown``5 4 3 2 1 Blast off! `
What happens when this program is run? The first step is to evaluate `main`. That occurs as follows:
`main``runActions [from5](Hello___-World___/Step-By-Step/#from5 "Definition of example")``runActions (countdown 5)``runActions   [[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "5",    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "4",    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "3",    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "2",    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "1",    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Blast off!"]``[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "5"    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "4"    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "3"    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "2"    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "1"    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Blast off!"    [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()`
The resulting `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action is a `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block. Each step of the `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` block is then executed, one at a time, yielding the expected output. The final step, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()`, does not have any effects, and it is only present because the definition of `runActions` needs a base case.
##  2.2.5. Exercise[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=step-by-step-exercise "Permalink")
Step through the execution of the following program on a piece of paper:
`def main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let englishGreeting := [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Hello!"   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Bonjour!"   englishGreeting`
While stepping through the program's execution, identify when an expression is being evaluated and when an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action is being executed. When executing an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action results in a side effect, write it down. After doing this, run the program with Lean and double-check that your predictions about the side effects were correct.
[←2.1. Running a Program](Hello___-World___/Running-a-Program/#running-a-program "2.1. Running a Program")[2.3. Starting a Project→](Hello___-World___/Starting-a-Project/#starting-a-project "2.3. Starting a Project")
