title: Summary
markdown:
[←2.5. Additional Conveniences](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#hello-world-conveniences "2.5. Additional Conveniences")[Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing→](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#props-proofs-indexing "Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing")
#  2.6. Summary[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=hello-world-summary "Permalink")
##  2.6.1. Evaluation vs Execution
Side effects are aspects of program execution that go beyond the evaluation of mathematical expressions, such as reading files, throwing exceptions, or triggering industrial machinery. While most languages allow side effects to occur during evaluation, Lean does not. Instead, Lean has a type called `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` that represents _descriptions_ of programs that use side effects. These descriptions are then executed by the language's run-time system, which invokes the Lean expression evaluator to carry out specific computations. Values of type `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α` are called _`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`actions_. The simplest is `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`, which returns its argument and has no actual side effects.
`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions can also be understood as functions that take the whole world as an argument and return a new world in which the side effect has occurred. Behind the scenes, the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` library ensures that the world is never duplicated, created, or destroyed. While this model of side effects cannot actually be implemented, as the whole universe is too big to fit in memory, the real world can be represented by a token that is passed around through the program.
An `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action `main` is executed when the program starts. `main` can have one of three types:
  * `main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` is used for simple programs that cannot read their command-line arguments and always return exit code `0`,
  * `main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference")` is used for programs without arguments that may signal success or failure, and
  * `main : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference")` is used for programs that take command-line arguments and signal success or failure.


##  2.6.2. `do` Notation
The Lean standard library provides a number of basic `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that represent effects such as reading from and writing to files and interacting with standard input and standard output. These base `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions are composed into larger `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions using `do` notation, which is a built-in domain-specific language for writing descriptions of programs with side effects. A `do` expression contains a sequence of _statements_ , which may be:
  * expressions that represent `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions,
  * ordinary local definitions with `let` and `:=`, where the defined name refers to the value of the provided expression, or
  * local definitions with `let` and `←`, where the defined name refers to the result of executing the value of the provided expression.


`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that are written with `do` are executed one statement at a time.
Furthermore, `if` and `match` expressions that occur immediately under a `do` are implicitly considered to have their own `do` in each branch. Inside of a `do` expression, _nested actions_ are expressions with a left arrow immediately under parentheses. The Lean compiler implicitly lifts them to the nearest enclosing `do`, which may be implicitly part of a branch of a `match` or `if` expression, and gives them a unique name. This unique name then replaces the origin site of the nested action.
##  2.6.3. Compiling and Running Programs
A Lean program that consists of a single file with a `main` definition can be run using `lean --run FILE`. While this can be a nice way to get started with a simple program, most programs will eventually graduate to a multiple-file project that should be compiled before running.
Lean projects are organized into _packages_ , which are collections of libraries and executables together with information about dependencies and a build configuration. Packages are described using Lake, a Lean build tool. Use `lake new` to create a Lake package in a new directory, or `lake init` to create one in the current directory. Lake package configuration is another domain-specific language. Use `lake build` to build a project.
##  2.6.4. Partiality
One consequence of following the mathematical model of expression evaluation is that every expression must have a value. This rules out both incomplete pattern matches that fail to cover all constructors of a datatype and programs that can fall into an infinite loop. Lean ensures that all `match` expressions cover all cases, and that all recursive functions are either structurally recursive or have an explicit proof of termination.
However, some real programs require the possibility of looping infinitely, because they handle potentially-infinite data, such as POSIX streams. Lean provides an escape hatch: functions whose definition is marked `partial` are not required to terminate. This comes at a cost. Because types are a first-class part of the Lean language, functions can return types. Partial functions, however, are not evaluated during type checking, because an infinite loop in a function could cause the type checker to enter an infinite loop. Furthermore, mathematical proofs are unable to inspect the definitions of partial functions, which means that programs that use them are much less amenable to formal proof. 
[←2.5. Additional Conveniences](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#hello-world-conveniences "2.5. Additional Conveniences")[Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing→](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#props-proofs-indexing "Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing")

title: Worked Example:  cat
markdown:
[←2.3. Starting a Project](Hello___-World___/Starting-a-Project/#starting-a-project "2.3. Starting a Project")[2.5. Additional Conveniences→](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#hello-world-conveniences "2.5. Additional Conveniences")
#  2.4. Worked Example: `cat`[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat "Permalink")
The standard Unix utility `cat` takes a number of command-line options, followed by zero or more input files. If no files are provided, or if one of them is a dash (`-`), then it takes the standard input as the corresponding input instead of reading a file. The contents of the inputs are written, one after the other, to the standard output. If a specified input file does not exist, this is noted on standard error, but `cat` continues concatenating the remaining inputs. A non-zero exit code is returned if any of the input files do not exist.
This section describes a simplified version of `cat`, called `feline`. Unlike commonly-used versions of `cat`, `feline` has no command-line options for features such as numbering lines, indicating non-printing characters, or displaying help text. Furthermore, it cannot read more than once from a standard input that's associated with a terminal device.
To get the most benefit from this section, follow along yourself. It's OK to copy-paste the code examples, but it's even better to type them in by hand. This makes it easier to learn the mechanical process of typing in code, recovering from mistakes, and interpreting feedback from the compiler.
##  2.4.1. Getting Started[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat-start "Permalink")
The first step in implementing `feline` is to create a package and decide how to organize the code. In this case, because the program is so simple, all the code will be placed in `Main.lean`. The first step is to run `lake new feline`. Edit the Lakefile to remove the library, and delete the generated library code and the reference to it from `Main.lean`. Once this has been done, `lakefile.toml` should contain:
File: `lakefile.toml``name = "feline"``version = "0.1.0"``defaultTargets = ["feline"]````[[lean_exe]]``name = "feline"``root = "Main"`
and `Main.lean` should contain something like:
File: `Main.lean``def main : IO Unit :=``  IO.println s!"Hello, cats!"`
Alternatively, running `lake new feline exe` instructs `lake` to use a template that does not include a library section, making it unnecessary to edit the file.
Ensure that the code can be built by running `lake build`.
##  2.4.2. Concatenating Streams[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat-streams "Permalink")
Now that the basic skeleton of the program has been built, it's time to actually enter the code. A proper implementation of `cat` can be used with infinite IO streams, such as `/dev/random`, which means that it can't read its input into memory before outputting it. Furthermore, it should not work one character at a time, as this leads to frustratingly slow performance. Instead, it's better to read contiguous blocks of data all at once, directing the data to the standard output one block at a time.
The first step is to decide how big of a block to read. For the sake of simplicity, this implementation uses a conservative 20 kilobyte block. `[USize](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#USize___ofBitVec "Documentation for USize in Lean Language Reference")` is analogous to `size_t` in C—it's an unsigned integer type that is big enough to represent all valid array sizes.
`def bufsize : [USize](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#USize___ofBitVec "Documentation for USize in Lean Language Reference") := 20 * 1024`
###  2.4.2.1. Streams[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=streams "Permalink")
The main work of `feline` is done by `dump`, which reads input one block at a time, dumping the result to standard output, until the end of the input has been reached. The end of the input is indicated by `[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference")` returning an empty byte array:
`partial def dump (stream : [IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let buf ← stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference") [bufsize](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#bufsize "Definition of example")   if buf.isEmpty then     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   else     let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")     stdout.[write](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.write in Lean Language Reference") buf     dump stream`
The `dump` function is declared `partial`, because it calls itself recursively on input that is not immediately smaller than an argument. When a function is declared to be partial, Lean does not require a proof that it terminates. On the other hand, partial functions are also much less amenable to proofs of correctness, because allowing infinite loops in Lean's logic would make it unsound. However, there is no way to prove that `dump` terminates, because infinite input (such as from `/dev/random`) would mean that it does not, in fact, terminate. In cases like this, there is no alternative to declaring the function `partial`.
The type `[IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")` represents a POSIX stream. Behind the scenes, it is represented as a structure that has one field for each POSIX stream operation. Each operation is represented as an IO action that provides the corresponding operation:
`structure Stream where   flush   : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   read    : [USize](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#USize___ofBitVec "Documentation for USize in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") ByteArray   write   : ByteArray → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   getLine : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   putStr  : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   isTty   : [BaseIO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#BaseIO "Documentation for BaseIO in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`
The type `[BaseIO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#BaseIO "Documentation for BaseIO in Lean Language Reference")` is a variant of `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` that rules out run-time errors. The Lean compiler contains `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions (such as `[IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")`, which is called in `dump`) to get streams that represent standard input, standard output, and standard error. These are `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions rather than ordinary definitions because Lean allows these standard POSIX streams to be replaced in a process, which makes it easier to do things like capturing the output from a program into a string by writing a custom `[IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")`.
The control flow in `dump` is essentially a `while` loop. When `dump` is called, if the stream has reached the end of the file, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()` terminates the function by returning the constructor for `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`. If the stream has not yet reached the end of the file, one block is read, and its contents are written to `stdout`, after which `dump` calls itself directly. The recursive calls continue until `stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference")` returns an empty byte array, which indicates the end of the file.
When an `if` expression occurs as a statement in a `do`, as in `dump`, each branch of the `if` is implicitly provided with a `do`. In other words, the sequence of steps following the `else` are treated as a sequence of `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions to be executed, just as if they had a `do` at the beginning. Names introduced with `let` in the branches of the `if` are visible only in their own branches, and are not in scope outside of the `if`.
There is no danger of running out of stack space while calling `dump` because the recursive call happens as the very last step in the function, and its result is returned directly rather than being manipulated or computed with. This kind of recursion is called _tail recursion_ , and it is described in more detail [later in this book](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Tail-Recursion/#tail-recursion). Because the compiled code does not need to retain any state, the Lean compiler can compile the recursive call to a jump.
If `feline` only redirected standard input to standard output, then `dump` would be sufficient. However, it also needs to be able to open files that are provided as command-line arguments and emit their contents. When its argument is the name of a file that exists, `fileStream` returns a stream that reads the file's contents. When the argument is not a file, `fileStream` emits an error and returns `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`.
`def fileStream (filename : [System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let fileExists ← filename.[pathExists](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___pathExists "Documentation for System.FilePath.pathExists in Lean Language Reference")   if [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") fileExists then     let stderr ← [IO.getStderr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStderr "Documentation for IO.getStderr in Lean Language Reference")     stderr.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"File not found: {filename}"     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   else     let handle ← [IO.FS.Handle.mk](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Handle___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Handle.mk in Lean Language Reference") filename [IO.FS.Mode.read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Mode___read "Documentation for IO.FS.Mode.read in Lean Language Reference")     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([IO.FS.Stream.ofHandle](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___ofHandle "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.ofHandle in Lean Language Reference") handle))`
Opening a file as a stream takes two steps. First, a file handle is created by opening the file in read mode. A Lean file handle tracks an underlying file descriptor. When there are no references to the file handle value, a finalizer closes the file descriptor. Second, the file handle is given the same interface as a POSIX stream using `[IO.FS.Stream.ofHandle](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___ofHandle "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.ofHandle in Lean Language Reference")`, which fills each field of the `[Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")` structure with the corresponding `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that works on file handles.
###  2.4.2.2. Handling Input[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=handling-input "Permalink")
The main loop of `feline` is another tail-recursive function, called `process`. In order to return a non-zero exit code if any of the inputs could not be read, `process` takes an argument `exitCode` that represents the current exit code for the whole program. Additionally, it takes a list of input files to be processed.
`def process (exitCode : [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference")) (args : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match args with   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") exitCode   | "-" :: args =>     let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")     dump stdin     process exitCode args   | filename :: args =>     let stream ← fileStream ⟨filename⟩     match stream with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") =>       process 1 args     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") stream =>       dump stream       process exitCode args`
Just as with `if`, each branch of a `match` that is used as a statement in a `do` is implicitly provided with its own `do`.
There are three possibilities. One is that no more files remain to be processed, in which case `process` returns the error code unchanged. Another is that the specified filename is `"-"`, in which case `process` dumps the contents of the standard input and then processes the remaining filenames. The final possibility is that an actual filename was specified. In this case, `fileStream` is used to attempt to open the file as a POSIX stream. Its argument is encased in `⟨ ... ⟩` because a `[FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")` is a single-field structure that contains a string. If the file could not be opened, it is skipped, and the recursive call to `process` sets the exit code to `1`. If it could, then it is dumped, and the recursive call to `process` leaves the exit code unchanged.
`process` does not need to be marked `partial` because it is structurally recursive. Each recursive call is provided with the tail of the input list, and all Lean lists are finite. Thus, `process` does not introduce any non-termination.
###  2.4.2.3. Main[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat-main "Permalink")
The final step is to write the `main` action. Unlike prior examples, `main` in `feline` is a function. In Lean, `main` can have one of three types:
  * `main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` corresponds to programs that cannot read their command-line arguments and always indicate success with an exit code of `0`,
  * `main : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference")` corresponds to `int main(void)` in C, for programs without arguments that return exit codes, and
  * `main : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference")` corresponds to `int main(int argc, char **argv)` in C, for programs that take arguments and signal success or failure.


If no arguments were provided, `feline` should read from standard input as if it were called with a single `"-"` argument. Otherwise, the arguments should be processed one after the other.
`def main (args : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") args [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => process 0 ["-"]   | _ =>  process 0 args`
##  2.4.3. Meow![🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat-running "Permalink")
To check whether `feline` works, the first step is to build it with `lake build`. First off, when called without arguments, it should emit what it receives from standard input. Check that
``$ `echo "It works!" | lake exe feline `
emits `It works!`.
Secondly, when called with files as arguments, it should print them. If the file `test1.txt` contains
File: `test1.txt``It's time to find a warm spot`
and `test2.txt` contains
File: `test2.txt``and curl up!`
then the command
``$ `lake exe feline test1.txt test2.txt`
should emit

```
It's time to find a warm spot
and curl up!

```

Finally, the `-` argument should be handled appropriately.
``$ `echo "and purr" | lake exe feline test1.txt - test2.txt `
should yield

```
It's time to find a warm spot
and purr
and curl up!

```

##  2.4.4. Exercise[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-cat-exercise "Permalink")
Extend `feline` with support for usage information. The extended version should accept a command-line argument `--help` that causes documentation about the available command-line options to be written to standard output. 
[←2.3. Starting a Project](Hello___-World___/Starting-a-Project/#starting-a-project "2.3. Starting a Project")[2.5. Additional Conveniences→](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#hello-world-conveniences "2.5. Additional Conveniences")

title: Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing
markdown:
[←2.6. Summary](Hello___-World___/Summary/#hello-world-summary "2.6. Summary")[3. Overloading and Type Classes→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/#type-classes "3. Overloading and Type Classes")
#  Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=props-proofs-indexing "Permalink")
Like many languages, Lean uses square brackets for indexing into arrays and lists. For instance, if `woodlandCritters` is defined as follows:
`def woodlandCritters : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   ["hedgehog", "deer", "snail"]`
then the individual components can be extracted:
`def hedgehog := woodlandCritters[0] def deer := woodlandCritters[1] def snail := woodlandCritters[2]`
However, attempting to extract the fourth element results in a compile-time error, rather than a run-time error:
`def oops := `failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:   - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid   - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid   - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type   - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid ⊢ 3 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") woodlandCritters.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")`woodlandCritters[3]`
```
failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:
  - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid
  - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid
  - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type
  - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid
⊢ 3 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") woodlandCritters.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")
```

This error message is saying Lean tried to automatically mathematically prove that `3 < woodlandCritters.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")` (i.e. `3 < [List.length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") woodlandCritters`), which would mean that the lookup was safe, but that it could not do so. Out-of-bounds errors are a common class of bugs, and Lean uses its dual nature as a programming language and a theorem prover to rule out as many as possible.
Understanding how this works requires an understanding of three key ideas: propositions, proofs, and tactics.
##  Propositions and Proofs[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=propositions-and-proofs "Permalink")
A _proposition_ is a statement that can be true or false. All of the following English sentences are propositions:
  * `1+1=21 + 1 = 21+1=2`
  * Addition is commutative.
  * There are infinitely many prime numbers.
  * `1+1=151 + 1 = 151+1=15`
  * Paris is the capital of France.
  * Buenos Aires is the capital of South Korea.
  * All birds can fly.


On the other hand, nonsense statements are not propositions. Despite being grammatical, none of the following are propositions:
  * 1 + green = ice cream
  * All capital cities are prime numbers.
  * At least one gorg is a fleep.


Propositions come in two varieties: those that are purely mathematical, relying only on our definitions of concepts, and those that are facts about the world. Theorem provers like Lean are concerned with the former category, and have nothing to say about the flight capabilities of penguins or the legal status of cities.
A _proof_ is a convincing argument that a proposition is true. For mathematical propositions, these arguments make use of the definitions of the concepts that are involved as well as the rules of logical argumentation. Most proofs are written for people to understand, and leave out many tedious details. Computer-aided theorem provers like Lean are designed to allow mathematicians to write proofs while omitting many details, and it is the software's responsibility to fill in the missing explicit steps. These steps can be mechanically checked. This decreases the likelihood of oversights or mistakes.
In Lean, a program's type describes the ways it can be interacted with. For instance, a program of type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` is a function that takes a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` argument and produces a list of strings. In other words, each type specifies what counts as a program with that type.
In Lean, propositions are in fact types. They specify what counts as evidence that the statement is true. The proposition is proved by providing this evidence, which is checked by Lean. On the other hand, if the proposition is false, then it will be impossible to construct this evidence.
For example, the proposition `1+1=21 + 1 = 21+1=2` can be written directly in Lean. The evidence for this proposition is the constructor `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`, which is short for _reflexivity_. In mathematics, a relation is _reflexive_ if every element is related to itself; this is a basic requirement in order to have a sensible notion of equality. Because `1 + 1` computes to `2`, they are really the same thing:
`def onePlusOneIsTwo : 1 + 1 = 2 := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`
On the other hand, `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")` does not prove the false proposition `1+1=151 + 1 = 151+1=15`:
`def `Not a definitional equality: the left-hand side   1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 is not definitionally equal to the right-hand side   15`onePlusOneIsFifteen : 1 + 1 = 15 := `Type mismatch   [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") has type   ?m.16 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") ?m.16 but is expected to have type   1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 15`[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`
```
Type mismatch
  [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")
has type
  ?m.16 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") ?m.16
but is expected to have type
  1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 15
```

This error message indicates that `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")` can prove that two expressions are equal when both sides of the equality statement are already the same number. Because `1 + 1` evaluates directly to `2`, they are considered to be the same, which allows `onePlusOneIsTwo` to be accepted. Just as `Type` describes types such as `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`, and `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") × ([Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")))` that represent data structures and functions, `Prop` describes propositions.
When a proposition has been proven, it is called a _theorem_. In Lean, it is conventional to declare theorems with the `theorem` keyword instead of `def`. This helps readers see which declarations are intended to be read as mathematical proofs, and which are definitions. Generally speaking, with a proof, what matters is that there is evidence that a proposition is true, but it's not particularly important _which_ evidence was provided. With definitions, on the other hand, it matters very much which particular value is selected—after all, a definition of addition that always returns `0` is clearly wrong. Because the details of a proof don't matter for later proofs, using the `theorem` keyword enables greater parallelism in the Lean compiler.
The prior example could be rewritten as follows:
`def OnePlusOneIsTwo : Prop := 1 + 1 = 2  theorem onePlusOneIsTwo : [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example") := [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`
##  Tactics[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=tactics "Permalink")
Proofs are normally written using _tactics_ , rather than by providing evidence directly. Tactics are small programs that construct evidence for a proposition. These programs run in a _proof state_ that tracks the statement that is to be proved (called the _goal_) along with the assumptions that are available to prove it. Running a tactic on a goal results in a new proof state that contains new goals. The proof is complete when all goals have been proven.
To write a proof with tactics, begin the definition with `by`. Writing `by` puts Lean into tactic mode until the end of the next indented block. While in tactic mode, Lean provides ongoing feedback about the current proof state. Written with tactics, `onePlusOneIsTwo` is still quite short:
`theorem onePlusOneIsTwo : 1 + 1 = 2 := by⊢ 1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 2   [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
The `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic invokes a _decision procedure_ , which is a program that can check whether a statement is true or false, returning a suitable proof in either case. It is primarily used when working with concrete values like `1` and `2`. The other important tactics in this book are `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, short for “simplify,” and `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, which can automatically prove many theorems.
Tactics are useful for a number of reasons:
  1. Many proofs are complicated and tedious when written out down to the smallest detail, and tactics can automate these uninteresting parts.
  2. Proofs written with tactics are easier to maintain over time, because flexible automation can paper over small changes to definitions.
  3. Because a single tactic can prove many different theorems, Lean can use tactics behind the scenes to free users from writing proofs by hand. For instance, an array lookup requires a proof that the index is in bounds, and a tactic can typically construct that proof without the user needing to worry about it.


Behind the scenes, indexing notation uses a tactic to prove that the user's lookup operation is safe. This tactic takes many facts about arithmetic into account, combining them with any locally-known facts to attempt to prove that the index is in bounds.
The `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is a workhorse of Lean proofs. It rewrites the goal to as simple a form as possible. In many cases, this rewriting simplifies the statement so much that it can be automatically proved. Behind the scenes, a detailed formal proof is constructed, but using `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` hides this complexity.
Like `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, the `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is used to finish proofs. It uses a collection of techniques from SMT solvers that can prove a wide variety of theorems. Unlike `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can never make progress towards a proof without completing it entirely; it either succeeds fully or fails. The `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic is very powerful, customizable, and extensible; due to this power and flexibility, its output when it fails to prove a theorem contains a lot of information that can help trained Lean users diagnose the reason for the failure. This can be overwhelming in the beginning, so this chapter uses only `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` and `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`.
##  Connectives[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=connectives "Permalink")
The basic building blocks of logic, such as “and”, “or”, “true”, “false”, and “not”, are called _logical connectives_. Each connective defines what counts as evidence of its truth. For example, to prove a statement “ _A_ and _B_ ”, one must prove both _A_ and _B_. This means that evidence for “ _A_ and _B_ ” is a pair that contains both evidence for _A_ and evidence for _B_. Similarly, evidence for “ _A_ or _B_ ” consists of either evidence for _A_ or evidence for _B_.
In particular, most of these connectives are defined like datatypes, and they have constructors. If `A` and `B` are propositions, then “`A` and `B`” (written `A ∧ B`) is a proposition. Evidence for `A ∧ B` consists of the constructor `[And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference")`, which has the type `A → B → A ∧ B`. Replacing `A` and `B` with concrete propositions, it is possible to prove `1 + 1 = 2 ∧ "Str".[append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") "ing" = "String"` with `[And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference") [rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`. Of course, `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` is also powerful enough to find this proof:
`theorem addAndAppend : 1 + 1 = 2 ∧ "Str".[append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") "ing" = "String" := by⊢ 1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 2 [∧](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") "Str".[append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") "ing" [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") "String"   [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
Similarly, “`A` or `B`” (written `A ∨ B`) has two constructors, because a proof of “`A` or `B`” requires only that one of the two underlying propositions be true. There are two constructors: `[Or.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or.inl in Lean Language Reference")`, with type `A → A ∨ B`, and `[Or.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or.inr in Lean Language Reference")`, with type `B → A ∨ B`.
Implication (if `A` then `B`) is represented using functions. In particular, a function that transforms evidence for `A` into evidence for `B` is itself evidence that `A` implies `B`. This is different from the usual description of implication, in which `A → B` is shorthand for `¬A ∨ B`, but the two formulations are equivalent.
Because evidence for an “and” is a constructor, it can be used with pattern matching. For instance, a proof that `A` and `B` implies `A` or `B` is a function that pulls the evidence of `A` (or of `B`) out of the evidence for `A` and `B`, and then uses this evidence to produce evidence of `A` or `B`:
`theorem andImpliesOr : A ∧ B → A ∨ B :=   fun andEvidence =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") andEvidence [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") a b => [Or.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or.inl in Lean Language Reference") a`  
|  Connective  |  Lean Syntax  |  Evidence   |  
| --- | --- | --- |  
|  True  |  `[True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`  |  `[True.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True.intro in Lean Language Reference") : [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
|  False  |  `[False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`  |  No evidence  |  
|  `A` and `B`  |  `A ∧ B`  |  `[And.intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And.intro in Lean Language Reference") : A → B → A ∧ B`  |  
|  `A` or `B`  |  `A ∨ B`  |  Either `[Or.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or.inl in Lean Language Reference") : A → A ∨ B` or `[Or.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or.inr in Lean Language Reference") : B → A ∨ B`  |  
|  `A` implies `B`  |  `A → B`  |  A function that transforms evidence of `A` into evidence of `B`  |  
|  not `A`  |  `¬A`  |  A function that would transform evidence of `A` into evidence of `[False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
The `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can prove theorems that use these connectives. For example:
`theorem onePlusOneOrLessThan : 1 + 1 = 2 ∨ 3 < 5 := by⊢ 1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 2 [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") 3 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") 5 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 theorem notTwoEqualFive : ¬(1 + 1 = 5) := by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")1 [+](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") 1 [=](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#Eq___refl "Documentation for Eq in Lean Language Reference") 5 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 theorem trueIsTrue : [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") := by⊢ [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 theorem trueOrFalse : [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") ∨ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") := by⊢ [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") [∨](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Or___inl "Documentation for Or in Lean Language Reference") [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙 theorem falseImpliesTrue : [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") → [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") := by⊢ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference") → [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference") [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
##  Evidence as Arguments[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=evidence-passing "Permalink")
In some cases, safely indexing into a list requires that the list have some minimum size, but the list itself is a variable rather than a concrete value. For this lookup to be safe, there must be some evidence that the list is long enough. One of the easiest ways to make indexing safe is to have the function that performs a lookup into a data structure take the required evidence of safety as an argument. For instance, a function that returns the third entry in a list is not generally safe because lists might contain zero, one, or two entries:
`def [third](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#third "Definition of example") (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : α := `failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:   - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid   - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid   - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type   - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid α:Type ?u.9260xs:[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α⊢ 2 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")`xs[2]`
```
failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:
  - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid
  - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid
  - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type
  - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid
α:Type ?u.9260xs:[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α⊢ 2 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")
```

However, the obligation to show that the list has at least three entries can be imposed on the caller by adding an argument that consists of evidence that the indexing operation is safe:
`def third (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (ok : xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2) : α := xs[2]`
In this example, `xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2` is not a program that checks _whether_ `xs` has more than 2 entries. It is a proposition that could be true or false, and the argument `ok` must be evidence that it is true.
When the function is called on a concrete list, its length is known. In these cases, `by [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can construct the evidence automatically:
``"snail"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [third](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#third "Definition of example") woodlandCritters (by⊢ woodlandCritters.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙)`
```
"snail"
```

##  Indexing Without Evidence[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=indexing-without-evidence "Permalink")
In cases where it's not practical to prove that an indexing operation is in bounds, there are other alternatives. Adding a question mark results in an `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, where the result is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` if the index is in bounds, and `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` otherwise. For example:
`def thirdOption (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α := xs[2]?```[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") "snail"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [thirdOption](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#thirdOption "Definition of example") woodlandCritters`
```
[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") "snail"
```
``[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [thirdOption](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#thirdOption "Definition of example") ["only", "two"]`
```
[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")
```

There is also a version that crashes the program when the index is out of bounds, rather than returning an `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`:
``"deer"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") woodlandCritters[1]!`
```
"deer"
```

##  Messages You May Meet[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=props-proofs-indexing-messages "Permalink")
In addition to proving that a statement is true, the `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can also prove that it is false. When asked to prove that a one-element list has more than two elements, it returns an error that indicates that the statement is indeed false:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [third](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#third "Definition of example") ["rabbit"] (by⊢ [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"rabbit"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2 `Tactic `decide` proved that the proposition   [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"rabbit"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2 is false`[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")⊢ [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"rabbit"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2)`
```
Tactic `decide` proved that the proposition
  [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")"rabbit"[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > 2
is false
```

The `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` and `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactics do not automatically unfold definitions with `def`. Attempting to prove `[OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")` using `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` fails:
`theorem [onePlusOneIsStillTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___onePlusOneIsStillTwo "Definition of example") : [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example") := by⊢ [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example") ``simp` made no progress`[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")⊢ [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")`
The error messages simply states that it could do nothing, because without unfolding `[OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")`, no progress can be made:

```
`simp` made no progress
```

Using `[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` also fails:
`theorem onePlusOneIsStillTwo : [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example") := by⊢ [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example") `failed to synthesize   [Decidable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Decidable___isFalse "Documentation for Decidable in Lean Language Reference") [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`[decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")⊢ [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")`
This is also due to it not unfolding `[OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")`:

```
failed to synthesize
  [Decidable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Decidable___isFalse "Documentation for Decidable in Lean Language Reference") [OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```

Defining `[OnePlusOneIsTwo](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#Foo___OnePlusOneIsTwo "Definition of example")` with [`abbrev` fixes the problem](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Functions-and-Definitions/#abbrev-vs-def) by marking the definition for unfolding.
In addition to the error that occurs when Lean is unable to find compile-time evidence that an indexing operation is safe, polymorphic functions that use unsafe indexing may produce the following message:
`def unsafeThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : α := `failed to synthesize   [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`xs[2]!`
```
failed to synthesize
  [Inhabited](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Inhabited___mk "Documentation for Inhabited in Lean Language Reference") α

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```

This is due to a technical restriction that is part of keeping Lean usable as both a logic for proving theorems and a programming language. In particular, only programs whose types contain at least one value are allowed to crash. This is because a proposition in Lean is a kind of type that classifies evidence of its truth. False propositions have no such evidence. If a program with an empty type could crash, then that crashing program could be used as a kind of fake evidence for a false proposition.
Internally, Lean contains a table of types that are known to have at least one value. This error is saying that some arbitrary type `α` is not necessarily in that table. The next chapter describes how to add to this table, and how to successfully write functions like `unsafeThird`.
Adding whitespace between a list and the brackets used for lookup can cause another message:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `Function expected at   woodlandCritters but this term has type   [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")  Note: Expected a function because this term is being applied to the argument   [1]`woodlandCritters [1]`
```
Function expected at
  woodlandCritters
but this term has type
  [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")

Note: Expected a function because this term is being applied to the argument
  [1]
```

Adding a space causes Lean to treat the expression as a function application, and the index as a list that contains a single number. This error message results from having Lean attempt to treat `woodlandCritters` as a function.
###  Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=props-proofs-indexing-exercises "Permalink")
  * Prove the following theorems using `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")`: `2 + 3 = 5`, `15 - 8 = 7`, `"Hello, ".[append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") "world" = "Hello, world"`. What happens if `[rfl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#rfl-next "Documentation for rfl in Lean Language Reference")` is used to prove `5 < 18`? Why?
  * Prove the following theorems using `by [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`: `2 + 3 = 5`, `15 - 8 = 7`, `"Hello, ".[append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") "world" = "Hello, world"`, `5 < 18`.
  * Write a function that looks up the fifth entry in a list. Pass the evidence that this lookup is safe as an argument to the function. 

[←2.6. Summary](Hello___-World___/Summary/#hello-world-summary "2.6. Summary")[3. Overloading and Type Classes→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/#type-classes "3. Overloading and Type Classes")
