title: Interlude: Tactics, Induction, and Proofs
markdown:
[←7.6. Summary](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Summary/#dependent-types-summary "7.6. Summary")[8. Programming, Proving, and Performance→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance "8. Programming, Proving, and Performance")
#  Interlude: Tactics, Induction, and Proofs[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=tactics-induction-proofs "Permalink")
##  A Note on Proofs and User Interfaces[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=proofs-and-uis "Permalink")
This book presents the process of writing proofs as if they are written in one go and submitted to Lean, which then replies with error messages that describe what remains to be done. The actual process of interacting with Lean is much more pleasant. Lean provides information about the proof as the cursor is moved through it and there are a number of interactive features that make proving easier. Please consult the documentation of your Lean development environment for more information.
The approach in this book that focuses on incrementally building a proof and showing the messages that result demonstrates the kinds of interactive feedback that Lean provides while writing a proof, even though it is much slower than the process used by experts. At the same time, seeing incomplete proofs evolve towards completeness is a useful perspective on proving. As your skill in writing proofs increases, Lean's feedback will come to feel less like errors and more like support for your own thought processes. Learning the interactive approach is very important.
##  Recursion and Induction[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=recursion-vs-induction "Permalink")
The functions `plusR_succ_left` and `plusR_zero_left` from the preceding chapter can be seen from two perspectives. On the one hand, they are recursive functions that build up evidence for a proposition, just as other recursive functions might construct a list, a string, or any other data structure. On the other, they also correspond to proofs by _mathematical induction_.
Mathematical induction is a proof technique where a statement is proven for _all_ natural numbers in two steps:
  1. The statement is shown to hold for `000`. This is called the _base case_.
  2. Under the assumption that the statement holds for some arbitrarily chosen number `nnn`, it is shown to hold for `n+1n + 1n+1`. This is called the _induction step_. The assumption that the statement holds for `nnn` is called the _induction hypothesis_.


Because it's impossible to check the statement for _every_ natural number, induction provides a means of writing a proof that could, in principle, be expanded to any particular natural number. For example, if a concrete proof were desired for the number 3, then it could be constructed by using first the base case and then the induction step three times, to show the statement for 0, 1, 2, and finally 3. Thus, it proves the statement for all natural numbers.
##  The Induction Tactic[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=induction-tactic "Permalink")
Writing proofs by induction as recursive functions that use helpers such as `[congrArg](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Propositional-Equality/#congrArg "Documentation for congrArg in Lean Language Reference")` does not always do a good job of expressing the intentions behind the proof. While recursive functions indeed have the structure of induction, they should probably be viewed as an _encoding_ of a proof. Furthermore, Lean's tactic system provides a number of opportunities to automate the construction of a proof that are not available when writing the recursive function explicitly. Lean provides an induction _tactic_ that can carry out an entire proof by induction in a single tactic block. Behind the scenes, Lean constructs the recursive function that corresponds the use of induction.
To prove `plusR_zero_left` with the `induction` tactic, begin by writing its signature (using `theorem`, because this really is a proof). Then, use `by [induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") k` as the body of the definition:
`theorem [plusR_zero_left](Interlude___-Tactics___-Induction___-and-Proofs/#Tactical___plusR_zero_left "Definition of example") (k : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : k = Nat.plusR 0 k := `unsolved goals zero⊢ 0 ```

A tactic block is a program that is run while the Lean type checker processes a file, somewhat like a much more powerful C preprocessor macro. The tactics generate the actual program.
In the tactic language, there can be a number of goals. Each goal consists of a type together with some assumptions. These are analogous to using underscores as placeholders—the type in the goal represents what is to be proved, and the assumptions represent what is in-scope and can be used. In the case of the goal `case zero`, there are no assumptions and the type is `[Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference") = Nat.plusR 0 [Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")`—this is the theorem statement with `0` instead of `k`. In the goal `case succ`, there are two assumptions, named `n✝` and `n_ih✝`. Behind the scenes, the `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic creates a dependent pattern match that refines the overall type, and `n✝` represents the argument to `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")` in the pattern. The assumption `n_ih✝` represents the result of calling the generated function recursively on `n✝`. Its type is the overall type of the theorem, just with `n✝` instead of `k`. The type to be fulfilled as part of the goal `case succ` is the overall theorem statement, with `Nat.succ n✝` instead of `k`.
The two goals that result from the use of the `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic correspond to the base case and the induction step in the description of mathematical induction. The base case is `case zero`. In `case succ`, `n_ih✝` corresponds to the induction hypothesis, while the whole of `case succ` is the induction step.
The next step in writing the proof is to focus on each of the two goals in turn. Just as `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()` can be used in a `do` block to indicate “do nothing”, the tactic language has a statement `skip` that also does nothing. This can be used when Lean's syntax requires a tactic, but it's not yet clear which one should be used. Adding `with` to the end of the `induction` statement provides a syntax that is similar to pattern matching:

One of the most important is `rw`, which takes a list of equality proofs and replaces the left side with the right side in the goal. ##  Tactic Golf[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=tactic-golf "Permalink")
So far, the tactic language has not shown its true value. The above proof is no shorter than the recursive function; it's merely written in a domain-specific language instead of the full Lean language. But proofs with tactics can be shorter, easier, and more maintainable. Just as a lower score is better in the game of golf, a shorter proof is better in the game of tactic golf.
The induction step of `plusR_zero_left` can be proved using the simplification tactic `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`. Using `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` on its own does not help:

In particular, the goal is now identical to the induction hypothesis. In addition to automatically proving simple equality statements, the simplifier automatically replaces goals like `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") A = [Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference") B` with `A = B`. Because the induction hypothesis `ih` has exactly the right type, the `exact` tactic can indicate that it should be used:
`theorem plusR_zero_left (k : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : k = Nat.plusR 0 k := byk:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ k However, the use of `exact` is somewhat fragile. Renaming the induction hypothesis, which may happen while “golfing” the proof, would cause this proof to stop working. The `assumption` tactic solves the current goal if _any_ of the assumptions match it:

This proof is no shorter than the prior proof that used unfolding and explicit rewriting. However, a series of transformations can make it much shorter, taking advantage of the fact that `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can solve many kinds of goals. The first step is to drop the `with` at the end of `induction`. For structured, readable proofs, the `with` syntax is convenient. It complains if any cases are missing, and it shows the structure of the induction clearly. But shortening proofs can often require a more liberal approach.
Using `induction` without `with` simply results in a proof state with two goals. The `case` tactic can be used to select one of them, just as in the branches of the `induction`` ...``with` tactic. In other words, the following proof is equivalent to the prior proof:

In a context with a single goal (namely, `k = Nat.plusR 0 k`), the `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") k` tactic yields two goals. In general, a tactic will either fail with an error or take a goal and transform it into zero or more new goals. Each new goal represents what remains to be proved. If the result is zero goals, then the tactic was a success, and that part of the proof is done.
The `<;>` operator takes two tactics as arguments, resulting in a new tactic. `T1 ``<;>`` T2` applies `T1` to the current goal, and then applies `T2` in _all_ goals created by `T1`. In other words, `<;>` enables a general tactic that can solve many kinds of goals to be used on multiple new goals all at once. One such general tactic is `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`.
Because `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can both complete the proof of the base case and make progress on the proof of the induction step, using it with `induction` and `<;>` shortens the proof:

Here, `exact` would not have been possible, because `ih` was never explicitly named.
For beginners, this proof is not easier to read. However, a common pattern for expert users is to take care of a number of simple cases with powerful tactics like `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, allowing them to focus the text of the proof on the interesting cases. Additionally, these proofs tend to be more robust in the face of small changes to the functions and datatypes involved in the proof. The game of tactic golf is a useful part of developing good taste and style when writing proofs.
##  Induction on Other Datatypes[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=induction-other-types "Permalink")
Mathematical induction proves a statement for natural numbers by providing a base case for `[Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")` and an induction step for `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")`. The principle of induction is also valid for other datatypes. Constructors without recursive arguments form the base cases, while constructors with recursive arguments form the induction steps. The ability to carry out proofs by induction is the very reason why they are called _inductive_ datatypes.
One example of this is induction on binary trees. Induction on binary trees is a proof technique where a statement is proven for _all_ binary trees in two steps:
  1. The statement is shown to hold for `[BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")`. This is called the base case.
  2. Under the assumption that the statement holds for some arbitrarily chosen trees `l` and `r`, it is shown to hold for `[BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") l x r`, where `x` is an arbitrarily-chosen new data point. This is called the _induction step_. The assumptions that the statement holds for `l` and `r` are called the _induction hypotheses_.


`BinTree.count` counts the number of branches in a tree:
`def BinTree.count : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   | [.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => 0   | [.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") l _ r =>     1 + l.count + r.count`
[Mirroring a tree](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#leading-dot-notation) does not change the number of branches in it. This can be proven using induction on trees. The first step is to state the theorem and invoke `induction`:
`theorem [BinTree.mirror_count](Interlude___-Tactics___-Induction___-and-Proofs/#BinTree___mirror_count "Definition of example") (t : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree In addition to definitions to be unfolded, the simplifier can also be passed names of equality proofs to use as rewrites while it simplifies proof goals. `BinTree.mirror_count` can also be written:

As proofs grow more complicated, listing assumptions by hand can become tedious. Furthermore, manually writing assumption names can make it more difficult to re-use proof steps for multiple subgoals. The argument `*` to `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` or `simp +arith` instructs them to use _all_ assumptions while simplifying or solving the goal. ##  The `grind` Tactic[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=grind "Permalink")
The `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic can automatically prove many theorems. Like `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, it accepts an optional list of additional facts to take into consideration or functions to unfold; unlike `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, it automatically takes local hypotheses into consideration. Additionally, `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`'s support for reasoning about specific mathematical domains is far stronger than `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`'s arithmetic support. The proof of `BinTree.mirror_count` can rewritten to use `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`:

Because the proofs in this book are fairly modest, most of them do not provide an opportunity for `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` to show its full power. However, it is very convenient in some of the later proofs in the book.
##  Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=tactics-induction-proofs-exercises "Permalink")
  * Prove `plusR_succ_left` using the `induction`` ...``with` tactic.
  * Rewrite the proof of `plusR_succ_left` to use `<;>` in a single line.
  * Prove that appending lists is associative using induction on lists:
`theorem List.append_assoc (xs ys zs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) :     xs ++ (ys ++ zs) = (xs ++ ys) ++ zs`

title: Introduction
markdown:
[← Functional Programming in Lean](./../ " Functional Programming in Lean")[Acknowledgments→](Acknowledgments/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Acknowledgments "Acknowledgments")
#  Introduction
Lean is an interactive theorem prover based on dependent type theory. Originally developed at Microsoft Research, development now takes place at the [Lean FRO](https://lean-fro.org). Dependent type theory unites the worlds of programs and proofs; thus, Lean is also a programming language. Lean takes its dual nature seriously, and it is designed to be suitable for use as a general-purpose programming language—Lean is even implemented in itself. This book is about writing programs in Lean.
When viewed as a programming language, Lean is a strict pure functional language with dependent types. A large part of learning to program with Lean consists of learning how each of these attributes affects the way programs are written, and how to think like a functional programmer. _Strictness_ means that function calls in Lean work similarly to the way they do in most languages: the arguments are fully computed before the function's body begins running. _Purity_ means that Lean programs cannot have side effects such as modifying locations in memory, sending emails, or deleting files without the program's type saying so. Lean is a _functional_ language in the sense that functions are first-class values like any other and that the execution model is inspired by the evaluation of mathematical expressions. _Dependent types_ , which are the most unusual feature of Lean, make types into a first-class part of the language, allowing types to contain programs and programs to compute types.
This book is intended for programmers who want to learn Lean, but who have not necessarily used a functional programming language before. Familiarity with functional languages such as Haskell, OCaml, or F# is not required. On the other hand, this book does assume knowledge of concepts like loops, functions, and data structures that are common to most programming languages. While this book is intended to be a good first book on functional programming, it is not a good first book on programming in general.
Mathematicians who are using Lean as a proof assistant will likely need to write custom proof automation tools at some point. This book is also for them. As these tools become more sophisticated, they begin to resemble programs in functional languages, but most working mathematicians are trained in languages like Python and Mathematica. This book can help bridge the gap, empowering more mathematicians to write maintainable and understandable proof automation tools.
This book is intended to be read linearly, from the beginning to the end. Concepts are introduced one at a time, and later sections assume familiarity with earlier sections. Sometimes, later chapters will go into depth on a topic that was only briefly addressed earlier on. Some sections of the book contain exercises. These are worth doing, in order to cement your understanding of the section. It is also useful to explore Lean as you read the book, finding creative new ways to use what you have learned.
##  Getting Lean[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=getting-lean "Permalink")
Before writing and running programs written in Lean, you'll need to set up Lean on your own computer. The Lean tooling consists of the following:
  * `elan` manages the Lean compiler toolchains, similarly to `rustup` or `ghcup`.
  * `lake` builds Lean packages and their dependencies, similarly to `cargo`, `make`, or Gradle.
  * `lean` type checks and compiles individual Lean files as well as providing information to programmer tools about files that are currently being written. Normally, `lean` is invoked by other tools rather than directly by users.
  * Plugins for editors, such as Visual Studio Code or Emacs, that communicate with `lean` and present its information conveniently.


Please refer to the [Lean manual](https://lean-lang.org/lean4/doc/quickstart.html) for up-to-date instructions for installing Lean.
##  Typographical Conventions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=typographical-conventions "Permalink")
Code examples that are provided to Lean as _input_ are formatted like this:
`def add1 (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := n + 1``[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") add1 7`
The last line above (beginning with `#eval`) is a command that instructs Lean to calculate an answer. Lean's replies are formatted like this:

```
8
```

Error messages returned by Lean are formatted like this:

```
Application type mismatch: The argument
  "seven"
has type
  [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")
but is expected to have type
  [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
in the application
  add1 "seven"
```

Warnings are formatted like this:

```
declaration uses 'sorry'
```

##  Unicode[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=unicode "Permalink")
Idiomatic Lean code makes use of a variety of Unicode characters that are not part of ASCII. For instance, Greek letters like `α` and `β` and the arrow `→` both occur in the first chapter of this book. This allows Lean code to more closely resemble ordinary mathematical notation.
With the default Lean settings, both Visual Studio Code and Emacs allow these characters to be typed with a backslash (`\`) followed by a name. For example, to enter `α`, type `\alpha`. To find out how to type a character in Visual Studio Code, point the mouse at it and look at the tooltip. In Emacs, use `C-c C-k` with point on the character in question.
##  Release history[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=release-history "Permalink")
###  October, 2025
The book has been updated to the latest stable Lean release (version 4.23.0), and now describes functional induction and the `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic.
###  August, 2025
This is a maintenance release to resolve an issue with copy-pasting code from the book.
###  July, 2025
The book has been updated for version 4.21 of Lean.
###  June, 2025
The book has been reformatted with Verso.
###  April, 2025
The book has been extensively updated and now describes Lean version 4.18.
###  January, 2024
This is a minor bugfix release that fixes a regression in an example program.
###  October, 2023
In this first maintenance release, a number of smaller issues were fixed and the text was brought up to date with the latest release of Lean.
###  May, 2023
The book is now complete! Compared to the April pre-release, many small details have been improved and minor mistakes have been fixed.
###  April, 2023
This release adds an interlude on writing proofs with tactics as well as a final chapter that combines discussion of performance and cost models with proofs of termination and program equivalence. This is the last release prior to the final release.
###  March, 2023
This release adds a chapter on programming with dependent types and indexed families.
###  January, 2023
This release adds a chapter on monad transformers that includes a description of the imperative features that are available in `do`-notation.
###  December, 2022
This release adds a chapter on applicative functors that additionally describes structures and type classes in more detail. This is accompanied with improvements to the description of monads. The December 2022 release was delayed until January 2023 due to winter holidays.
###  November, 2022
This release adds a chapter on programming with monads. Additionally, the example of using JSON in the coercions section has been updated to include the complete code.
###  October, 2022
This release completes the chapter on type classes. In addition, a short interlude introducing propositions, proofs, and tactics has been added just before the chapter on type classes, because a small amount of familiarity with the concepts helps to understand some of the standard library type classes.
###  September, 2022
This release adds the first half of a chapter on type classes, which are Lean's mechanism for overloading operators and an important means of organizing code and structuring libraries. Additionally, the second chapter has been updated to account for changes in Lean's stream API.
###  August, 2022
This third public release adds a second chapter, which describes compiling and running programs along with Lean's model for side effects.
###  July, 2022
The second public release completes the first chapter.
###  June, 2022
This was the first public release, consisting of an introduction and part of the first chapter.
##  About the Author[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=about-the-author "Permalink")
David Thrane Christiansen has been using functional languages for twenty years, and dependent types for ten. Together with Daniel P. Friedman, he wrote [_The Little Typer_](https://thelittletyper.com/), an introduction to the key ideas of dependent type theory. He has a Ph.D. from the IT University of Copenhagen. During his studies, he was a major contributor to the first version of the Idris language. Since leaving academia, he has worked as a software developer at Galois in Portland, Oregon and Deon Digital in Copenhagen, Denmark, and he was the Executive Director of the Haskell Foundation. At the time of writing, he is employed at the [Lean Focused Research Organization](https://lean-fro.org) working full-time on Lean.
##  License[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=license "Permalink")
[](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)  
This work is licensed under a [Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).
The original version of the book was written by David Thrane Christiansen on contract to Microsoft Corporation, who generously released it under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License. The current version has been modified by the author from the original version to account for changes in newer versions of Lean. A detailed account of the changes can be found in the book's [source code repository](https://github.com/leanprover/fp-lean/).

title: Monad Transformers
markdown:
[←5.7. Summary](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/Summary/#structure-applicative-monad-summary "5.7. Summary")[6.1. Combining IO and Reader→](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#io-reader "6.1. Combining IO and Reader")
#  6. Monad Transformers
A monad is a way to encode some collection of side effects in a pure language. Different monads provide different effects, such as state and error handling. Many monads even provide useful effects that aren't available in most languages, such as nondeterministic searches, readers, and even continuations.
A typical application has a core set of easily testable functions written without monads paired with an outer wrapper that uses a monad to encode the necessary application logic. These monads are constructed from well-known components. For example:
  * Mutable state is encoded with a function parameter and a return value that have the same type
  * Error handling is encoded by having a return type that is similar to `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`, with constructors for success and failure
  * Logging is encoded by pairing the return value with the log


Writing each monad by hand is tedious, however, involving boilerplate definitions of the various type classes. Each of these components can also be extracted to a definition that modifies some other monad to add an additional effect. Such a definition is called a _monad transformer_. A concrete monad can be build from a collection of monad transformers, which enables much more code re-use.
  1. [6.1. Combining IO and Reader](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#io-reader)
  2. [6.2. A Monad Construction Kit](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers--A-Monad-Construction-Kit)

title: A Monad Construction Kit
markdown:
[←6.1. Combining IO and Reader](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#io-reader "6.1. Combining IO and Reader")[6.3. Ordering Monad Transformers→](Monad-Transformers/Ordering-Monad-Transformers/#monad-transformer-order "6.3. Ordering Monad Transformers")
#  6.2. A Monad Construction Kit
`[ReaderT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#ReaderT "Documentation for ReaderT in Lean Language Reference")` is far from the only useful monad transformer. This section describes a number of additional transformers. Each monad transformer consists of the following:
  1. A definition or datatype `T` that takes a monad as an argument. It should have a type like `(Type u → Type v) → Type u → Type v`, though it may accept additional arguments prior to the monad.
  2. A `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `T m` that relies on an instance of `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m`. This enables the transformed monad to be used as a monad.
  3. A `[MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference")` instance that translates actions of type `m α` into actions of type `T m α`, for arbitrary monads `m`. This enables actions from the underlying monad to be used in the transformed monad.


Furthermore, the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for the transformer should obey the contract for `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")`, at least if the underlying `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance does. In addition, `[monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x : m α)` should be equivalent to `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x` in the transformed monad, and `[monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference")` should distribute over `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` so that `[monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") (x >>= f : m α)` is the same as `([monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") x : m α) >>= fun y => [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") (f y)`.
Many monad transformers additionally define type classes in the style of `[MonadReader](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadReader___mk "Documentation for MonadReader in Lean Language Reference")` that describe the actual effects available in the monad. This can provide more flexibility: it allows programs to be written that rely only on an interface, and don't constrain the underlying monad to be implemented by a given transformer. The type classes are a way for programs to express their requirements, and monad transformers are a convenient way to meet these requirements.
##  6.2.1. Failure with `OptionT`[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=OptionT "Permalink")
Failure, represented by the `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` monad, and exceptions, represented by the `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` monad, both have corresponding transformers. In the case of `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, failure can be added to a monad by having it contain values of type `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α` where it would otherwise contain values of type `α`. For example, `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α)` represents `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that don't always return a value of type `α`. This suggests the definition of the monad transformer `OptionT`:
`def OptionT (m : Type u → Type v) (α : Type u) : Type v :=   m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α)`
As an example of `OptionT` in action, consider a program that asks the user questions. The function `getSomeInput` asks for a line of input and removes whitespace from both ends. If the resulting trimmed input is non-empty, then it is returned, but the function fails if there are no non-whitespace characters:
`def getSomeInput : OptionT [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let input ← (← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")).[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")   let trimmed := input.[trim](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___trim "Documentation for String.trim in Lean Language Reference")   if trimmed == "" then     failure   else [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") trimmed`
This particular application tracks users with their name and their favorite species of beetle:
`structure UserInfo where   name : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   favoriteBeetle : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`
Asking the user for input is no more verbose than a function that uses only `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` would be:
`def getUserInfo : OptionT [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UserInfo](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Opt___UserInfo "Definition of example") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "What is your name?"   let name ← getSomeInput   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "What is your favorite species of beetle?"   let beetle ← getSomeInput   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ⟨name, beetle⟩`
However, because the function runs in an `OptionT [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` context rather than just in `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`, failure in the first call to `getSomeInput` causes the whole `getUserInfo` to fail, with control never reaching the question about beetles. The main function, `interact`, invokes `getUserInfo` in a purely `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` context, which allows it to check whether the call succeeded or failed by matching on the inner `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def interact : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← getUserInfo with   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") =>     [IO.eprintln](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___eprintln "Documentation for IO.eprintln in Lean Language Reference") "Missing info"   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ⟨name, beetle⟩ =>     [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") s!"Hello {name}, whose favorite beetle is {beetle}."`
###  6.2.1.1. The Monad Instance[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=OptionT-monad-instance "Permalink")
Writing the monad instance reveals a difficulty. Based on the types, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` should use `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` from the underlying monad `m` together with `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")`. Just as `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` branches on the first argument, propagating `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `OptionT` should run the monadic action that makes up the first argument, branch on the result, and then propagate `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`. Following this sketch yields the following definition, which Lean does not accept:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := `failed to synthesize   [Pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m)  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") `Application type mismatch: The argument   [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x has type   [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α✝ but is expected to have type   α✝ in the application   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x)`([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x) [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") action next := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `failed to synthesize   [Bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m)  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`match (← action) with | `Type mismatch   [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") has type   [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ?m.24 but is expected to have type   α✝`[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => pure none | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v => next v`
The error message shows a cryptic type mismatch:

```
Application type mismatch: The argument
  [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x
has type
  [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α✝
but is expected to have type
  α✝
in the application
  [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x)
```

The problem here is that Lean is selecting the wrong `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for the surrounding use of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`. Similar errors occur for the definition of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")`. One solution is to use type annotations to guide Lean to the correct `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x) : m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") _))   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") action next := ([do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match (← action) with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v => next v : m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") _))`
While this solution works, it is inelegant and the code becomes a bit noisy.
An alternative solution is to define functions whose type signatures guide Lean to the correct instances. In fact, `OptionT` could have been defined as a structure:
`structure OptionT (m : Type u → Type v) (α : Type u) : Type v where   run : m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α)`
This would solve the problem, because the constructor `OptionT.mk` and the field accessor `[OptionT.run](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Opt___Structed___OptionT___run "Definition of example")` would guide type class inference to the correct instances. The downside to doing this is that the resulting code is more complicated, and these structures can make it more difficult to read proofs. The best of both worlds can be achieved by defining functions that serve the same role as the constructor `OptionT.mk` and the field `[OptionT.run](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Opt___Structed___OptionT___run "Definition of example")`, but that work with the direct definition:
`def OptionT.mk (x : m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α)) : OptionT m α := x  def OptionT.run (x : OptionT m α) : m ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α) := x`
Both functions return their inputs unchanged, but they indicate the boundary between code that is intended to present the interface of `OptionT` and code that is intended to present the interface of the underlying monad `m`. Using these helpers, the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance becomes more readable:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := OptionT.mk ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x))   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") action next := OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match ← action with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v => next v`
Here, the use of `OptionT.mk` indicates that its arguments should be considered as code that uses the interface of `m`, which allows Lean to select the correct `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instances.
After defining the monad instance, it's a good idea to check that the monad contract is satisfied. The first step is to show that `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") v) f` is the same as `f v`. Here's the steps:
`[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") v) f`
Unfolding the definitions of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` and `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`
`OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v) with   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => f x`
Desugaring nested action syntax
`OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let y ← [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v)   match y with   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => f x`
Desugaring `[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")`-notation
`OptionT.mk   ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v) >>= fun y =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") y [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => f x)`
Using the first monad rule for `m`
`OptionT.mk   ([match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")    | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")    | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => f x)`
Reduce `match`
`OptionT.mk (f v)`
Definition of `OptionT.mk`
`f v`
The second rule states that `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") w [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is the same as `w`. To demonstrate this, unfold the definitions of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` and `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`, yielding:
`OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match ← w with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") v)`
In this pattern match, the result of both cases is the same as the pattern being matched, just with `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` around it. In other words, it is equivalent to `w >>= fun y => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") y`, which is an instance of `m`'s second monad rule.
The final rule states that `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") ([bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") v f) g` is the same as `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") v (fun x => [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") (f x) g)`. It can be checked in the same way, by expanding the definitions of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` and `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` and then delegating to the underlying monad `m`.
###  6.2.1.2. An `Alternative` Instance[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=OptionT-Alternative-instance "Permalink")
One convenient way to use `OptionT` is through the `[Alternative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Alternative___mk "Documentation for Alternative in Lean Language Reference")` type class. Successful return is already indicated by `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`, and the `failure` and `orElse` methods of `[Alternative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Alternative___mk "Documentation for Alternative in Lean Language Reference")` provide a way to write a program that returns the first successful result from a number of subprograms:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Alternative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Alternative___mk "Documentation for Alternative in Lean Language Reference") (OptionT m) where   failure := OptionT.mk ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference"))   orElse x y := OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match ← x with     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") result => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") result)     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => y ()`
###  6.2.1.3. Lifting[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=OptionT-lifting "Permalink")
Lifting an action from `m` to `OptionT m` only requires wrapping `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` around the result of the computation:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference") m (OptionT m) where   [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") action := OptionT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (← action))`
##  6.2.2. Exceptions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=exceptions "Permalink")
The monad transformer version of `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` is very similar to the monad transformer version of `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`. Adding exceptions of type `ε` to some monadic action of type `m`` ``α` can be accomplished by adding exceptions to `α`, yielding type `m ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α)`:
`def ExceptT (ε : Type u) (m : Type u → Type v) (α : Type u) : Type v :=   m ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α)`
`OptionT` provides `OptionT.mk` and `OptionT.run` functions to guide the type checker towards the correct `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instances. This trick is also useful for `ExceptT`:
`def ExceptT.mk {ε α : Type u} (x : m ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α)) : ExceptT ε m α := x  def ExceptT.run {ε α : Type u} (x : ExceptT ε m α) : m ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α) := x`
The `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `ExceptT` is also very similar to the instance for `OptionT`. The only difference is that it propagates a specific error value, rather than `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`:
`instance {ε : Type u} {m : Type u → Type v} [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] :     [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (ExceptT ε m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := ExceptT.mk ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x))   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") result next := ExceptT.mk [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match ← result with     | [.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") e => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ([.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") e)     | [.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x => next x`
The type signatures of `ExceptT.mk` and `ExceptT.run` contain a subtle detail: they annotate the universe levels of `α` and `ε` explicitly. If they are not explicitly annotated, then Lean generates a more general type signature in which they have distinct polymorphic universe variables. However, the definition of `ExceptT` expects them to be in the same universe, because they can both be provided as arguments to `m`. This can lead to a problem in the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance where the universe level solver fails to find a working solution:
`def ExceptT.mk (x : m ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α)) : ExceptT ε m α := x``instance {ε : Type u} {m : Type u → Type v} [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] :     [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language v`[.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") e => pure
