```
stuck at solving universe constraint
  max ?u.19380 ?u.19381 =?= u
while trying to unify
  ExceptT ε m β✝ : Type v
with
  ExceptT.{max ?u.19381 ?u.19380, v} ε m β✝ : Type v
```

This kind of error message is typically caused by underconstrained universe variables. Diagnosing it can be tricky, but a good first step is to look for reused universe variables in some definitions that are not reused in others.
Unlike `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, the `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` datatype is typically not used as a data structure. It is always used as a control structure with its `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance. This means that it is reasonable to lift `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε` actions into `ExceptT ε m`, as well as actions from the underlying monad `m`. Lifting `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` actions into `ExceptT` actions is done by wrapping them in `m`'s `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")`, because an action that only has exception effects cannot have any effects from the monad `m`:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference") ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε) (ExceptT ε m) where   [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") action := ExceptT.mk ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") action)`
Because actions from `m` do not have any exceptions in them, their value should be wrapped in `[Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference")`. This can be accomplished using the fact that `[Functor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor in Lean Language Reference")` is a superclass of `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")`, so applying a function to the result of any monadic computation can be accomplished using `[Functor.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")`:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference") m (ExceptT ε m) where   [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") action := ExceptT.mk ([.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") <$> action)`
###  6.2.2.1. Type Classes for Exceptions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=exceptions-type-classes "Permalink")
Exception handling fundamentally consists of two operations: the ability to throw exceptions, and the ability to recover from them. Thus far, this has been accomplished using the constructors of `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` and pattern matching, respectively. However, this ties a program that uses exceptions to one specific encoding of the exception handling effect. Using a type class to capture these operations allows a program that uses exceptions to be used in _any_ monad that supports throwing and catching.
Throwing an exception should take an exception as an argument, and it should be allowed in any context where a monadic action is requested. The “any context” part of the specification can be written as a type by writing `m α`—because there's no way to produce a value of any arbitrary type, the `throw` operation must be doing something that causes control to leave that part of the program. Catching an exception should accept any monadic action together with a handler, and the handler should explain how to get back to the action's type from an exception:
`class MonadExcept (ε : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type u)) (m : Type v → Type w) where   throw : ε → m α   tryCatch : m α → (ε → m α) → m α`
The universe levels on `MonadExcept` differ from those of `ExceptT`. In `ExceptT`, both `ε` and `α` have the same level, while `MonadExcept` imposes no such limitation. This is because `MonadExcept` never places an exception value inside of `m`. The most general universe signature recognizes the fact that `ε` and `α` are completely independent in this definition. Being more general means that the type class can be instantiated for a wider variety of types.
An example program that uses `MonadExcept` is a simple division service. The program is divided into two parts: a frontend that supplies a user interface based on strings that handles errors, and a backend that actually does the division. Both the frontend and the backend can throw exceptions, the former for ill-formed input and the latter for division by zero errors. The exceptions are an inductive type:
`inductive Err where   | divByZero   | notANumber : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err "Definition of example")`
The backend checks for zero, and divides if it can:
`def divBackend [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err "Definition of example") m] (n k : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : m [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") k == 0 [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") [.divByZero](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___divByZero "Definition of example")   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (n / k)`
The frontend's helper `asNumber` throws an exception if the string it is passed is not a number. The overall frontend converts its inputs to `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")`s and calls the backend, handling exceptions by returning a friendly string error:
`def asNumber [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err "Definition of example") m] (s : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : m [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s.[toInt?](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___toInt___ "Documentation for String.toInt? in Lean Language Reference") [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") ([.notANumber](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___notANumber "Definition of example") s)   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") i => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i``def divFrontend [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err "Definition of example") m] (n k : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : m [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   [tryCatch](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.tryCatch in Lean Language Reference") ([do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (toString (← [divBackend](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___divBackend "Definition of example") (← asNumber n) (← asNumber k))))     fun       | [.divByZero](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___divByZero "Definition of example") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") "Division by zero!"       | [.notANumber](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___notANumber "Definition of example") s => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") s!"Not a number: \"{s}\""`
Throwing and catching exceptions is common enough that Lean provides a special syntax for using `[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference")`. Just as `+` is short for `[HAdd.hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")`, `try` and `catch` can be used as shorthand for the `tryCatch` method:
`def divFrontend [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err "Definition of example") m] (n k : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : m [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   try     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (toString (← [divBackend](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___divBackend "Definition of example") (← asNumber n) (← asNumber k)))   catch     | [.divByZero](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___divByZero "Definition of example") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") "Division by zero!"     | [.notANumber](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Ex___Err___notANumber "Definition of example") s => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") s!"Not a number: \"{s}\""`
In addition to `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` and `ExceptT`, there are useful `MonadExcept` instances for other types that may not seem like exceptions at first glance. For example, failure due to `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` can be seen as throwing an exception that contains no data whatsoever, so there is an instance of `[MonadExcept](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` that allows `try`` ...``catch`` ...` syntax to be used with `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`.
##  6.2.3. State[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=state-monad "Permalink")
A simulation of mutable state is added to a monad by having monadic actions accept a starting state as an argument and return a final state together with their result. The bind operator for a state monad provides the final state of one action as an argument to the next action, threading the state through the program. This pattern can also be expressed as a monad transformer:
`def StateT (σ : Type u)     (m : Type u → Type v) (α : Type u) : Type (max u v) :=   σ → m (α × σ)`
Once again, the monad instance is very similar to that for `State`. The only difference is that the input and output states are passed around and returned in the underlying monad, rather than with pure code:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") ([StateT](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#St___StateT "Definition of example") σ m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := fun s => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x, s)   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") result next := fun s => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let (v, s') ← result s     next v s'`
The corresponding type class has `get` and `set` methods. One downside of `get` and `set` is that it becomes too easy to `set` the wrong state when updating it. This is because retrieving the state, updating it, and saving the updated state is a natural way to write some programs. For example, the following program counts the number of diacritic-free English vowels and consonants in a string of letters:
`structure LetterCounts where   vowels : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   consonants : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  inductive Err where   | notALetter : [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference") → [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err "Definition of example") deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")  def vowels :=   let lowerVowels := "aeiuoy"   lowerVowels ++ lowerVowels.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___map "Documentation for String.map in Lean Language Reference") (·.[toUpper](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___toUpper "Documentation for Char.toUpper in Lean Language Reference"))  def consonants :=   let lowerConsonants := "bcdfghjklmnpqrstvwxz"   lowerConsonants ++ lowerConsonants.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___map "Documentation for String.map in Lean Language Reference") (·.[toUpper](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___toUpper "Documentation for Char.toUpper in Lean Language Reference") )  def countLetters (str : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference") [LetterCounts](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts "Definition of example") ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err "Definition of example")) [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   let rec loop (chars : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match chars with     | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | c :: cs =>       let st ← get       let st' ←         if c.[isAlpha](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlpha "Documentation for Char.isAlpha in Lean Language Reference") then           if [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___vowels "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation st.[consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___consonants "Definition of example") + 1}           else -- modified or non-English letter             [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") st         else [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") ([.notALetter](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err___notALetter "Definition of example") c) [loop](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___countLetters___loop "Definition of example") cs   [loop](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___countLetters___loop "Definition of example") str.[toList](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___toList "Documentation for String.toList in Lean Language Reference")`
It would be very easy to write `set st` instead of `[set](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf.set in Lean Language Reference") st'`. In a large program, this kind of mistake can lead to difficult-to-diagnose bugs.
While using a nested action for the call to `get` would solve this problem, it can't solve all such problems. For example, a function might update a field on a structure based on the values of two other fields. This would require two separate nested-action calls to `get`. Because the Lean compiler contains optimizations that are only effective when there is a single reference to a value, duplicating the references to the state might lead to code that is significantly slower. Both the potential performance problem and the potential bug can be worked around by using `[modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference")`, which transforms the state using a function:
`def countLetters (str : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference") Lean Language Reference") :=   let rec loop (chars : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match chars with     | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | c :: cs =>       if c.[isAlpha](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlpha "Documentation for Char.isAlpha in Lean Language Reference") then         if [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___vowels "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation The type class contains a function akin to `modify` called `modifyGet`, which allows the function to both compute a return value and transform an old state in a single step. The function returns a pair in which the first element is the return value, and the second element is the new state; `modify` just adds the constructor of `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` to the pair used in `modifyGet`:
`def modify [[MonadState](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadState___mk "Documentation for MonadState in Lean Language Reference") σ m] (f : σ → σ) : m [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   modifyGet fun s => ((), f s)`
The definition of `MonadState` is as follows:
`class MonadState (σ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type u)) (m : Type u → Type v) :     Type (max (u+1) v) where   get : m σ   set : σ → m [PUnit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#PUnit___unit "Documentation for PUnit in Lean Language Reference")   modifyGet : (σ → α × σ) → m α`
`[PUnit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#PUnit___unit "Documentation for PUnit in Lean Language Reference")` is a version of the `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` type that is universe-polymorphic to allow it to be in `Type u` instead of `Type`. While it would be possible to provide a default implementation of `modifyGet` in terms of `get` and `set`, it would not admit the optimizations that make `modifyGet` useful in the first place, rendering the method useless.
##  6.2.4. `Of` Classes and `The` Functions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=of-and-the "Permalink")
Thus far, each monad type class that takes extra information, like the type of exceptions for `MonadExcept` or the type of the state for `MonadState`, has this type of extra information as an output parameter. For simple programs, this is generally convenient, because a monad that combines one use each of `[StateT](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#St___StateT "Definition of example")`, `[ReaderT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#ReaderT "Documentation for ReaderT in Lean Language Reference")`, and `ExceptT` has only a single state type, environment type, and exception type. As monads grow in complexity, however, they may involve multiple states or errors types. In this case, the use of an output parameter makes it impossible to target both states in the same `do`-block.
For these cases, there are additional type classes in which the extra information is not an output parameter. These versions of the type classes use the word `Of` in the name. For example, `[MonadStateOf](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf in Lean Language Reference")` is like `MonadState`, but without an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")` modifier.
Instead of an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")`, these classes use a `[semiOutParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#semiOutParam "Documentation for semiOutParam in Lean Language Reference")` for their respective state, environment, or exception types. Like an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")`, a `[semiOutParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#semiOutParam "Documentation for semiOutParam in Lean Language Reference")` is not required be known before Lean begins the process of searching for an instance. However, there is an important difference: `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")`s are ignored during the search for an instance, and as a result they are truly outputs. If an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")` is known prior to the search, then Lean merely checks that the result of the search is the same as what was known. On the other hand, a `[semiOutParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#semiOutParam "Documentation for semiOutParam in Lean Language Reference")` that is known prior to the start of the search can be used to narrow down candidates, just like an input parameter.
When a state monad's state type is an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")`, then each monad can have at most one type of state. This is convenient, because it improves type inference: the state type can be inferred in more circumstances. This is also inconvenient, because a monad built from multiple uses of `[StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference")` cannot provide a useful `[MonadState](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadState___mk "Documentation for MonadState in Lean Language Reference")` instance. Using `[MonadStateOf](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf in Lean Language Reference")`, however, causes Lean to take the state type into account when it is available to select which instance to use, so one monad may provide multiple types of state. The downside of this is that the resulting instance may not be the one that was intended when the state type has not been specified explicitly enough, which can lead to confusing error messages.
Similarly, there are versions of the type class methods that accept the type of the extra information as an _explicit_ , rather than implicit, argument. For `[MonadStateOf](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf in Lean Language Reference")`, there are `[getThe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#getThe "Documentation for getThe in Lean Language Reference")` with type
`(σ : Type u) → {m : Type u → Type v} → [[MonadStateOf](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf in Lean Language Reference") σ m] → m σ`
and `[modifyThe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modifyThe "Documentation for modifyThe in Lean Language Reference")` with type
`(σ : Type u) → {m : Type u → Type v} → [[MonadStateOf](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadStateOf___mk "Documentation for MonadStateOf in Lean Language Reference") σ m] → (σ → σ) → m [PUnit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#PUnit___unit "Documentation for PUnit in Lean Language Reference")`
There is no `setThe` because the type of the new state is enough to decide which surrounding state monad transformer to use.
In the Lean standard library, there are instances of the non-`Of` versions of the classes defined in terms of the instances of the versions with `Of`. In other words, implementing the `Of` version yields implementations of both. It's generally a good idea to implement the `Of` version, and then start writing programs using the non-`Of` versions of the class, transitioning to the `Of` version if the output parameter becomes inconvenient.
##  6.2.5. Transformers and `Id`[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=transformers-and-Id "Permalink")
The identity monad `[Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")` is the monad that has no effects whatsoever, to be used in contexts that expect a monad for some reason but where none is actually necessary. Another use of `[Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")` is to serve as the bottom of a stack of monad transformers. For instance, `[StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference") σ [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")` works just like `State σ`.
##  6.2.6. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-transformer-exercises "Permalink")
###  6.2.6.1. Monad Contract
Using pencil and paper, check that the rules of the monad transformer contract are satisfied for each monad transformer in this section.
###  6.2.6.2. Logging Transformer
Define a monad transformer version of `WithLog`. Also define the corresponding type class `MonadWithLog`, and write a program that combines logging and exceptions.
###  6.2.6.3. Counting Files
Modify `doug`'s monad with `[StateT](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#St___StateT "Definition of example")` such that it counts the number of directories and files seen. At the end of execution, it should display a report like:

```
  Viewed 38 files in 5 directories.

```

title: Additional Conveniences
markdown:
[←6.4. More do Features](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#more-do-features "6.4. More do Features")[6.6. Summary→](Monad-Transformers/Summary/#monad-transformer-summary "6.6. Summary")
#  6.5. Additional Conveniences[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-transformer-conveniences "Permalink")
##  6.5.1. Pipe Operators[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=pipe-operators "Permalink")
Functions are normally written before their arguments. When reading a program from left to right, this promotes a view in which the function's _output_ is paramount—the function has a goal to achieve (that is, a value to compute), and it receives arguments to support it in this process. But some programs are easier to understand in terms of an input that is successively refined to produce the output. For these situations, Lean provides a _pipeline_ operator which is similar to the that provided by F#. Pipeline operators are useful in the same situations as Clojure's threading macros.
The pipeline `E₁ |> E₂` is short for `E₂ E₁`. For example, evaluating:
``"(some 5)"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") 5 |> toString`
results in:

```
"(some 5)"
```

While this change of emphasis can make some programs more convenient to read, pipelines really come into their own when they contain many components.
With the definition:
`def times3 (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := n * 3`
the following pipeline:
``"It is 15"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") 5 |> [times3](Monad-Transformers/Additional-Conveniences/#PipelineEx___times3 "Definition of example") |> toString |> ("It is " ++ ·)`
yields:

```
"It is 15"
```

More generally, a series of pipelines `E₁ |> E₂ |> E₃ |> E₄` is short for nested function applications `E₄ (E₃ (E₂ E₁))`.
Pipelines may also be written in reverse. In this case, they do not place the subject of data transformation first; however, in cases where many nested parentheses pose a challenge for readers, they can clarify the steps of application. The prior example could be equivalently written as:
``"It is 15"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ("It is " ++ ·) <| toString <| [times3](Monad-Transformers/Additional-Conveniences/#PipelineEx___times3 "Definition of example") <| 5`
which is short for:
``"It is 15"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ("It is " ++ ·) (toString ([times3](Monad-Transformers/Additional-Conveniences/#PipelineEx___times3 "Definition of example") 5))`
Lean's method dot notation that uses the name of the type before the dot to resolve the namespace of the operator after the dot serves a similar purpose to pipelines. Even without the pipeline operator, it is possible to write `[1, 2, 3].[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")` instead of `[List.reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") [1, 2, 3]`. However, the pipeline operator is also useful for dotted functions when using many of them. `([1, 2, 3].[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference").[drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") 1).[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")` can also be written as `[1, 2, 3] |> [List.reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") |> [List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") 1 |> [List.reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")`. This version avoids having to parenthesize expressions simply because they accept arguments, and it recovers the convenience of a chain of method calls in languages like Kotlin or C#. However, it still requires the namespace to be provided by hand. As a final convenience, Lean provides the “pipeline dot” operator, which groups functions like the pipeline but uses the name of the type to resolve namespaces. With “pipeline dot”, the example can be rewritten to `[1, 2, 3] |>.[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference") |>.[drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") 1 |>.[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")`.
##  6.5.2. Infinite Loops[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=infinite-loops "Permalink")
Within a `do`-block, the `repeat` keyword introduces an infinite loop. For example, a program that spams the string `"Spam!"` can use it:
`def spam : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   repeat [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Spam!"`
A `repeat` loop supports `break` and `continue`, just like `for` loops.
The `dump` function from the [implementation of `feline`](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#streams) uses a recursive function to run forever:
`partial def dump (stream : [IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let buf ← stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference") [bufsize](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#bufsize "Definition of example")   if buf.isEmpty then     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   else     let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")     stdout.[write](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.write in Lean Language Reference") buf     dump stream`
This function can be greatly shortened using `repeat`:
`def dump (stream : [IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")   repeat do     let buf ← stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference") [bufsize](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#bufsize "Definition of example")     if buf.isEmpty then break     stdout.[write](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.write in Lean Language Reference") buf`
Neither `spam` nor `dump` need to be declared as `partial` because they are not themselves infinitely recursive. Instead, `repeat` makes use of a type whose `ForM` instance is `partial`. Partiality does not “infect” calling functions.
##  6.5.3. While Loops[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=while-loops "Permalink")
When programming with local mutability, `while` loops can be a convenient alternative to `repeat` with an `if`-guarded `break`:
`def dump (stream : [IO.FS.Stream](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")   let mut buf ← stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference") [bufsize](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#bufsize "Definition of example")   while [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") buf.isEmpty do     stdout.[write](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.write in Lean Language Reference") buf     buf ← stream.[read](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.read in Lean Language Reference") [bufsize](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#bufsize "Definition of example")`
Behind the scenes, `while` is just a simpler notation for `repeat`.

title: Combining IO and Reader
markdown:
[←6. Monad Transformers](Monad-Transformers/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers "6. Monad Transformers")[6.2. A Monad Construction Kit→](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers--A-Monad-Construction-Kit "6.2. A Monad Construction Kit")
#  6.1. Combining IO and Reader[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=io-reader "Permalink")
One case where a reader monad can be useful is when there is some notion of the “current configuration” of the application that is passed through many recursive calls. An example of such a program is `tree`, which recursively prints the files in the current directory and its subdirectories, indicating their tree structure using characters. The version of `tree` in this chapter, called `doug` after the mighty Douglas Fir tree that adorns the west coast of North America, provides the option of Unicode box-drawing characters or their ASCII equivalents when indicating directory structure.
For example, the following commands create a directory structure and some empty files in a directory called `doug-demo`:
`cd doug-demo``mkdir -p a/b/c``mkdir -p a/d``mkdir -p a/e/f``touch a/b/hello``touch a/d/another-file``touch a/e/still-another-file-again`
Running `doug` results in the following:
`doug``├── doug-demo/ │   ├── a/ │   │   ├── b/ │   │   │   ├── c/ │   │   │   ├── hello │   │   ├── d/ │   │   │   ├── another-file │   │   ├── e/ │   │   │   ├── f/ │   │   │   ├── still-another-file-again `
##  6.1.1. Implementation[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=reader-io-implementation "Permalink")
Internally, `doug` passes a configuration value downwards as it recursively traverses the directory structure. This configuration contains two fields: `useASCII` determines whether to use Unicode box-drawing characters or ASCII vertical line and dash characters to indicate structure, and `currentPrefix` contains a string to prepend to each line of output. As the current directory deepens, the prefix string accumulates indicators of being in a directory. The configuration is a structure:
`structure Config where   useASCII : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") := [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")   currentPrefix : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := ""`
This structure has default definitions for both fields. The default `Config` uses Unicode display with no prefix.
Users who invoke `doug` will need to be able to provide command-line arguments. The usage information is as follows:
`def usage : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   "Usage: doug [--ascii] Options: \t--ascii\tUse ASCII characters to display the directory structure"`
Accordingly, a configuration can be constructed by examining a list of command-line arguments:
`def configFromArgs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") Config   | [] => [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") {} -- both fields default   | ["--ascii"] => [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") {useASCII := [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")}   | _ => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`
The `main` function is a wrapper around an inner worker, called `dirTree`, that shows the contents of a directory using a configuration. Before calling `dirTree`, `main` is responsible for processing command-line arguments. It must also return the appropriate exit code to the operating system:
`def main (args : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match [configFromArgs](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___configFromArgs "Definition of example") args with   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") config =>     dirTree config (← [IO.currentDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___currentDir "Documentation for IO.currentDir in Lean Language Reference"))     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 0   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") =>     [IO.eprintln](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___eprintln "Documentation for IO.eprintln in Lean Language Reference") s!"Didn't understand argument(s) {" ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") args}\n"     [IO.eprintln](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___eprintln "Documentation for IO.eprintln in Lean Language Reference") [usage](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___usage "Definition of example")     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 1`
`[IO.eprintln](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___eprintln "Documentation for IO.eprintln in Lean Language Reference")` is a version of `[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference")` that outputs to standard error.
Not all paths should be shown in the directory tree. In particular, files named `.` or `..` should be skipped, as they are actually features used for navigation rather than files _per se_. Of those files that should be shown, there are two kinds: ordinary files and directories:
`inductive Entry where   | file : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Entry](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry "Definition of example")   | dir : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Entry](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry "Definition of example")`
To determine whether a file should be shown, along with which kind of entry it is, `doug` uses `toEntry`:
`def toEntry (path : [System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [.file](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___file "Definition of example") name))`
`[System.FilePath.components](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___components "Documentation for System.FilePath.components in Lean Language Reference")` converts a path into a list of path components, splitting the name at directory separators. If there is no last component, then the path is the root directory. If the last component is a special navigation file (`.` or `..`), then the file should be excluded. Otherwise, directories and files are wrapped in the corresponding constructors.
Lean's logic has no way to know that directory trees are finite. Indeed, some systems allow the construction of circular directory structures. Thus, `dirTree` is declared `partial`:
`partial def dirTree (cfg : Config) (path : [System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← toEntry path with   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.file](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___file "Definition of example") name) => showFileName cfg name   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.dir](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___dir "Definition of example") name) =>     showDirName cfg name     let contents ← path.[readDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___readDir "Documentation for System.FilePath.readDir in Lean Language Reference") dirLT).toList fun d =>       dirTree newConfig d.[path](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___path "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry.path in Lean Language Reference")`
The call to `toEntry` is a [nested action](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#nested-actions)—the parentheses are optional in positions where the arrow couldn't have any other meaning, such as `match`. When the filename doesn't correspond to an entry in the tree (e.g. because it is `..`), `dirTree` does nothing. When the filename points to an ordinary file, `dirTree` calls a helper to show it with the current configuration. When the filename points to a directory, it is shown with a helper, and then its contents are recursively shown in a new configuration in which the prefix has been extended to account for being in a new directory. The contents of the directory are sorted in order to make the output deterministic, compared according to `dirLT`.
`def dirLT (e1 : [IO.FS.DirEntry](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry in Lean Language Reference")) (e2 : [IO.FS.DirEntry](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry in Lean Language Reference")) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   e1.[fileName](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry.fileName in Lean Language Reference") < e2.[fileName](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry.fileName in Lean Language Reference")`
Showing the names of files and directories is achieved with `showFileName` and `showDirName`:
`def showFileName (cfg : Config) (file : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") (cfg.[fileName](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___fileName "Definition of example") file)  def showDirName (cfg : Config) (dir : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") (cfg.[dirName](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___dirName "Definition of example") dir)`
Both of these helpers delegate to functions on `Config` that take the ASCII vs Unicode setting into account:
`def Config.preFile (cfg : Config) :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") cfg.useASCII [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "|--" [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "├──"  def Config.preDir (cfg : Config) :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") cfg.useASCII [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "|  " [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "│  "  def Config.fileName (cfg : Config) (file : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   s!"{cfg.currentPrefix}{cfg.[preFile](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___preFile "Definition of example")} {file}"  def Config.dirName (cfg : Config) (dir : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   s!"{cfg.currentPrefix}{cfg.[preFile](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___preFile "Definition of example")} {dir}/"`
Similarly, `Config.inDirectory` extends the prefix with a directory marker:
`def Config.inDirectory (cfg : Config) : Config :=   {cfg with currentPrefix := cfg.[preDir](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___preDir "Definition of example") ++ " " ++ cfg.currentPrefix}`
Iterating an IO action over a list of directory contents is achieved using `doList`. Because `doList` carries out all the actions in a list and does not base control-flow decisions on the values returned by any of the actions, the full power of `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` is not necessary, and it will work for any `[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def doList [[Applicative](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Applicative___mk "Documentation for Applicative in Lean Language Reference") f] : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → (α → f [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")) → f [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   | [], _ => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | x :: xs, action =>     action x *>     doList xs action`
##  6.1.2. Using a Custom Monad[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=reader-io-custom-monad "Permalink")
While this implementation of `doug` works, manually passing the configuration around is verbose and error-prone. The type system will not catch it if the wrong configuration is passed downwards, for instance. A reader effect ensures that the same configuration is passed to all recursive calls, unless it is manually overridden, and it helps make the code less verbose.
To create a version of `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` that is also a reader of `Config`, first define the type and its `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance, following the recipe from [the evaluator example](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#custom-environments):
`def ConfigIO (α : Type) : Type :=   Config → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α  instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") ConfigIO where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := fun _ => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") result next := fun cfg => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let v ← result cfg     next v cfg`
The difference between this `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance and the one for `Reader` is that this one uses `do`-notation in the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` monad as the body of the function that `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` returns, rather than applying `next` directly to the value returned from `result`. Any `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` effects performed by `result` must occur before `next` is invoked, which is ensured by the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` monad's `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` operator. `ConfigIO` is not universe polymorphic because the underlying `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` type is also not universe polymorphic.
Running a `ConfigIO` action involves transforming it into an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action by providing it with a configuration:
`def ConfigIO.run (action : ConfigIO α) (cfg : Config) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α :=   action cfg`
This function is not really necessary, as a caller could simply provide the configuration directly. However, naming the operation can make it easier to see which parts of the code are intended to run in which monad.
The next step is to define a means of accessing the current configuration as part of `ConfigIO`:
`def currentConfig : ConfigIO Config :=   fun cfg => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") cfg`
This is just like `read` from [the evaluator example](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#custom-environments), except it uses `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`'s `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` to return its value rather than doing so directly. Because entering a directory modifies the current configuration for the scope of a recursive call, it will be necessary to have a way to override a configuration:
`def locally (change : Config → Config) (action : ConfigIO α) : ConfigIO α :=   fun cfg => action (change cfg)`
Much of the code used in `doug` has no need for configurations, and `doug` calls ordinary Lean `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions from the standard library that certainly don't need a `Config`. Ordinary `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions can be run using `runIO`, which ignores the configuration argument:
`def runIO (action : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α) : ConfigIO α :=   fun _ => action`
With these components, `showFileName` and `showDirName` can be updated to take their configuration arguments implicitly through the `ConfigIO` monad. They use [nested actions](Hello___-World___/Additional-Conveniences/#nested-actions) to retrieve the configuration, and `runIO` to actually execute the call to `[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def showFileName (file : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   runIO ([IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") ((← currentConfig).[fileName](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___fileName "Definition of example") file))  def showDirName (dir : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   runIO ([IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") ((← currentConfig).[dirName](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Config___dirName "Definition of example") dir))`
In the new version of `dirTree`, the calls to `toEntry` and `[readDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___readDir "Documentation for System.FilePath.readDir in Lean Language Reference")` are wrapped in `runIO`. Additionally, instead of building a new configuration and then requiring the programmer to keep track of which one to pass to recursive calls, it uses `locally` to naturally delimit the modified configuration to only a small region of the program, in which it is the _only_ valid configuration:
`partial def dirTree (path : [System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← runIO (toEntry path) with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.file](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___file "Definition of example") name) => showFileName name     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.dir](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___dir "Definition of example") name) =>       showDirName name       let contents ← runIO path.[readDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___readDir "Documentation for System.FilePath.readDir in Lean Language Reference")       locally (·.inDirectory)         (doList (contents.[qsort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___qsort "Documentation for Array.qsort in Lean Language Reference") dirLT).toList fun d =>           dirTree d.[path](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___path "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry.path in Lean Language Reference"))`
The new version of `main` uses `ConfigIO.run` to invoke `dirTree` with the initial configuration:
`def main (args : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO.currentDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___currentDir "Documentation for IO.currentDir in Lean Language Reference"))).run config
