".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") args}\n"       [IO.eprintln](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___eprintln "Documentation for IO.eprintln in Lean Language Reference") [usage](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___usage "Definition of example")       [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 1`
This custom monad has a number of advantages over passing configurations manually:
  1. It is easier to ensure that configurations are passed down unchanged, except when changes are desired
  2. The concern of passing the configuration onwards is more clearly separated from the concern of printing directory contents
  3. As the program grows, there will be more and more intermediate layers that do nothing with configurations except propagate them, and these layers don't need to be rewritten as the configuration logic changes


However, there are also some clear downsides:
  1. As the program evolves and the monad requires more features, each of the basic operators such as `locally` and `currentConfig` will need to be updated
  2. Wrapping ordinary `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions in `runIO` is noisy and distracts from the flow of the program
  3. Writing monads instances by hand is repetitive, and the technique for adding a reader effect to another monad is a design pattern that requires documentation and communication overhead


Using a technique called _monad transformers_ , all of these downsides can be addressed. A monad transformer takes a monad as an argument and returns a new monad. Monad transformers consist of:
  1. A definition of the transformer itself, which is typically a function from types to types
  2. A `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance that assumes the inner type is already a monad
  3. An operator to “lift” an action from the inner monad to the transformed monad, akin to `runIO`


##  6.1.3. Adding a Reader to Any Monad[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=ReaderT "Permalink")
Adding a reader effect to `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` was accomplished in `ConfigIO` by wrapping `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α` in a function type. The Lean standard library contains a function that can do this to _any_ polymorphic type, called `ReaderT`:
`def ReaderT (ρ : Type u) (m : Type u → Type v) (α : Type u) :     Type (max u v) :=   ρ → m α`
Its arguments are as follows:
  * `ρ` is the environment that is accessible to the reader
  * `m` is the monad that is being transformed, such as `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`
  * `α` is the type of values being returned by the monadic computation Both `α` and `ρ` are in the same universe because the operator that retrieves the environment in the monad will have type `m ρ`.


With `ReaderT`, `ConfigIO` becomes:
`abbrev ConfigIO (α : Type) : Type := ReaderT Config [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") α`
It is an `abbrev` because `ReaderT` has many useful features defined in the standard library that a non-reducible definition would hide. Rather than taking responsibility for making these work directly for `ConfigIO`, it's easier to simply have `ConfigIO` behave identically to `ReaderT Config [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`.
The manually-written `currentConfig` obtained the environment out of the reader. This effect can be defined in a generic form for all uses of `ReaderT`, under the name `read`:
`def read [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : ReaderT ρ m ρ :=    fun env => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") env`
However, not every monad that provides a reader effect is built with `ReaderT`. The type class `MonadReader` allows any monad to provide a `read` operator:
`class MonadReader (ρ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type u)) (m : Type u → Type v) :     Type (max (u + 1) v) where   read : m ρ  instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : MonadReader ρ (ReaderT ρ m) where   read := fun env => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") env  [export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") MonadReader (read)`
The type `ρ` is an output parameter because any given monad typically only provides a single type of environment through a reader, so automatically selecting it when the monad is known makes programs more convenient to write.
The `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `ReaderT` is essentially the same as the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `ConfigIO`, except `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` has been replaced by some arbitrary monad argument `m`:
`instance [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (ReaderT ρ m) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := fun _ => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") result next := fun env => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let v ← result env     next v env`
The next step is to eliminate uses of `runIO`. When Lean encounters a mismatch in monad types, it automatically attempts to use a type class called `MonadLift` to transform the actual monad into the expected monad. This process is similar to the use of coercions. `MonadLift` is defined as follows:
`class MonadLift (m : Type u → Type v) (n : Type u → Type w) where   monadLift : {α : Type u} → m α → n α`
The method `monadLift` translates from the monad `m` to the monad `n`. The process is called “lifting” because it takes an action in the embedded monad and makes it into an action in the surrounding monad. In this case, it will be used to “lift” from `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` to `ReaderT Config [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`, though the instance works for _any_ inner monad `m`:
`instance : [MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference") m (ReaderT ρ m) where   [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") action := fun _ => action`
The implementation of `[monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift.monadLift in Lean Language Reference")` is very similar to that of `runIO`. Indeed, it is enough to define `showFileName` and `showDirName` without using `runIO`:
`def showFileName (file : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") s!"{(← read).currentPrefix} {file}"  def showDirName (dir : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") s!"{(← read).currentPrefix} {dir}/"`
One final operation from the original `ConfigIO` remains to be translated to a use of `ReaderT`: `locally`. The definition can be translated directly to `ReaderT`, but the Lean standard library provides a more general version. The standard version is called `withReader`, and it is part of a type class called `MonadWithReader`:
`class MonadWithReader (ρ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (Type u)) (m : Type u → Type v) where   withReader {α : Type u} : (ρ → ρ) → m α → m α`
Just as in `MonadReader`, the environment `ρ` is an `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")`. The `withReader` operation is exported, so that it doesn't need to be written with the type class name before it:
`[export](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___export "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") MonadWithReader (withReader)`
The instance for `ReaderT` is essentially the same as the definition of `locally`:
`instance : MonadWithReader ρ (ReaderT ρ m) where   withReader change action :=     fun cfg => action (change cfg)`
With these definitions in place, the new version of `dirTree` can be written:
`partial def dirTree (path : [System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")) : ConfigIO [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← toEntry path with     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.file](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___file "Definition of example") name) => showFileName name     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") ([.dir](Monad-Transformers/Combining-IO-and-Reader/#DirTree___Entry___dir "Definition of example") name) =>       showDirName name       let contents ← path.[readDir](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___readDir "Documentation for System.FilePath.readDir in Lean Language Reference")       withReader (·.inDirectory)         (doList (contents.[qsort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___qsort "Documentation for Array.qsort in Lean Language Reference") dirLT).toList fun d =>           dirTree d.[path](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___DirEntry___path "Documentation for IO.FS.DirEntry.path in Lean Language Reference"))`
Aside from replacing `locally` with `withReader`, it is the same as before.
Replacing the custom `ConfigIO` type with `ReaderT` did not save a large number of lines of code in this section. However, rewriting the code using components from the standard library does have long-term benefits. First, readers who know about `ReaderT` don't need to take time to understand the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `ConfigIO`, working backwards to the meaning of monad itself. Instead, they can be confident in their initial understanding. Next, adding further effects to the monad (such as a state effect to count the files in each directory and display a count at the end) requires far fewer changes to the code, because the monad transformers and `[MonadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLift___mk "Documentation for MonadLift in Lean Language Reference")` instances provided in the library work well together. Finally, using a set of type classes included in the standard library, polymorphic code can be written in such a way that it can work with a variety of monads without having to care about details like the order in which the monad transformers were applied. Just as some functions work in any monad, others can work in any monad that provides a certain type of state, or a certain type of exceptions, without having to specifically describe the _way_ in which a particular concrete monad provides the state or exceptions.
##  6.1.4. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=reader-io-exercises "Permalink")
###  6.1.4.1. Controlling the Display of Dotfiles
Files whose names begin with a dot character (`'.'`) typically represent files that should usually be hidden, such as source-control metadata and configuration files. Modify `doug` with an option to show or hide filenames that begin with a dot. This option should be controlled with a `-a` command-line option.
###  6.1.4.2. Starting Directory as Argument
Modify `doug` so that it takes a starting directory as an additional command-line argument. 
[←6. Monad Transformers](Monad-Transformers/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers "6. Monad Transformers")[6.2. A Monad Construction Kit→](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Monad-Transformers--A-Monad-Construction-Kit "6.2. A Monad Construction Kit")

title: More do Features
markdown:
[←6.3. Ordering Monad Transformers](Monad-Transformers/Ordering-Monad-Transformers/#monad-transformer-order "6.3. Ordering Monad Transformers")[6.5. Additional Conveniences→](Monad-Transformers/Additional-Conveniences/#monad-transformer-conveniences "6.5. Additional Conveniences")
#  6.4. More do Features[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=more-do-features "Permalink")
Lean's `do`-notation provides a syntax for writing programs with monads that resembles imperative programming languages. In addition to providing a convenient syntax for programs with monads, `do`-notation provides syntax for using certain monad transformers.
##  6.4.1. Single-Branched `if`[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=single-branched-if "Permalink")
When working in a monad, a common pattern is to carry out a side effect only if some condition is true. For instance, `countLetters` contains a check for vowels or consonants, and letters that are neither have no effect on the state. This is captured by having the `else` branch evaluate to `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()`, which has no effects:
`def countLetters (str : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference") [LetterCounts](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts "Definition of example") ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err "Definition of example")) [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   let rec loop (chars : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match chars with     | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | c :: cs =>       if c.[isAlpha](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlpha "Documentation for Char.isAlpha in Lean Language Reference") then         if [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___vowels "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation for String.contains in Lean Language Reference") c then           [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") fun st => {st with [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___vowels "Definition of example") := st.[vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___vowels "Definition of example") + 1}         else if [consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___consonants "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation for String.contains in Lean Language Reference") c then           [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") fun st => {st with [consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___consonants "Definition of example") := st.[consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___consonants "Definition of example") + 1}         else -- modified or non-English letter           [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()       else [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") ([.notALetter](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err___notALetter "Definition of example") c)       loop cs   loop str.[toList](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___toList "Documentation for String.toList in Lean Language Reference")`
When an `if` is a statement in a `do`-block, rather than being an expression, then `else [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()` can simply be omitted, and Lean inserts it automatically. The following definition of `countLetters` is completely equivalent:
`def countLetters (str : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [StateT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#StateT "Documentation for StateT in Lean Language Reference") [LetterCounts](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts "Definition of example") ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [Err](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err "Definition of example")) [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   let rec loop (chars : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     match chars with     | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()     | c :: cs =>       if c.[isAlpha](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlpha "Documentation for Char.isAlpha in Lean Language Reference") then         if [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___vowels "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation for String.contains in Lean Language Reference") c then           [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") fun st => {st with [vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___vowels "Definition of example") := st.[vowels](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___vowels "Definition of example") + 1}         else if [consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___consonants "Definition of example").[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation for String.contains in Lean Language Reference") c then           [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") fun st => {st with [consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___consonants "Definition of example") := st.[consonants](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___LetterCounts___consonants "Definition of example") + 1}       else [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") ([.notALetter](Monad-Transformers/A-Monad-Construction-Kit/#StEx___Err___notALetter "Definition of example") c)       [loop](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#StEx___FancyDo___countLetters___loop "Definition of example") cs   [loop](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#StEx___FancyDo___countLetters___loop "Definition of example") str.[toList](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___toList "Documentation for String.toList in Lean Language Reference")`
A program that uses a state monad to count the entries in a list that satisfy some monadic check can be written as follows:
`def count [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadState](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadState___mk "Documentation for MonadState in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") m] (p : α → m [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | x :: xs => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     if ← p x then       [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") (· + 1)     count p xs`
Similarly, `if not E1 then STMT...` can instead be written `unless E1 do STMT...`. The converse of `count` that counts entries that don't satisfy the monadic check can be written by replacing `if` with `unless`:
`def countNot [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] [[MonadState](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadState___mk "Documentation for MonadState in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") m] (p : α → m [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()   | x :: xs => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     unless ← p x do       [modify](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#modify "Documentation for modify in Lean Language Reference") (· + 1)     [countNot](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#ThenDoUnless___countNot "Definition of example") p xs`
Understanding single-branched `if` and `unless` does not require thinking about monad transformers. They simply replace the missing branch with `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()`. The remaining extensions in this section, however, require Lean to automatically rewrite the `do`-block to add a local transformer on top of the monad that the `do`-block is written in.
##  6.4.2. Early Return[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=early-return "Permalink")
The standard library contains a function `List.find?` that returns the first entry in a list that satisfies some check. A simple implementation that doesn't make use of the fact that `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` is a monad loops over the list using a recursive function, with an `if` to stop the loop when the desired entry is found:
`def List.find? (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | [] => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | x :: xs =>     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p x [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x     [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [find?](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#EarlyReturn___Non___List___find___ "Definition of example") p xs`
Imperative languages typically sport the `return` keyword that aborts the execution of a function, immediately returning some value to the caller. In Lean, this is available in `do`-notation, and `return` halts the execution of a `do`-block, with `return`'s argument being the value returned from the monad. In other words, `List.find?` could have been written like this:
`def List.find? (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | [] => failure   | x :: xs => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     if p x then return x     find? p xs`
Early return in imperative languages is a bit like an exception that can only cause the current stack frame to be unwound. Both early return and exceptions terminate execution of a block of code, effectively replacing the surrounding code with the thrown value. Behind the scenes, early return in Lean is implemented using a version of `[ExceptT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#ExceptT "Documentation for ExceptT in Lean Language Reference")`. Each `do`-block that uses early return is wrapped in an exception handler (in the sense of the function `tryCatch`). Early returns are translated to throwing the value as an exception, and the handlers catch the thrown value and return it immediately. In other words, the `do`-block's original return value type is also used as the exception type.
Making this more concrete, the helper function `runCatch` strips a layer of `[ExceptT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#ExceptT "Documentation for ExceptT in Lean Language Reference")` from the top of a monad transformer stack when the exception type and return type are the same:
`def runCatch [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (action : [ExceptT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#ExceptT "Documentation for ExceptT in Lean Language Reference") α m α) : m α := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match ← action with   | [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x   | [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") x => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x`
The `do`-block in `List.find?` that uses early return is translated to a `do`-block that does not use early return by wrapping it in a use of `runCatch`, and replacing early returns with `[throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def List.find? (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | [] => failure   | x :: xs =>     runCatch [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       if p x then [throw](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#MonadExcept___mk "Documentation for MonadExcept.throw in Lean Language Reference") x else [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()       [monadLift](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Lifting-Monads/#MonadLiftT___mk "Documentation for MonadLiftT.monadLift in Lean Language Reference") ([find?](Monad-Transformers/More-do-Features/#EarlyReturn___Desugared___List___find___ "Definition of example") p xs)`
Another situation in which early return is useful is command-line applications that terminate early if the arguments or input are incorrect. Many programs begin with a section that validates arguments and inputs before proceeding to the main body of the program. The following version of [the greeting program `hello-name`](Hello___-World___/Running-a-Program/#running-a-program) checks that no command-line arguments were provided:
`def main (argv : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : Running it with no arguments and typing the name `David` yields the same result as the previous version:
`lean --run EarlyReturn.lean``How would you like to be addressed? David Hello, David! `
Providing the name as a command-line argument instead of an answer causes an error:
`lean --run EarlyReturn.lean David``Expected no arguments, but got 1 `
And providing no name causes the other error:
`lean --run EarlyReturn.lean``How would you like to be addressed?  No name provided `
