#  4. Monads[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads "Permalink")
In C# and Kotlin, the `?.` operator is a way to look up a property or call a method on a potentially-null value. If the receiver is `null`, the whole expression is null. Otherwise, the underlying non-`null` value receives the call. Uses of `?.` can be chained, in which case the first `null` result terminates the chain of lookups. Chaining null-checks like this is much more convenient than writing and maintaining deeply nested `if`s.
Similarly, exceptions are significantly more convenient than manually checking and propagating error codes. At the same time, logging is easiest to accomplish by having a dedicated logging framework, rather than having each function return both its log results and its return value. Chained null checks and exceptions typically require language designers to anticipate this use case, while logging frameworks typically make use of side effects to decouple code that logs from the accumulation of the logs.
  1. [4.1. One API, Many Applications](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#monad-api-examples)

#  4.6. Additional Conveniences[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-conveniences "Permalink")
##  4.6.1. Shared Argument Types[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=shared-argument-types "Permalink")
When defining a function that takes multiple arguments that have the same type, both can be written before the same colon. For example,
`def equal? [[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference") α] (x : α) (y : α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x == y [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`
can be written
`def equal? [[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference") α] (x y : α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x == y [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`
This is especially useful when the type signature is large.
##  4.6.2. Leading Dot Notation[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=leading-dot-notation "Permalink")
The constructors of an inductive type are in a namespace. This allows multiple related inductive types to use the same constructor names, but it can lead to programs becoming verbose. In contexts where the inductive type in question is known, the namespace can be omitted by preceding the constructor's name with a dot, and Lean uses the expected type to resolve the constructor names. For example, a function that mirrors a binary tree can be written:
`def BinTree.mirror : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α   | [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")   | [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") l x r => [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") ([mirror](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Old___BinTree___mirror "Definition of example") r) x ([mirror](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Old___BinTree___mirror "Definition of example") l)`
Omitting the namespaces makes it significantly shorter, at the cost of making the program harder to read in contexts like code review tools that don't include the Lean compiler:
`def BinTree.mirror : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α   | [.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => .leaf   | [.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") l x r => [.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") (mirror r) x (mirror l)`
Using the expected type of an expression to disambiguate a namespace is also applicable to names other than constructors. If `BinTree.empty` is defined as an alternative way of creating `[BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example")`s, then it can also be used with dot notation:
`def BinTree.empty : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α := [.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")```BinTree.empty : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (.empty : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
BinTree.empty : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```

##  4.6.3. Or-Patterns[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=or-patterns "Permalink")
In contexts that allow multiple patterns, such as `match`-expressions, multiple patterns may share their result expressions. The datatype `Weekday` that represents days of the week:
`inductive Weekday where   | monday   | tuesday   | wednesday   | thursday   | friday   | saturday   | sunday deriving [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")`
Pattern matching can be used to check whether a day is a weekend:
`def Weekday.isWeekend (day : Weekday) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") day [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [Weekday.saturday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___saturday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | [Weekday.sunday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___sunday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | _ => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`
This can already be simplified by using constructor dot notation:
`def Weekday.isWeekend (day : Weekday) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") day [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [.saturday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___saturday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | [.sunday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___sunday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | _ => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`
Because both weekend patterns have the same result expression (`[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`), they can be condensed into one:
`def Weekday.isWeekend (day : Weekday) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") day [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [.saturday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___saturday "Definition of example") | [.sunday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___sunday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | _ => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`
This can be further simplified into a version in which the argument is not named:
`def Weekday.isWeekend : Weekday → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")   | [.saturday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___saturday "Definition of example") | [.sunday](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#Weekday___sunday "Definition of example") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | _ => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`
Behind the scenes, the result expression is simply duplicated across each pattern. This means that patterns can bind variables, as in this example that removes the `[inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference")` and `[inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference")` constructors from a sum type in which both contain the same type of value:
`def condense : α ⊕ α → α   | [.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") x | [.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") x => x`
Because the result expression is duplicated, the variables bound by the patterns are not required to have the same types. Overloaded functions that work for multiple types may be used to write a single result expression that works for patterns that bind variables of different types:
`def stringy : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ⊕ Weekday → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   | [.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") x | [.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") x => s!"It is {[repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#repr-next "Documentation for repr in Lean Language Reference") x}"`
In practice, only variables shared in all patterns can be referred to in the result expression, because the result must make sense for each pattern. In `getTheNat`, only `n` can be accessed, and attempts to use either `x` or `y` lead to errors.
`def getTheNat : ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) ⊕ ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × β) → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   | [.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") (n, x) | [.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") (n, y) => n`
Attempting to access `x` in a similar definition causes an error because there is no `x` available in the second pattern:
`def getTheAlpha : ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) ⊕ ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) → α   | [.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") (n, x) | [.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") (n, y) => `Unknown identifier `x``x`
```
Unknown identifier `x`
```

The fact that the result expression is essentially copy-pasted to each branch of the pattern match can lead to some surprising behavior. For example, the following definitions are acceptable because the `[inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference")` version of the result expression refers to the global definition of `str`:
`def str := "Some string"  def getTheString : ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) ⊕ ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × β) → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   | [.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") (n, str) | [.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") (n, y) => str`
Calling this function on both constructors reveals the confusing behavior. In the first case, a type annotation is needed to tell Lean which type `β` should be:
``"twenty"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [getTheString](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#getTheString "Definition of example") ([.inl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inl in Lean Language Reference") (20, "twenty") : ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) ⊕ ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")))`
```
"twenty"
```

In the second case, the global definition is used:
``"Some string"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [getTheString](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#getTheString "Definition of example") ([.inr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Sum-Types/#Sum___inl "Documentation for Sum.inr in Lean Language Reference") (20, "twenty"))`
```
"Some string"
```

Using or-patterns can vastly simplify some definitions and increase their clarity, as in `Weekday.isWeekend`. Because there is a potential for confusing behavior, it's a good idea to be careful when using them, especially when variables of multiple types or disjoint sets of variables are involved. 
[←4.5. The IO Monad](Monads/The-IO-Monad/#io-monad "4.5. The IO Monad")[4.7. Summary→](Monads/Summary/#monads-summary "4.7. Summary")

title: Example: Arithmetic in Monads
markdown:
[←4.2. The Monad Type Class](Monads/The-Monad-Type-Class/#monad-type-class "4.2. The Monad Type Class")[4.4. do-Notation for Monads→](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#monad-do-notation "4.4. do-Notation for Monads")
#  4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example "Permalink")
Monads are a way of encoding programs with side effects into a language that does not have them. It would be easy to read this as a sort of admission that pure functional programs are missing something important, requiring programmers to jump through hoops just to write a normal program. However, while using the `Monad` API does impose a syntactic cost on a program, it brings two important benefits:
  1. Programs must be honest about which effects they use in their types. A quick glance at a type signature describes _everything_ that the program can do, rather than just what it accepts and what it returns.
  2. Not every language provides the same effects. For example, only some languages have exceptions. Other languages have unique, exotic effects, such as [Icon's searching over multiple values](https://www2.cs.arizona.edu/icon/) and Scheme or Ruby's continuations. Because monads can encode _any_ effect, programmers can choose which ones are the best fit for a given application, rather than being stuck with what the language developers provided.


One example of a program that can make sense in a variety of monads is an evaluator for arithmetic expressions.
##  4.3.1. Arithmetic Expressions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-expr "Permalink")
An arithmetic expression is either a literal integer or a primitive binary operator applied to two expressions. The operators are addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division:
`inductive Expr (op : Type) where   | const : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") op   | prim : op → [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") op → [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") op → [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") op   inductive Arith where   | plus   | minus   | times   | div`
The expression `2 + 3` is represented:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expr [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Arith [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") def twoPlusThree : [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") :=   [prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___plus "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 2) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 3)`
and `14 / (45 - 5 * 9)` is represented:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expr [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Arith [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") def fourteenDivided : [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") :=   [prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [div](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___div "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 14)     ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___minus "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 45)       ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [times](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___times "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 5)         ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 9)))`
##  4.3.2. Evaluating Expressions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-eval "Permalink")
Because expressions include division, and division by zero is undefined, evaluation might fail. One way to represent failure is to use `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def evaluateOption : [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | [Expr.const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") i => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i   | [Expr.prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") p e1 e2 =>     [evaluateOption](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#One___evaluateOption "Definition of example") e1 >>= fun v1 =>     [evaluateOption](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#One___evaluateOption "Definition of example") e2 >>= fun v2 =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [Arith.plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___plus "Definition of example") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (v1 + v2)     | [Arith.minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___minus "Definition of example") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (v1 - v2)     | [Arith.times](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___times "Definition of example") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (v1 * v2)     | [Arith.div](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___div "Definition of example") => [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") v2 == 0 [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (v1 / v2)`
This definition uses the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` instance to propagate failures from evaluating both branches of a binary operator. However, the function mixes two concerns: evaluating subexpressions and applying a binary operator to the results. It can be improved by splitting it into two functions:
`def applyPrim : [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x + y)   | [Arith.minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___minus "Definition of example"), x, y => "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x * y)   | [Arith.div](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___div "Definition of example"), x, y => [Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | [Expr.const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") i => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i   | [Expr.prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") p e1 e2 =>     [evaluateOption](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Two___evaluateOption "Definition of example") e1 >>= fun v1 =>     [evaluateOption](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Two___evaluateOption "Definition of example") e2 >>= fun v2 =>     [applyPrim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Two___applyPrim "Definition of example") p v1 v2`
Running `[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [evaluateOption](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Two___evaluateOption "Definition of example") [fourteenDivided](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#fourteenDivided "Definition of example")` yields `none`, as expected, but this is not a very useful error message. Because the code was written using `>>=` rather than by explicitly handling the `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` constructor, only a small modification is required for it to provide an error message on failure:
`def applyPrim : [Arith](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith "Definition of example") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x + y)   | [Arith.minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___minus "Definition of example"), x, y => "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x * y)   | [Arith.div](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Arith___div "Definition of example"), x, y =>     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") y == 0 [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i   | [Expr.prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") p e1 e2 =>     [evaluateExcept](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Three___evaluateExcept "Definition of example") e1 >>= fun v1 =>     [evaluateExcept](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Three___evaluateExcept "Definition of example") e2 >>= fun v2 =>     [applyPrim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Three___applyPrim "Definition of example") p v1 v2`
The only difference is that the type signature mentions `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` instead of `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, and the failing case uses `[Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference")` instead of `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`. By making the evaluator polymorphic over its monad and passing it `applyPrim` as an argument, a single evaluator becomes capable of both forms of error reporting:

##  4.3.3. Further Effects[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-effects "Permalink")
Failure and exceptions are not the only kinds of effects that can be interesting when working with an evaluator. While division's only side effect is failure, adding other primitive operators to the expressions make it possible to express other effects.
The first step is an additional refactoring, extracting division from the datatype of primitives:
`inductive Prim (special : Type) where   | plus   | minus   | times   | other : special → Prim special  inductive CanFail where   | div`
The name `CanFail` suggests that the effect introduced by division is potential failure.
The second step is to broaden the scope of the division handler argument to `evaluateM` so that it can process any special operator:

###  4.3.3.1. No Effects[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-no-effects "Permalink")
The type `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` has no constructors, and thus no values, like the `Nothing` type in Scala or Kotlin. In Scala and Kotlin, `Nothing` can represent computations that never return a result, such as functions that crash the program, throw exceptions, or always fall into infinite loops. An argument to a function or method of type `Nothing` indicates dead code, as there will never be a suitable argument value. Lean doesn't support infinite loops and exceptions, but `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` is still useful as an indication to the type system that a function cannot be called. Using the syntax `[nomatch](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___nomatch "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") E` when `E` is an expression whose type has no constructors indicates to Lean that the current expression need not return a result, because it could never have been called.
Using `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` as the parameter to `Prim` indicates that there are no additional cases beyond `[Prim.plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___plus "Definition of example")`, `[Prim.minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___minus "Definition of example")`, and `[Prim.times](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___times "Definition of example")`, because it is impossible to come up with a value of type `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` to place in the `[Prim.other](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___other "Definition of example")` constructor. Because a function to apply an operator of type `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` to two integers can never be called, it doesn't need to return a result. Thus, it can be used in _any_ monad:
`def applyEmpty [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (op : [Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")) (_ : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) (_ : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : m [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   [nomatch](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___nomatch "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") op`
This can be used together with `[Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")`, the identity monad, to evaluate expressions that have no effects whatsoever:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expr Prim [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `-9`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") evaluateM (m := [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")) [applyEmpty](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#applyEmpty "Definition of example") ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___plus "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 5) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") (-14)))`
```
-9
```

###  4.3.3.2. Nondeterministic Search[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=nondeterministic-search "Permalink")
Instead of simply failing when encountering division by zero, it would also be sensible to backtrack and try a different input. Given the right monad, the very same `evaluateM` can perform a nondeterministic search for a _set_ of answers that do not result in failure. This requires, in addition to division, some means of specifying a choice of results. One way to do this is to add a function `choose` to the language of expressions that instructs the evaluator to pick either of its arguments while searching for non-failing results.
The result of the evaluator is now a multiset of values, rather than a single value. The rules for evaluation into a multiset are:
  * Constants `nnn` evaluate to singleton sets `{n}\{n\}{n}`.
  * Arithmetic operators other than division are called on each pair from the Cartesian product of the operators, so `X+YX + YX+Y` evaluates to `{x+y∣x∈X,y∈Y}\{ x + y \mid x ∈ X, y ∈ Y \}{x+y∣x∈X,y∈Y}`.
  * Division `X/YX / YX/Y` evaluates to `{x/y∣x∈X,y∈Y,y≠0}\{ x / y \mid x ∈ X, y ∈ Y, y ≠ 0\}{x/y∣x∈X,y∈Y,y=0}`. In other words, all `000` values in `YYY` are thrown out.
  * A choice `choose(x,y)\mathrm{choose}(x, y)choose(x,y)` evaluates to `{x,y}\{ x, y \}{x,y}`.


For example, `1+choose(2,5)1 + \mathrm{choose}(2, 5)1+choose(2,5)` evaluates to `{3,6}\{ 3, 6 \}{3,6}`, `1+2/01 + 2 / 01+2/0` evaluates to `{}\{\}{}`, and `90/(choose(−5,5)+5)90 / (\mathrm{choose}(-5, 5) + 5)90/(choose(−5,5)+5)` evaluates to `{9}\{ 9 \}{9}`. Using multisets instead of true sets simplifies the code by removing the need to check for uniqueness of elements.
A monad that represents this non-deterministic effect must be able to represent a situation in which there are no answers, and a situation in which there is at least one answer together with any remaining answers:
`inductive Many (α : Type) where   | none : Many α   | more : α → ([Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") → Many α) → Many α`
This datatype looks very much like `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`. The difference is that where `[List.cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")` stores the rest of the list, `more` stores a function that should compute the remaining values on demand. This means that a consumer of `Many` can stop the search when some number of results have been found.
A single result is represented by a `more` constructor that returns no further results:
`def Many.one (x : α) : Many α := Many.more x (fun () => [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example"))`
The union of two multisets of results can be computed by checking whether the first multiset is empty. If so, the second multiset is the union. If not, the union consists of the first element of the first multiset followed by the union of the rest of the first multiset with the second multiset:
`def Many.union : Many α → Many α → Many α   | [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example"), ys => ys   | Many.more x xs, ys => Many.more x (fun () => union (xs ()) ys)`
It can be convenient to start a search process with a list of values. `Many.fromList` converts a list into a multiset of results:
`def Many.fromList : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → Many α   | [] => [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example")   | x :: xs => Many.more x (fun () => fromList xs)`
Similarly, once a search has been specified, it can be convenient to extract either a number of values, or all the values:
`def Many.take : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Many α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α   | 0, _ => []   | _ + 1, [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example") => []   | n + 1, Many.more x xs => x :: (xs ()).[take](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___take "Definition of example") n  def Many.takeAll : Many α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α   | [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example") => []   | Many.more x xs => x :: (xs ()).[takeAll](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___takeAll "Definition of example")`
A `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") Many` instance requires a `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` operator. In a nondeterministic search, sequencing two operations consists of taking all possibilities from the first step and running the rest of the program on each of them, taking the union of the results. In other words, if the first step returns three possible answers, the second step needs to be tried for all three. Because the second step can return any number of answers for each input, taking their union represents the entire search space.
`def Many.bind : Many α → (α → Many β) → Many β   | [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example"), _ =>     [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example")   | Many.more x xs, f =>     (f x).union (bind (xs ()) f)`
`Many.one` and `Many.bind` obey the monad contract. To check that `Many.bind (Many.one v) f` is the same as `f v`, start by evaluating the expression as far as possible:
`Many.bind (Many.one v) f``Many.bind (Many.more v (fun () => [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example"))) f``(f v).union (Many.bind [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example") f)``(f v).union [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example")`
The empty multiset is a right identity of `union`, so the answer is equivalent to `f v`. To check that `Many.bind v Many.one` is the same as `v`, consider that `Many.bind` takes the union of applying `Many.one` to each element of `v`. In other words, if `v` has the form `{v₁, v₂, v₃, …, vₙ}`, then `Many.bind v Many.one` is `{v₁} ∪ {v₂} ∪ {v₃} ∪ … ∪ {vₙ}`, which is `{v₁, v₂, v₃, …, vₙ}`.
Finally, to check that `Many.bind` is associative, check that `Many.bind (Many.bind v f) g` is the same as `Many.bind v (fun x => Many.bind (f x) g)`. If `v` has the form `{v₁, v₂, v₃, …, vₙ}`, then:
`Many.bind v f``f v₁ ∪ f v₂ ∪ f v₃ ∪ … ∪ f vₙ`
which means that
`Many.bind (Many.bind v f) g``Many.bind (f v₁) g ∪ Many.bind (f v₂) g ∪ Many.bind (f v₃) g ∪ … ∪ Many.bind (f vₙ) g`
Similarly,
`Many.bind v (fun x => Many.bind (f x) g)``(fun x => Many.bind (f x) g) v₁ ∪ (fun x => Many.bind (f x) g) v₂ ∪ (fun x => Many.bind (f x) g) v₃ ∪ … ∪ (fun x => Many.bind (f x) g) vₙ``Many.bind (f v₁) g ∪ Many.bind (f v₂) g ∪ Many.bind (f v₃) g ∪ … ∪ Many.bind (f vₙ) g`
Thus, both sides are equal, so `Many.bind` is associative.
The resulting monad instance is:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") Many where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") := Many.one   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") := Many.bind`
An example search using this monad finds all the combinations of numbers in a list that add to 15:
`def addsTo (goal : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") []     [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example")   | x :: xs =>     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") x > goal [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [addsTo](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#addsTo "Definition of example") goal xs     [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       ([addsTo](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#addsTo "Definition of example") goal xs).union         ([addsTo](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#addsTo "Definition of example") (goal - x) xs >>= fun answer =>          [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (x :: answer))`
The search process is recursive over the list. The empty list is a successful search when the goal is `0`; otherwise, it fails. When the list is non-empty, there are two possibilities: either the head of the list is greater than the goal, in which case it cannot participate in any successful searches, or it is not, in which case it can. If the head of the list is _not_ a candidate, then the search proceeds to the tail of the list. If the head is a candidate, then there are two possibilities to be combined with `Many.union`: either the solutions found contain the head, or they do not. The solutions that do not contain the head are found with a recursive call on the tail, while the solutions that do contain it result from subtracting the head from the goal, and then attaching the head to the solutions that result from the recursive call.
The helper `printList` ensures that one result is displayed per line:

Returning to the arithmetic evaluator that produces multisets of results, the `choose` operator can be used to nondeterministically select a value, with division by zero rendering prior selections invalid.
`inductive NeedsSearch   | div   | choose  def applySearch : [NeedsSearch](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#NeedsSearch "Definition of example") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → Many [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | NeedsSearch.choose, x, y =>     Many.fromList [x, y]   | [NeedsSearch.div](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#NeedsSearch___div "Definition of example"), x, y =>     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") y == 0 [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [Many.none](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Many___none "Definition of example")     [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Many.one (x / y)`

###  4.3.3.3. Custom Environments[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=custom-environments "Permalink")
The evaluator can be made user-extensible by allowing strings to be used as operators, and then providing a mapping from strings to a function that implements them. For example, users could extend the evaluator with a remainder operator or with one that returns the maximum of its two arguments. The mapping from function names to function implementations is called an _environment_.
The environments needs to be passed in each recursive call. Initially, it might seem that `evaluateM` needs an extra argument to hold the environment, and that this argument should be passed to each recursive invocation. However, passing an argument like this is another form of monad, so an appropriate `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance allows the evaluator to be used unchanged.
Using functions as a monad is typically called a _reader_ monad. When evaluating expressions in the reader monad, the following rules are used:
  * Constants `nnn` evaluate to constant functions `λe.nλ e . nλe.n`,
  * Arithmetic operators evaluate to functions that pass their arguments on, so `f+gf + gf+g` evaluates to `λe.f(e)+g(e)λ e . f(e) + g(e)λe.f(e)+g(e)`, and
  * Custom operators evaluate to the result of applying the custom operator to the arguments, so `f OP gf \ \mathrm{OP}\ gf OP g` evaluates to `λe.{h(f(e),g(e))if e contains (OP,h)0otherwise      λ e .      \begin{cases}      h(f(e), g(e)) & \mathrm{if}\ e\ \mathrm{contains}\ (\mathrm{OP}, h) \\      0 & \mathrm{otherwise}      \end{cases}    λe.{h(f(e),g(e))0​if e contains (OP,h)otherwise​` with `000` serving as a fallback in case an unknown operator is applied.


To define the reader monad in Lean, the first step is to define the `Reader` type and the effect that allows users to get ahold of the environment:
`def Reader (ρ : Type) (α : Type) : Type := ρ → α  def read : Reader ρ ρ := fun env => env`
By convention, the Greek letter `ρ`, which is pronounced “rho”, is used for environments.
The fact that constants in arithmetic expressions evaluate to constant functions suggests that the appropriate definition of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` for `Reader` is a a constant function:
`def Reader.pure (x : α) : Reader ρ α := fun _ => x`
On the other hand, `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` is a bit tricker. Its type is `Reader ρ α → (α → Reader ρ β) → Reader ρ β`. This type can be easier to understand by unfolding the definition of `Reader`, which yields `(ρ → α) → (α → ρ → β) → (ρ → β)`. It should take an environment-accepting function as its first argument, while the second argument should transform the result of the environment-accepting function into yet another environment-accepting function. The result of combining these is itself a function, waiting for an environment.
It's possible to use Lean interactively to get help writing this function. The first step is to write down the arguments and return type, being very explicit in order to get as much help as possible, with an underscore for the definition's body:
`def [Reader.bind](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Temp___Reader___bind "Definition of example") {ρ : Type} {α : Type} {β : Type}   (result : ρ → α) (next : α → ρ → β) : ρ → β :=   `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → β⊢ ρ → β`_`
Lean provides a message that describes which variables are available in scope, and the type that's expected for the result. The `⊢` symbol, called a _turnstile_ due to its resemblance to subway entrances, separates the local variables from the desired type, which is `ρ → β` in this message:

```

context:
ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → β⊢ ρ → β
```

Because the return type is a function, a good first step is to wrap a `fun` around the underscore:
`def [Reader.bind](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Temp___Reader___bind "Definition of example") {ρ : Type} {α : Type} {β : Type}   (result : ρ → α) (next : α → ρ → β) : ρ → β :=   fun env => `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ β`_`
The resulting message now shows the function's argument as a local variable:

```
don't know how to synthesize placeholder
context:
ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ β
```

The only thing in the context that can produce a `β` is `next`, and it will require two arguments to do so. Each argument can itself be an underscore:
`def [Reader.bind](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Temp___Reader___bind "Definition of example") {ρ : Type} {α : Type} {β : Type}   (result : ρ → α) (next : α → ρ → β) : ρ → β :=   fun env => next `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ α`_ `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ ρ`_`
The two underscores have the following respective messages associated with them:

```
don't know how to synthesize placeholder
context:
ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ α
```

```
don't know how to synthesize placeholder
context:
ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ ρ
```

Attacking the first underscore, only one thing in the context can produce an `α`, namely `result`:
`def [Reader.bind](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Temp___Reader___bind "Definition of example") {ρ : Type} {α : Type} {β : Type}   (result : ρ → α) (next : α → ρ → β) : ρ → β :=   fun env => next (result `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ ρ`_) `don't know how to synthesize placeholder context: ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ ρ`_`
Now, both underscores have the same error message:

```
don't know how to synthesize placeholder
context:
ρ α β:Typeresult:ρ → αnext:α → ρ → βenv:ρ⊢ ρ
```

Happily, both underscores can be replaced by `env`, yielding:
`def Reader.bind {ρ : Type} {α : Type} {β : Type}   (result : ρ → α) (next : α → ρ → β) : ρ → β :=   fun env => next (result env) env`
The final version can be obtained by undoing the unfolding of `Reader` and cleaning up the explicit details:
`def Reader.bind     (result : Reader ρ α)     (next : α → Reader ρ β) : Reader ρ β :=   fun env => next (result env) env`
It's not always possible to write correct functions by simply “following the types”, and it carries the risk of not understanding the resulting program. However, it can also be easier to understand a program that has been written than one that has not, and the process of filling in the underscores can bring insights. In this case, `Reader.bind` works just like `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `Id`, except it accepts an additional argument that it then passes down to its arguments, and this intuition can help in understanding how it works.
`Reader.pure` (which generates constant functions) and `Reader.bind` obey the monad contract. To check that `Reader.bind (Reader.pure v) f` is the same as `f v`, it's enough to replace definitions until the last step:
`Reader.bind (Reader.pure v) f``fun env => f ((Reader.pure v) env) env``fun env => f ((fun _ => v) env) env``fun env => f v env``f v`
For every function `f`, `fun x => f x` is the same as `f`, so the first part of the contract is satisfied. To check that `Reader.bind r Reader.pure` is the same as `r`, a similar technique works:
`Reader.bind r Reader.pure``fun env => Reader.pure (r env) env``fun env => (fun _ => (r env)) env``fun env => r env`
Because reader actions `r` are themselves functions, this is the same as `r`. To check associativity, the same thing can be done for both `Reader.bind (Reader.bind r f) g` and `Reader.bind r (fun x => Reader.bind (f x) g)`:
`Reader.bind (Reader.bind r f) g``fun env => g ((Reader.bind r f) env) env``fun env => g ((fun env' => f (r env') env') env) env``fun env => g (f (r env) env) env`
`Reader.bind r (fun x => Reader.bind (f x) g)` reduces to the same expression:
`Reader.bind r (fun x => Reader.bind (f x) g)``Reader.bind r (fun x => fun env => g (f x env) env)``fun env => (fun x => fun env' => g (f x env') env') (r env) env``fun env => (fun env' => g (f (r env) env') env') env``fun env => g (f (r env) env) env`
Thus, a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (Reader ρ)` instance is justified:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (Reader ρ) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := fun _ => x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") x f := fun env => f (x env) env`
The custom environments that will be passed to the expression evaluator can be represented as lists of pairs:
`abbrev Env : Type := [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") × ([Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")))`
For instance, `exampleEnv` contains maximum and modulus functions:
`def exampleEnv : [Env](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Env "Definition of example") := [("max", [max](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Max___mk "Documentation for Max.max in Lean Language Reference")), ("mod", (· % ·))]`
Lean already has a function `[List.lookup](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___lookup "Documentation for List.lookup in Lean Language Reference")` that finds the value associated with a key in a list of pairs, so `applyPrimReader` needs only check whether the custom function is present in the environment. It returns `0` if the function is unknown:
`def applyPrimReader (op : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (x : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) (y : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : Reader [Env](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Env "Definition of example") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   read >>= fun env =>   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") env.[lookup](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___lookup "Documentation for List.lookup in Lean Language Reference") op [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 0   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") f => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (f x y)`
Using `evaluateM` with `applyPrimReader` and an expression results in a function that expects an environment. Luckily, `exampleEnv` is available:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expr Prim [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `9`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") evaluateM applyPrimReader ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") ([other](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___other "Definition of example") "max") ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___plus "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 5) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 4)) ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") [times](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___times "Definition of example") ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 3) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 2))) exampleEnv`
```
9
```

Like `Many`, `Reader` is an example of an effect that is difficult to encode in most languages, but type classes and monads make it just as convenient as any other effect. The dynamic or special variables found in Common Lisp, Clojure, and Emacs Lisp can be used like `Reader`. Similarly, Scheme and Racket's parameter objects are an effect that exactly correspond to `Reader`. The Kotlin idiom of context objects can solve a similar problem, but they are fundamentally a means of passing function arguments automatically, so this idiom is more like the encoding as a reader monad than it is an effect in the language.
###  4.3.3.4. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-exercises "Permalink")
####  4.3.3.4.1. Checking Contracts
Check the monad contract for `State σ` and `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε`.
####  4.3.3.4.2. Readers with Failure
Adapt the reader monad example so that it can also indicate failure when the custom operator is not defined, rather than just returning zero. In other words, given these definitions:
`def ReaderOption (ρ : Type) (α : Type) : Type := ρ → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α  def ReaderExcept (ε : Type) (ρ : Type) (α : Type) : Type := ρ → [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α`
do the following:
  1. Write suitable `pure` and `bind` functions
  2. Check that these functions satisfy the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` contract
  3. Write `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instances for `ReaderOption` and `ReaderExcept`
  4. Define suitable `applyPrim` operators and test them with `evaluateM` on some example expressions


####  4.3.3.4.3. A Tracing Evaluator[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-arithmetic-example-exercise-trace "Permalink")
The `WithLog` type can be used with the evaluator to add optional tracing of some operations. In particular, the type `ToTrace` can serve as a signal to trace a given operator:
`inductive ToTrace (α : Type) : Type where   | trace : α → ToTrace α`
For the tracing evaluator, expressions should have type `[Expr](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr "Definition of example") (Prim (ToTrace (Prim [Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference"))))`. This says that the operators in the expression consist of addition, subtraction, and multiplication, augmented with traced versions of each. The innermost argument is `[Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` to signal that there are no further special operators inside of `trace`, only the three basic ones.
Do the following:
  1. Implement a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (WithLog logged)` instance
  2. Write an `applyTraced` function to apply traced operators to their arguments, logging both the operator and the arguments, with type `ToTrace (Prim [Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")) → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → WithLog (Prim [Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference") × [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") × [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")`


If the exercise has been completed correctly, then
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Expr Prim ToTrace [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `{ log := [(Prim.plus, 1, 2), (Prim.minus, 3, 4), (Prim.times, 3, -1)], val := -3 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") evaluateM applyTraced ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") ([other](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___other "Definition of example") (trace [times](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___times "Definition of example"))) ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") ([other](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___other "Definition of example") (trace [plus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___plus "Definition of example"))) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 1) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 2)) ([prim](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___prim "Definition of example") ([other](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___other "Definition of example") (trace [minus](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Prim___minus "Definition of example"))) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 3) ([const](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#Expr___const "Definition of example") 4)))`
should result in

```
{ log := [(Prim.plus, 1, 2), (Prim.minus, 3, 4), (Prim.times, 3, -1)], val := -3 }
```

Hint: values of type `Prim [Empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Empty-Type/#Empty "Documentation for Empty in Lean Language Reference")` will appear in the resulting log. In order to display them as a result of `#eval`, the following instances are required:
`[deriving](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Deriving-Instances/#Lean___Parser___Command___deriving-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")
