title: One API, Many Applications
markdown:
[←4. Monads](Monads/#monads "4. Monads")[4.2. The Monad Type Class→](Monads/The-Monad-Type-Class/#monad-type-class "4.2. The Monad Type Class")
#  4.1. One API, Many Applications[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-api-examples "Permalink")
All these features and more can be implemented in library code as instances of a common API called `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")`. Lean provides dedicated syntax that makes this API convenient to use, but can also get in the way of understanding what is going on behind the scenes. This chapter begins with the nitty-gritty presentation of manually nesting null checks, and builds from there to the convenient, general API. Please suspend your disbelief in the meantime.
##  4.1.1. Checking for `none`: Don't Repeat Yourself[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-option-monad "Permalink")
In Lean, pattern matching can be used to chain checks for null. Getting the first entry from a list can just use the optional indexing notation:
`def first (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α :=   xs[0]?`
The result must be an `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` because empty lists have no first entry. Extracting the first and third entries requires a check that each is not `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def firstThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[0]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") first =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[2]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") third =>       [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third)`
Similarly, extracting the first, third, and fifth entries requires more checks that the values are not `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def firstThirdFifth (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α × α) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[0]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") first =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[2]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") third =>       [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[4]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")       | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") fifth =>         [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth)`
And adding the seventh entry to this sequence begins to become quite unmanageable:
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α × α × α) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[0]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") first =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[2]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") third =>       [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[4]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")       | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") fifth =>         [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[6]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")         | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")         | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") seventh =>           [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
The fundamental problem with this code is that it addresses two concerns: extracting the numbers and checking that all of them are present. The second concern is addressed by copying and pasting the code that handles the `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` case. It is often good style to lift a repetitive segment into a helper function:
`def andThen (opt : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α) (next : α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") β) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") β :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") opt [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => next x`
This helper, which is used similarly to `?.` in C# and Kotlin, takes care of propagating `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` values. It takes two arguments: an optional value and a function to apply when the value is not `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`. If the first argument is `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, then the helper returns `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`. If the first argument is not `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, then the function is applied to the contents of the `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` constructor.
Now, `firstThird` can be rewritten to use `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")` instead of pattern matching:
`def firstThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   [andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example") xs[0]? fun first =>   [andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example") xs[2]? fun third =>   [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third)`
In Lean, functions don't need to be enclosed in parentheses when passed as arguments. The following equivalent definition uses more parentheses and indents the bodies of functions:
`def firstThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   [andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example") xs[0]? (fun first =>     [andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example") xs[2]? (fun third =>       [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third)))`
The `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")` helper provides a sort of “pipeline” through which values flow, and the version with the somewhat unusual indentation is more suggestive of this fact. Improving the syntax used to write `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")` can make these computations even easier to understand.
###  4.1.1.1. Infix Operators[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=defining-infix-operators "Permalink")
In Lean, infix operators can be declared using the `infix`, `infixl`, and `infixr` commands, which create (respectively) non-associative, left-associative, and right-associative operators. When used multiple times in a row, a _left associative_ operator stacks up the opening parentheses on the left side of the expression. The addition operator `+` is left associative, so `w + x + y + z` is equivalent to `(((w + x) + y) + z)`. The exponentiation operator `^` is right associative, so `w ^ x ^ y ^ z` is equivalent to `w ^ (x ^ (y ^ z))`. Comparison operators such as `<` are non-associative, so `x < y < z` is a syntax error and requires manual parentheses.
The following declaration makes `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")` into an infix operator:
`infixl:55 " ~~> " => [andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")`
The number following the colon declares the _precedence_ of the new infix operator. In ordinary mathematical notation, `x + y * z` is equivalent to `x + (y * z)` even though both `+` and `*` are left associative. In Lean, `+` has precedence 65 and `*` has precedence 70. Higher-precedence operators are applied before lower-precedence operators. According to the declaration of `~~>`, both `+` and `*` have higher precedence, and thus apply first. Typically, figuring out the most convenient precedences for a group of operators requires some experimentation and a large collection of examples.
Following the new infix operator is a double arrow `=>`, which specifies the named function to be used for the infix operator. Lean's standard library uses this feature to define `+` and `*` as infix operators that point at `[HAdd.hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")` and `[HMul.hMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference")`, respectively, allowing type classes to be used to overload the infix operators. Here, however, `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")` is just an ordinary function.
Having defined an infix operator for `[andThen](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Option___M___andThen "Definition of example")`, `firstThird` can be rewritten in a way that brings the “pipeline” feeling of `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`-checks front and center:
`def firstThirdInfix (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   xs[0]? ~~> fun first =>   xs[2]? ~~> fun third =>   [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third)`
This style is much more concise when writing larger functions:
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") (α × α × α × α) :=   xs[0]? ~~> fun first =>   xs[2]? ~~> fun third =>   xs[4]? ~~> fun fifth =>   xs[6]? ~~> fun seventh =>   [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
##  4.1.2. Propagating Error Messages[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-except-monad "Permalink")
Pure functional languages such as Lean have no built-in exception mechanism for error handling, because throwing or catching an exception is outside of the step-by-step evaluation model for expressions. However, functional programs certainly need to handle errors. In the case of `firstThirdFifthSeventh`, it is likely relevant for a user to know just how long the list was and where the lookup failed.
This is typically accomplished by defining a datatype that can be either an error or a result, and translating functions with exceptions into functions that return this datatype:
`inductive Except (ε : Type) (α : Type) where   | error : ε → Except ε α   | ok : α → Except ε α deriving [BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference"), [Hashable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Hashable___mk "Documentation for Hashable in Lean Language Reference"), [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")`
The type variable `ε` stands for the type of errors that can be produced by the function. Callers are expected to handle both errors and successes, which makes the type variable `ε` play a role that is a bit like that of a list of checked exceptions in Java.
Similarly to `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, `Except` can be used to indicate a failure to find an entry in a list. In this case, the error type is a `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def get (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[i]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") s!"Index {i} not found (maximum is {xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") - 1})"   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x`
Looking up an in-bounds value yields an `[Except.ok](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#FakeExcept___Except___ok "Definition of example")`:
`def ediblePlants : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   ["ramsons", "sea plantain", "sea buckthorn", "garden nasturtium"]```Except.ok "sea buckthorn"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") [ediblePlants](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___ediblePlants "Definition of example") 2`
```
Except.ok "sea buckthorn"
```

Looking up an out-of-bounds value yields an `[Except.error](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#FakeExcept___Except___error "Definition of example")`:
``Except.error "Index 4 not found (maximum is 3)"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") [ediblePlants](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___ediblePlants "Definition of example") 4`
```
Except.error "Index 4 not found (maximum is 3)"
```

A single list lookup can conveniently return a value or an error:
`def first (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 0`
However, performing two list lookups requires handling potential failures:
`def firstThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 0 [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") msg => [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") msg   | [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") first =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 2 Once again, a common pattern can be factored out into a helper. Each step through the function checks for an error, and only proceeds with the rest of the computation if the result was a success. A new version of `andThen` can be defined for `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def andThen (attempt : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") e α) (next : α → [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") e β) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") e β :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") attempt [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") msg => [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") msg   | [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x => next x`
Just as with `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, this version of `andThen` allows a more concise definition of `firstThird'`:
`def firstThird' (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   andThen ([get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 0) fun first  =>   andThen ([get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 2) fun third =>   [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") (first, third)`
In both the `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` case, there are two repeating patterns: there is the checking of intermediate results at each step, which has been factored out into `andThen`, and there is the final successful result, which is `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` or `[Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference")`, respectively. For the sake of convenience, success can be factored out into a helper called `ok`:
`def ok (x : α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α := [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x`
Similarly, failure can be factored out into a helper called `fail`:
`def fail (err : ε) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε α := [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") err`
Using `ok` and `fail` makes `get` a little more readable:
`def get (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[i]? [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => fail s!"Index {i} not found (maximum is {xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") - 1})"   | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => ok x`
After adding the infix declaration for `andThen`, `firstThird` can be just as concise as the version that returns an `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`:
`infixl:55 " ~~> " => andThen``def firstThird (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") (α × α) :=   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 0 ~~> fun first =>   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 2 ~~> fun third =>   ok (first, third)`
The technique scales similarly to larger functions:
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") (α × α × α × α) :=   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 0 ~~> fun first =>   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 2 ~~> fun third =>   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 4 ~~> fun fifth =>   [get](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Err___get "Definition of example") xs 6 ~~> fun seventh =>   ok (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
##  4.1.3. Logging[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=logging "Permalink")
A number is even if dividing it by 2 leaves no remainder:
`def isEven (i : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   i % 2 == 0`
The function `sumAndFindEvens` computes the sum of a list while remembering the even numbers encountered along the way:
`def sumAndFindEvens : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") × [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => ([], 0)   | i :: is =>     let (moreEven, sum) := sumAndFindEvens is     ([if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [isEven](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___isEven "Definition of example") i [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") i :: moreEven [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") moreEven, sum + i)`
This function is a simplified example of a common pattern. Many programs need to traverse a data structure once, while both computing a main result and accumulating some kind of tertiary extra result. One example of this is logging: a program that is an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action can always log to a file on disk, but because the disk is outside of the mathematical world of Lean functions, it becomes much more difficult to prove things about logs based on `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`. Another example is a function that computes the sum of all the nodes in a tree with an inorder traversal, while simultaneously recording each nodes visited:
`def inorderSum : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") × [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => ([], 0)   | [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") l x r =>     let (leftVisited, leftSum) := inorderSum l     let (hereVisited, hereSum) := ([x], x)     let (rightVisited, rightSum) := inorderSum r     (leftVisited ++ hereVisited ++ rightVisited,      leftSum + hereSum + rightSum)`
Both `sumAndFindEvens` and `inorderSum` have a common repetitive structure. Each step of computation returns a pair that consists of a list of data that have been saved along with the primary result. The lists are then appended, and the primary result is computed and paired with the appended lists. The common structure becomes more apparent with a small rewrite of `sumAndFindEvens` that more cleanly separates the concerns of saving even numbers and computing the sum:
`def sumAndFindEvens : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") × [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => ([], 0)   | i :: is =>     let (moreEven, sum) := sumAndFindEvens is     let (evenHere, ()) := ([if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [isEven](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___isEven "Definition of example") i [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [i] [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [], ())     (evenHere ++ moreEven, sum + i)`
For the sake of clarity, a pair that consists of an accumulated result together with a value can be given its own name:
`structure WithLog (logged : Type) (α : Type) where   log : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") logged   val : α`
Similarly, the process of saving a list of accumulated results while passing a value on to the next step of a computation can be factored out into a helper, once again named `andThen`:
`def andThen (result : WithLog α β) (next : β → WithLog α γ) : WithLog α γ :=   let {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := thisOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := thisRes} := result   let {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := nextOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := nextRes} := next thisRes   {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := thisOut ++ nextOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := nextRes}`
In the case of errors, `ok` represents an operation that always succeeds. Here, however, it is an operation that simply returns a value without logging anything:
`def ok (x : β) : WithLog α β := {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := [], [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := x}`
Just as `Except` provides `fail` as a possibility, `WithLog` should allow items to be added to a log. This has no interesting return value associated with it, so it returns `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def save (data : α) : WithLog α [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := [data], [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := ()}`
`WithLog`, `andThen`, `ok`, and `save` can be used to separate the logging concern from the summing concern in both programs:

##  4.1.4. Numbering Tree Nodes[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=numbering-tree-nodes "Permalink")
An _inorder numbering_ of a tree associates each data point in the tree with the step it would be visited at in an inorder traversal of the tree. For example, consider `aTree`:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") BinTree [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") def aTree :=   [branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example")     ([branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example")        ([branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") "a" ([branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") "b" [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")))        "c"        [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example"))     "d"     ([branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") "e" [leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example"))`
Its inorder numbering is:

```
BinTree.branch
  (BinTree.branch
    (BinTree.branch (BinTree.leaf) (0, "a") (BinTree.branch (BinTree.leaf) (1, "b") (BinTree.leaf)))
    (2, "c")
    (BinTree.leaf))
  (3, "d")
  (BinTree.branch (BinTree.leaf) (4, "e") (BinTree.leaf))
```

Trees are most naturally processed with recursive functions, but the usual pattern of recursion on trees makes it difficult to compute an inorder numbering. This is because the highest number assigned anywhere in the left subtree is used to determine the numbering of a node's data value, and then again to determine the starting point for numbering the right subtree. In an imperative language, this issue can be worked around by using a mutable variable that contains the next number to be assigned. The following Python program computes an inorder numbering using a mutable variable:

```
class Branch:
    def __init__(self, value, left=None, right=None):
        self.left = left
        self.value = value
        self.right = right
    def __repr__(self):
        return f'Branch({self.value!r}, left={self.left!r}, right={self.right!r})'

def number(tree):
    num = 0
    def helper(t):
        nonlocal num
        if t is None:
            return None
        else:
            new_left = helper(t.left)
            new_value = (num, t.value)
            num += 1
            new_right = helper(t.right)
            return Branch(left=new_left, value=new_value, right=new_right)

    return helper(tree)

```

The numbering of the Python equivalent of `aTree` is:

```
a_tree = Branch("d",
                left=Branch("c",
                            left=Branch("a", left=None, right=Branch("b")),
                            right=None),
                right=Branch("e"))

```

and its numbering is:
``>>> `number(a_tree)`

```
Branch((3, 'd'), left=Branch((2, 'c'), left=Branch((0, 'a'), left=None, right=Branch((1, 'b'), left=None, right=None)), right=None), right=Branch((4, 'e'), left=None, right=None))

```

Even though Lean does not have mutable variables, a workaround exists. From the point of view of the rest of the world, the mutable variable can be thought of as having two relevant aspects: its value when the function is called, and its value when the function returns. In other words, a function that uses a mutable variable can be seen as a function that takes the mutable variable's starting value as an argument, returning a pair of the variable's final value and the function's result. This final value can then be passed as an argument to the next step.
Just as the Python example uses an outer function that establishes a mutable variable and an inner helper function that changes the variable, a Lean version of the function uses an outer function that provides the variable's starting value and explicitly returns the function's result along with an inner helper function that threads the variable's value while computing the numbered tree:
`def number (t : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   let rec helper (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α))     | [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => (n, [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example"))     | [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") left x right =>       let (k, numberedLeft) := [helper](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___State___number___helper "Definition of example") n left       let (i, numberedRight) := [helper](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___State___number___helper "Definition of example") (k + 1) right       (i, [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") numberedLeft (k, x) numberedRight)   ([helper](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___State___number___helper "Definition of example") 0 t).[snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference")`
This code, like the `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`-propagating `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` code, the `[error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference")`-propagating `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` code, and the log-accumulating `WithLog` code, commingles two concerns: propagating the value of the counter, and actually traversing the tree to find the result. Just as in those cases, an `andThen` helper can be defined to propagate state from one step of a computation to another. The first step is to give a name to the pattern of taking an input state as an argument and returning an output state together with a value:
`def State (σ : Type) (α : Type) : Type :=   σ → (σ × α)`
In the case of `State`, `ok` is a function that returns the input state unchanged, along with the provided value:
`def ok (x : α) : State σ α :=   fun s => (s, x)`
When working with a mutable variable, there are two fundamental operations: reading the value and replacing it with a new one. Reading the current value is accomplished with a function that places the input state unmodified into the output state, and also places it into the value field:
`def get : State σ σ :=   fun s => (s, s)`
Writing a new value consists of ignoring the input state, and placing the provided new value into the output state:
`def set (s : σ) : State σ [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") :=   fun _ => (s, ())`
Finally, two computations that use state can be sequenced by finding both the output state and return value of the first function, then passing them both into the next function:
`def andThen (first : State σ α) (next : α → State σ β) : State σ β :=   fun s =>     let (s', x) := first s     next x s'  infixl:55 " ~~> " => andThen`
Using `State` and its helpers, local mutable state can be simulated:
`def number (t : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   let rec helper : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → State [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ([BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α))     | [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => ok [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")     | [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") left x right =>       helper left ~~> fun numberedLeft =>       get ~~> fun n =>       set (n + 1) ~~> fun () =>       helper right ~~> fun numberedRight =>       ok ([BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") numberedLeft (n, x) numberedRight)   (helper t 0).snd`
Because `State` simulates only a single local variable, `get` and `set` don't need to refer to any particular variable name.
##  4.1.5. Monads: A Functional Design Pattern[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-as-design-pattern "Permalink")
Each of these examples has consisted of:
  * A polymorphic type, such as `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε`, `WithLog α`, or `State σ`
  * An operator `andThen` that takes care of some repetitive aspect of sequencing programs that have this type
  * An operator `ok` that is (in some sense) the most boring way to use the type
  * A collection of other operations, such as `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, `fail`, `save`, and `get`, that name ways of using the type


This style of API is called a _monad_. While the idea of monads is derived from a branch of mathematics called category theory, no understanding of category theory is needed in order to use them for programming. The key idea of monads is that each monad encodes a particular kind of side effect using the tools provided by the pure functional language Lean. For example, `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` represents programs that can fail by returning `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` represents programs that can throw exceptions, `WithLog` represents programs that accumulate a log while running, and `State` represents programs with a single mutable variable.

[←4.6. Additional Conveniences](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#monads-conveniences "4.6. Additional Conveniences")[5. Functors, Applicative Functors, and Monads→](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads "5. Functors, Applicative Functors, and Monads")
#  4.7. Summary[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monads-summary "Permalink")
##  4.7.1. Encoding Side Effects
Lean is a pure functional language. This means that it does not include side effects such as mutable variables, logging, or exceptions. However, most side effects can be _encoded_ using a combination of functions and inductive types or structures. For example, mutable state can be encoded as a function from an initial state to a pair of a final state and a result, and exceptions can be encoded as an inductive type with constructors for successful termination and errors.
Each set of encoded effects is a type. As a result, if a program uses these encoded effects, then this is apparent in its type. Functional programming does not mean that programs can't use effects, it simply requires that they be _honest_ about which effects they use. A Lean type signature describes not only the types of arguments that a function expects and the type of result that it returns, but also which effects it may use.
##  4.7.2. The Monad Type Class
It's possible to write purely functional programs in languages that allow effects anywhere. For example, `2 + 3` is a valid Python program that has no effects at all. Similarly, combining programs that have effects requires a way to state the order in which the effects must occur. It matters whether an exception is thrown before or after modifying a variable, after all.
The type class `Monad` captures these two important properties. It has two methods: `pure` represents programs that have no effects, and `bind` sequences effectful programs. The contract for `Monad` instances ensures that `bind` and `pure` actually capture pure computation and sequencing.
##  4.7.3. `do`-Notation for Monads
Rather than being limited to `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`, `do`-notation works for any monad. It allows programs that use monads to be written in a style that is reminiscent of statement-oriented languages, with statements sequenced after one another. Additionally, `do`-notation enables a number of additional convenient shorthands, such as nested actions. A program written with `do` is translated to applications of `>>=` behind the scenes.
##  4.7.4. Custom Monads
Different languages provide different sets of side effects. While most languages feature mutable variables and file I/O, not all have features like exceptions. Other languages offer effects that are rare or unique, like Icon's search-based program execution, Scheme and Ruby's continuations, and Common Lisp's resumable exceptions. An advantage to encoding effects with monads is that programs are not limited to the set of effects that are provided by the language. Because Lean is designed to make programming with any monad convenient, programmers are free to choose exactly the set of side effects that make sense for any given application.
##  4.7.5. The `IO` Monad
Programs that can affect the real world are written as `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions in Lean. `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` is one monad among many. The `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` monad encodes state and exceptions, with the state being used to keep track of the state of the world and the exceptions modeling failure and recovery.

Functors, Applicative Functors, and Monads→](Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Functors___-Applicative-Functors___-and-Monads "5. Functors, Applicative Functors, and Monads")

title: The IO Monad
markdown:
[←4.4. do-Notation for Monads](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#monad-do-notation "4.4. do-Notation for Monads")[4.6. Additional Conveniences→](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#monads-conveniences "4.6. Additional Conveniences")
#  4.5. The IO Monad[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=io-monad "Permalink")
`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` as a monad can be understood from two perspectives, which were described in the section on [running programs](Hello___-World___/Running-a-Program/#running-a-program). Each can help to understand the meanings of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` and `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`.
From the first perspective, an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action is an instruction to Lean's run-time system. For example, the instruction might be “read a string from this file descriptor, then re-invoke the pure Lean code with the string”. This perspective is an _exterior_ one, viewing the program from the perspective of the operating system. In this case, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that does not request any effects from the RTS, and `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` instructs the RTS to first carry out one potentially-effectful operation and then invoke the rest of the program with the resulting value.
From the second perspective, an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action transforms the whole world. `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions are actually pure, because they receive a unique world as an argument and then return the changed world. This perspective is an _interior_ one that matches how `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` is represented inside of Lean. The world is represented in Lean as a token, and the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` monad is structured to make sure that each token is used exactly once.
To see how this works, it can be helpful to peel back one definition at a time. The `#print` command reveals the internals of Lean datatypes and definitions. For example,
``inductive Nat : Type number of parameters: 0 constructors: Nat.zero : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Nat.succ : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`#print [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
results in

```
inductive Nat : Type
number of parameters: 0
constructors:
Nat.zero : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
Nat.succ : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```

and
``def Char.isAlpha : [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") := fun c => c.[isUpper](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isUpper "Documentation for Char.isUpper in Lean Language Reference") [||](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___or "Documentation for Bool.or in Lean Language Reference") c.[isLower](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isLower "Documentation for Char.isLower in Lean Language Reference")`#print [Char.isAlpha](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isAlpha "Documentation for Char.isAlpha in Lean Language Reference")`
results in

```
def Char.isAlpha : [Char](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___mk "Documentation for Char in Lean Language Reference") → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=
fun c => c.[isUpper](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isUpper "Documentation for Char.isUpper in Lean Language Reference") [||](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___or "Documentation for Bool.or in Lean Language Reference") c.[isLower](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Characters/#Char___isLower "Documentation for Char.isLower in Lean Language Reference")
```

Sometimes, the output of `#print` includes Lean features that have not yet been presented in this book. For example,
``def List.isEmpty.{u} : {α : Type u} → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") := fun {α} x =>   match x with   | [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")   | head [::](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") tail => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`#print [List.isEmpty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___isEmpty "Documentation for List.isEmpty in Lean Language Reference")`
produces

```
def List.isEmpty.{u} : {α : Type u} → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=
fun {α} x =>
  match x with
  | [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference") => [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")
  | head [::](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") tail => [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")
```

which includes a `.{u}` after the definition's name, and annotates types as `Type u` rather than just `Type`. This can be safely ignored for now.
Printing the definition of `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` shows that it's defined in terms of simpler structures:
