`[EIO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#EIO "Documentation for EIO in Lean Language Reference") ε α` represents `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions that will either terminate with an error of type `ε` or succeed with a value of type `α`. This means that, like the `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε` monad, the `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` monad includes the ability to define error handling and exceptions.
Peeling back another layer, `[EIO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#EIO "Documentation for EIO in Lean Language Reference")` is itself defined in terms of a simpler structure:
``def EIO : Type → Type → Type := fun ε α => [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε IO.RealWorld α`#print [EIO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#EIO "Documentation for EIO in Lean Language Reference")`
```
def EIO : Type → Type → Type :=
fun ε α => [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε IO.RealWorld α
```

The `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference")` monad includes both errors and state—it's similar to a combination of `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` and `State`. It is defined using another type, `EST.Out`:
``def EST : Type → Type → Type → Type := fun ε σ α => Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α`#print [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference")`
```
def EST : Type → Type → Type → Type :=
fun ε σ α => Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α
```

In other words, a program with type `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ α` is a function that accepts an initial state of type `σ` and returns an `EST.Out ε σ α`. The state is wrapped in the type `Void`, which is an internal primitive that causes a value to be erased from compiled code; `Void σ` has the same representation as `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`.
`EST.Out` is very much like the definition of `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`, with one constructor that indicates a successful termination and one constructor that indicates an error:
``inductive EST.Out : Type → Type → Type → Type number of parameters: 3 constructors: EST.Out.ok : {ε σ α : Type} → α → Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α EST.Out.error : {ε σ α : Type} → ε → Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α`#print EST.Out`
```
inductive EST.Out : Type → Type → Type → Type
number of parameters: 3
constructors:
EST.Out.ok : {ε σ α : Type} → α → Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α
EST.Out.error : {ε σ α : Type} → ε → Void σ → EST.Out ε σ α
```

Just like `Except ε α`, the `ok` constructor includes a result of type `α`, and the `error` constructor includes an exception of type `ε`. Unlike `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`, both constructors have an additional state field that includes the final state of the computation.
The `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ` requires `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` and `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")`. Just as with `State`, the implementation of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` for `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference")` accepts an initial state and returns it unchanged, and just as with `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`, it returns its argument in the `ok` constructor:
``protected def EST.pure : {α ε σ : Type} → α → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ α := fun {α ε σ} a s => EST.Out.ok a s`#print EST.pure`
```
protected def EST.pure : {α ε σ : Type} → α → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ α :=
fun {α ε σ} a s => EST.Out.ok a s
```

`protected` means that the full name `EST.pure` is needed even if the `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference")` namespace has been opened.
Similarly, `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `[EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference")` takes an initial state as an argument. It passes this initial state to its first action. Like `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` for `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`, it then checks whether the result is an error. If so, the error is returned unchanged and the second argument to `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` remains unused. If the result was a success, then the second argument is applied to both the returned value and to the resulting state.
``protected def EST.bind : {ε σ α β : Type} → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ α → (α → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ β) → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ β := fun {ε σ α β} x f s =>   match x s with   | EST.Out.ok a s => f a s   | EST.Out.error e s => EST.Out.error e s`#print EST.bind`
```
protected def EST.bind : {ε σ α β : Type} → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ α → (α → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ β) → [EST](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Mutable-References/#EST "Documentation for EST in Lean Language Reference") ε σ β :=
fun {ε σ α β} x f s =>
  match x s with
  | EST.Out.ok a s => f a s
  | EST.Out.error e s => EST.Out.error e s
```

Putting all of this together, `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` is a monad that tracks state and errors at the same time. The collection of available errors is that given by the datatype `[IO.Error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO___Error___alreadyExists "Documentation for IO.Error in Lean Language Reference")`, which has constructors that describe many things that can go wrong in a program. The state is a type that represents the real world, called `IO.RealWorld`. Each basic `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action receives this real world and returns another one, paired either with an error or a result. In `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` returns the world unchanged, while `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` passes the modified world from one action into the next action.
Because the entire universe doesn't fit in a computer's memory, the world being passed around is just a representation. So long as world tokens are not re-used, the representation is safe. The type `IO.RealWorld` is a trivial primitive type that does not need any representation at all, because it is only used inside of `Void`. 
[←4.4. do-Notation for Monads](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#monad-do-notation "4.4. do-Notation for Monads")[4.6. Additional Conveniences→](Monads/Additional-Conveniences/#monads-conveniences "4.6. Additional Conveniences")

title: The Monad Type Class
markdown:
[←4.1. One API, Many Applications](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#monad-api-examples "4.1. One API, Many Applications")[4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads→](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#monads-arithmetic-example "4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads")
#  4.2. The Monad Type Class[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-type-class "Permalink")
Rather than having to import an operator like `ok` or `andThen` for each type that is a monad, the Lean standard library contains a type class that allow them to be overloaded, so that the same operators can be used for _any_ monad. Monads have two operations, which are the equivalent of `ok` and `andThen`:
`class Monad (m : Type → Type) where   pure : α → m α   bind : m α → (α → m β) → m β`
This definition is slightly simplified. The actual definition in the Lean library is somewhat more involved, and will be presented later.
The `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instances for `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε` can be created by adapting the definitions of their respective `andThen` operations:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") opt next :=     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") opt [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference") => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")     | [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x => next x  instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") ([Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") ε) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") attempt next :=     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") attempt [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") e => [Except.error](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.error in Lean Language Reference") e     | [Except.ok](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except.ok in Lean Language Reference") x => next x`
As an example, `firstThirdFifthSeventh` was defined separately for `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α` and `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") α` return types. Now, it can be defined polymorphically for _any_ monad. It does, however, require a lookup function as an argument, because different monads might fail to find a result in different ways. The infix version of `bind` is `>>=`, which plays the same role as `~~>` in the examples.
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (lookup : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → m α)     (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : m (α × α × α × α) :=   lookup xs 0 >>= fun first =>   lookup xs 2 >>= fun third =>   lookup xs 4 >>= fun fifth =>   lookup xs 6 >>= fun seventh =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
Given example lists of slow mammals and fast birds, this implementation of `firstThirdFifthSeventh` can be used with `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def slowMammals : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   ["Three-toed sloth", "Slow loris"]  def fastBirds : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := [   "Peregrine falcon",   "Saker falcon",   "Golden eagle",   "Gray-headed albatross",   "Spur-winged goose",   "Swift",   "Anna's hummingbird" ]```[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") firstThirdFifthSeventh (fun xs i => xs[i]?) [slowMammals](Monads/The-Monad-Type-Class/#slowMammals "Definition of example")`
```

```
``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") [(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")"Peregrine falcon"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") "Golden eagle"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") "Spur-winged goose"[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") "Anna's hummingbird"[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") firstThirdFifthSeventh (fun xs i => xs[i]?) [fastBirds](Monads/The-Monad-Type-Class/#fastBirds "Definition of example")`

After renaming `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")`'s lookup function `get` to something more specific, the very same implementation of `firstThirdFifthSeventh` can be used with `[Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference")` as well:
`def getOrExcept (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Except](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Except___error "Documentation for Except in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs[i]? The fact that `m` must have a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance means that the `>>=` and `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` operations are available.
##  4.2.1. General Monad Operations[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-class-polymorphism "Permalink")
Because many different types are monads, functions that are polymorphic over _any_ monad are very powerful. For example, the function `mapM` is a version of `[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")` that uses a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` to sequence and combine the results of applying a function:
`def mapM [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : α → m β) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β)   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") []   | x :: xs =>     f x >>= fun hd =>     mapM f xs >>= fun tl =>     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (hd :: tl)`
The return type of the function argument `f` determines which `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance will be used. In other words, `mapM` can be used for functions that produce logs, for functions that can fail, or for functions that use mutable state. Because `f`'s type determines the available effects, they can be tightly controlled by API designers.
As described in [this chapter's introduction](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#numbering-tree-nodes), `State σ α` represents programs that make use of a mutable variable of type `σ` and return a value of type `α`. These programs are actually functions from a starting state to a pair of a value and a final state. The `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` class requires that its parameter expect a single type argument—that is, it should be a `Type → Type`. This means that the instance for `State` should mention the state type `σ`, which becomes a parameter to the instance:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (State σ) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := fun s => (s, x)   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") first next :=     fun s =>       let (s', x) := first s       next x s'`
This means that the type of the state cannot change between calls to `get` and `set` that are sequenced using `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")`, which is a reasonable rule for stateful computations. The operator `increment` increases a saved state by a given amount, returning the old value:
`def increment (howMuch : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : State [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   get >>= fun i =>   set (i + howMuch) >>= fun () =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i`
Using `mapM` with `increment` results in a program that computes the sum of the entries in a list. More specifically, the mutable variable contains the sum so far, while the resulting list contains a running sum. In other words, `mapM increment` has type `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → State [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference"))`, and expanding the definition of `State` yields `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") → ([Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") × [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference"))`. It takes an initial sum as an argument, which should be `0`:
``[(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")15[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")0[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 1[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 6[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 10[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") mapM increment [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] 0`
```
[(](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")15[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference") [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")0[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 1[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 6[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 10[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")[)](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.mk in Lean Language Reference")
```

A [logging effect](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#logging) can be represented using `WithLog`. Just like `State`, its `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance is polymorphic with respect to the type of the logged data:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") (WithLog logged) where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := [], [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := x}   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") result next :=     let {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := thisOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := thisRes} := result     let {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := nextOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := nextRes} := next thisRes     {[log](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___log "Definition of example") := thisOut ++ nextOut, [val](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___WithLog___val "Definition of example") := nextRes}`
`saveIfEven` is a function that logs even numbers but returns its argument unchanged:
`def saveIfEven (i : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")) : WithLog [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") :=   ([if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [isEven](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#Monads___Writer___isEven "Definition of example") i [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     save i    [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ()) >>= fun () =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") i`
Using this function with `mapM` results in a log containing even numbers paired with an unchanged input list:
``{ log := [2, 4], val := [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") mapM saveIfEven [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`
```
{ log := [2, 4], val := [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] }
```

##  4.2.2. The Identity Monad[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=Id-monad "Permalink")
Monads encode programs with effects, such as failure, exceptions, or logging, into explicit representations as data and functions. Sometimes, however, an API will be written to use a monad for flexibility, but the API's client may not require any encoded effects. The _identity monad_ is a monad that has no effects. It allows pure code to be used with monadic APIs:
`def Id (t : Type) : Type := t  instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") Id where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") x f := f x`
The type of `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` should be `α → [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference") α`, but `[Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference") α` reduces to just `α`. Similarly, the type of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` should be `α → (α → [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference") β) → [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference") β`. Because this reduces to `α → (α → β) → β`, the second argument can be applied to the first to find the result.
With the identity monad, `mapM` becomes equivalent to `[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Functor___mk "Documentation for Functor.map in Lean Language Reference")` To call it this way, however, Lean requires a hint that the intended monad is `[Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def numbers := mapM (m := [Id](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id "Documentation for Id in Lean Language Reference")) ([do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") return · + 1) [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`
Using `mapM` in a context in which the type doesn't provide any specific hints about which monad is to be used results in an “instance problem is stuck” message:
`def numbers := mapM ([do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `typeclass instance problem is stuck   [Pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure in Lean Language Reference") ?m.6  Note: Lean will not try to resolve this typeclass instance problem because the type argument to `[Pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure in Lean Language Reference")` is a metavariable. This argument must be fully determined before Lean will try to resolve the typeclass.  Hint: Adding type annotations and supplying implicit arguments to functions can give Lean more information for typeclass resolution. For example, if you have a variable `x` that you intend to be a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, but Lean reports it as having an unresolved type like `?m`, replacing `x` with `(x : Nat)` can get typeclass resolution un-stuck.`return · + 1) [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`
```

##  4.2.3. The Monad Contract[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-contract "Permalink")
Just as every pair of instances of `[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Hashable](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Hashable___mk "Documentation for Hashable in Lean Language Reference")` should ensure that any two equal values have the same hash, there is a contract that each instance of `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` should obey. First, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` should be a left identity of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")`. That is, `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") ([pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") v) f` should be the same as `f v`. Secondly, `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` should be a right identity of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")`, so `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") v [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` is the same as `v`. Finally, `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` should be associative, so `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") ([bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") v f) g` is the same as `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") v (fun x => [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") (f x) g)`.
This contract specifies the expected properties of programs with effects more generally. Because `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference")` has no effects, sequencing its effects with `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` shouldn't change the result. The associative property of `[bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference")` basically says that the sequencing bookkeeping itself doesn't matter, so long as the order in which things are happening is preserved.
##  4.2.4. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-class-exercises "Permalink")
###  4.2.4.1. Mapping on a Tree
Define a function `BinTree.mapM`. By analogy to `mapM` for lists, this function should apply a monadic function to each data entry in a tree, as a preorder traversal. The type signature should be:
`def BinTree.mapM [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : α → m β) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → m ([BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") β)`
###  4.2.4.2. The Option Monad Contract
First, write a convincing argument that the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` satisfies the monad contract. Then, consider the following instance:
`instance : [Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") where   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") x   [bind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Bind___mk "Documentation for Bind.bind in Lean Language Reference") opt next := [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`
Both methods have the correct type. Why does this instance violate the monad contract?
[←4.1. One API, Many Applications](Monads/One-API___-Many-Applications/#monad-api-examples "4.1. One API, Many Applications")[4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads→](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#monads-arithmetic-example "4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads")

title: do -Notation for Monads
markdown:
[←4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads](Monads/Example___-Arithmetic-in-Monads/#monads-arithmetic-example "4.3. Example: Arithmetic in Monads")[4.5. The IO Monad→](Monads/The-IO-Monad/#io-monad "4.5. The IO Monad")
#  4.4. `do`-Notation for Monads[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=monad-do-notation "Permalink")
While APIs based on monads are very powerful, the explicit use of `>>=` with anonymous functions is still somewhat noisy. Just as infix operators are used instead of explicit calls to `[HAdd.hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference")`, Lean provides a syntax for monads called _`do`-notation_ that can make programs that use monads easier to read and write. This is the very same `do`-notation that is used to write programs in `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")`, and `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` is also a monad.
In [Hello, World!](Hello___-World___/#hello-world), the `do` syntax is used to combine `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions, but the meaning of these programs is explained directly. Understanding how to program with monads means that `do` can now be explained in terms of how it translates into uses of the underlying monad operators.
The first translation of `do` is used when the only statement in the `do` is a single expression `E`. In this case, the `do` is removed, so
`[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") E`
translates to
`E`
The second translation is used when the first statement of the `do` is a `let` with an arrow, binding a local variable. This translates to a use of `>>=` together with a function that binds that very same variable, so
`[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let x ← E₁    Stmt    …    Eₙ`
translates to
`E₁ >>= fun x =>   [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Stmt      …      Eₙ`
When the first statement of the `do` block is an expression, then it is considered to be a monadic action that returns `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")`, so the function matches the `[Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference")` constructor and
`[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") E₁    Stmt    …    Eₙ`
translates to
`E₁ >>= fun () =>   [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Stmt      …      Eₙ`
Finally, when the first statement of the `do` block is a `let` that uses `:=`, the translated form is an ordinary let expression, so
`[do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") let x := E₁    Stmt    …    Eₙ`
translates to
`let x := E₁ [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Stmt    …    Eₙ`
The definition of `firstThirdFifthSeventh` that uses the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` class looks like this:
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (lookup : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → m α)     (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : m (α × α × α × α) :=   lookup xs 0 >>= fun first =>   lookup xs 2 >>= fun third =>   lookup xs 4 >>= fun fifth =>   lookup xs 6 >>= fun seventh =>   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
Using `do`-notation, it becomes significantly more readable:
`def firstThirdFifthSeventh [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (lookup : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → m α)     (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : m (α × α × α × α) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let first ← lookup xs 0   let third ← lookup xs 2   let fifth ← lookup xs 4   let seventh ← lookup xs 6   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (first, third, fifth, seventh)`
Without the `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` type class, the function `number` that numbers the nodes of a tree was written:
`def number (t : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   let rec helper : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → State [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ([BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α))     | [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example") => ok [BinTree.leaf](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___leaf "Definition of example")     | [BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") left x right =>       helper left ~~> fun numberedLeft =>       get ~~> fun n =>       set (n + 1) ~~> fun () =>       helper right ~~> fun numberedRight =>       ok ([BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") numberedLeft (n, x) numberedRight)   (helper t 0).snd`
With `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` and `do`, its definition is much less noisy:
`def number (t : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α) : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   let rec helper : [BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") α → State [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ([BinTree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree "Definition of example") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α))     | of example") left x right => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       let numberedLeft ← [helper](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#Numbering___number___helper "Definition of example") left       let n ← get       set (n + 1)       let numberedRight ← [helper](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#Numbering___number___helper "Definition of example") right       ok ([BinTree.branch](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#BinTree___branch "Definition of example") numberedLeft (n, x) numberedRight)   ([helper](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#Numbering___number___helper "Definition of example") t 0).[snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference")`
All of the conveniences from `do` with `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` are also available when using it with other monads. For example, nested actions also work in any monad. The original definition of `mapM` was:
`def mapM [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : α → m β) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β)   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") []   | x :: xs =>     f x >>= fun hd =>     mapM f xs >>= fun tl =>     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (hd :: tl)`
With `do`-notation, it can be written:
`def mapM [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : α → m β) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β)   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") []   | x :: xs => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let hd ← f x     let tl ← [mapM](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#WithDo___mapM "Definition of example") f xs     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") (hd :: tl)`
Using nested actions makes it almost as short as the original non-monadic `[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def mapM [[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") m] (f : α → m β) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → m ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β)   | [] => [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") []   | x :: xs => [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") ((← f x) :: (← [mapM](Monads/do--Notation-for-Monads/#mapM "Definition of example") f xs))`
Using nested actions, `number` can be made much more concise:
`def increment : State [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") := 1)   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") n  def number (t : title: Next Steps
markdown:
[←8.8. Summary](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Summary/#programs-proofs-summary "8.8. Summary")
#  9. Next Steps[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=next-steps "Permalink")
This book introduces the very basics of functional programming in Lean, including a tiny amount of interactive theorem proving. Using dependently-typed functional languages like Lean is a deep topic, and much can be said. Depending on your interests, the following resources might be useful for learning Lean 4.
##  9.1. Learning Lean[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=learning-lean "Permalink")
Lean 4 itself is described in the following resources:
  * [Theorem Proving in Lean 4](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/) is a tutorial on writing proofs using Lean.
  * [The Lean 4 Manual](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/latest/) provides a detailed description of the language and its features.
  * [How To Prove It With Lean](https://djvelleman.github.io/HTPIwL/) is a Lean-based accompaniment to the well-regarded textbook [_How To Prove It_](https://www.cambridge.org/highereducation/books/how-to-prove-it/6D2965D625C6836CD4A785A2C843B3DA) that provides an introduction to writing paper-and-pencil mathematical proofs.
  * [Metaprogramming in Lean 4](https://github.com/arthurpaulino/lean4-metaprogramming-book) provides an overview of Lean's extension mechanisms, from infix operators and notations to macros, custom tactics, and full-on custom embedded languages.
  * [Functional Programming in Lean](https://lean-lang.org/functional_programming_in_lean/) may be interesting to readers who enjoy jokes about recursion.


However, the best way to continue learning Lean is to start reading and writing code, consulting the documentation when you get stuck. Additionally, the [Lean Zulip](https://leanprover.zulipchat.com/) is an excellent place to meet other Lean users, ask for help, and help others.
##  9.2. Mathematics in Lean
A wide selection of learning resources for mathematicians are available at [the community site](https://leanprover-community.github.io/learn.html).
##  9.3. Using Dependent Types in Computer Science
Rocq is a language that has a lot in common with Lean. For computer scientists, the [Software Foundations](https://softwarefoundations.cis.upenn.edu/) series of interactive textbooks provides an excellent introduction to applications of Rocq in computer science. The fundamental ideas of Lean and Rocq are very similar, and skills are readily transferable between the systems.
##  9.4. Programming with Dependent Types
For programmers who are interested in learning to use indexed families and dependent types to structure programs, Edwin Brady's [_Type Driven Development with Idris_](https://www.manning.com/books/type-driven-development-with-idris) provides an excellent introduction. Like Rocq, Idris is a close cousin of Lean, though it lacks tactics.
##  9.5. Understanding Dependent Types
[_The Little Typer_](https://thelittletyper.com/) is a book for programmers who haven't formally studied logic or the theory of programming languages, but who want to build an understanding of the core ideas of dependent type theory. While all of the above resources aim to be as practical as possible, _The Little Typer_ presents an approach to dependent type theory where the very basics are built up from scratch, using only concepts from programming. Disclaimer: the author of _Functional Programming in Lean_ is also an author of _The Little Typer_. 
[←8.8. Summary](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Summary/#programs-proofs-summary "8.8. Summary")

title: Overloading and Type Classes
markdown:
[←Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#props-proofs-indexing "Interlude: Propositions, Proofs, and Indexing")[3.1. Positive Numbers→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#positive-numbers "3.1. Positive Numbers")
#  3. Overloading and Type Classes[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=type-classes "Permalink")
In many languages, the built-in datatypes get special treatment. For example, in C and Java, `+` can be used to add `float`s and `int`s, but not arbitrary-precision numbers from a third-party library. Similarly, numeric literals can be used directly for the built-in types, but not for user-defined number types. Other languages provide an _overloading_ mechanism for operators, where the same operator can be given a meaning for a new type. In these languages, such as C++ and C#, a wide variety of built-in operators can be overloaded, and the compiler uses the type checker to select a particular implementation.
In addition to numeric literals and operators, many languages allow overloading of functions or methods. In C++, Java, C# and Kotlin, multiple implementations of a method are allowed, with differing numbers and types of arguments. The compiler uses the number of arguments and their types to determine which overload was intended.
Function and operator overloading has a key limitation: polymorphic functions can't restrict their type arguments to types for which a given overload exists. For example, an overloaded method might be defined for strings, byte arrays, and file pointers, but there's no way to write a second method that works for any of these. Instead, this second method must itself be overloaded for each type that has an overload of the original method, resulting in many boilerplate definitions instead of a single polymorphic definition. Another consequence of this restriction is that some operators (such as equality in Java) end up being defined for _every_ combination of arguments, even when it is not necessarily sensible to do so. If programmers are not very careful, this can lead to programs that crash at runtime or silently compute an incorrect result.
Lean implements overloading using a mechanism called _type classes_ , pioneered in Haskell, that allows overloading of operators, functions, and literals in a manner that works well with polymorphism. A type class describes a collection of overloadable operations. To overload these operations for a new type, an _instance_ is created that contains an implementation of each operation for the new type. For example, a type class named `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` describes types that allow addition, and an instance of `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` provides an implementation of addition for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`.
The terms _class_ and _instance_ can be confusing for those who are used to object-oriented languages, because they are not closely related to classes and instances in object-oriented languages. However, they do share common roots: in everyday language, the term “class” refers to a group that shares some common attributes. While classes in object-oriented programming certainly describe groups of objects with common attributes, the term additionally refers to a specific mechanism in a programming language for describing such a group. Type classes are also a means of describing types that share common attributes (namely, implementations of certain operations), but they don't really have anything else in common with classes as found in object-oriented programming.
A Lean type class is much more analogous to a Java or C# _interface_. Both type classes and interfaces describe a conceptually related set of operations that are implemented for a type or collection of types. Similarly, an instance of a type class is akin to the code in a Java or C# class that is prescribed by the implemented interfaces, rather than an instance of a Java or C# class. Unlike Java or C#'s interfaces, types can be given instances for type classes that the author of the type does not have access to. In this way, they are very similar to Rust traits.
  1. [3.1. Positive Numbers](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#positive-numbers)
  2. [3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Type-Classes-and-Polymorphism/#tc-polymorphism)
  3. [3.3. Controlling Instance Search](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#out-params)
  4. [3.4. Arrays and Indexing](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Overloading-and-Type-Classes--Arrays-and-Indexing)
  5. [3.5. Standard Classes](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#standard-classes)
  6. [3.6. Coercions](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#coercions)
  7. [3.7. Additional Conveniences](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#type-class-conveniences)

title: Additional Conveniences
markdown:
[←3.6. Coercions](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#coercions "3.6. Coercions")[3.8. Summary→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Summary/#type-classes-summary "3.8. Summary")
#  3.7. Additional Conveniences[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=type-class-conveniences "Permalink")
##  3.7.1. Constructor Syntax for Instances[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=instance-constructor-syntax "Permalink")
Behind the scenes, type classes are structure types and instances are values of these types. The only differences are that Lean stores additional information about type classes, such as which parameters are output parameters, and that instances are registered for searching. While values that have structure types are typically defined using either `⟨...⟩` syntax or with braces and fields, and instances are typically defined using `where`, both syntaxes work for both kinds of definition.
For example, a forestry application might represent trees as follows:
`structure Tree : Type where   latinName : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")   commonNames : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")  def oak : [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") :=   ⟨"Quercus robur", ["common oak", "European oak"]⟩  def birch : [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") :=   { [latinName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___latinName "Definition of example") := "Betula pendula",     [commonNames](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___commonNames "Definition of example") := ["silver birch", "warty birch"]   }  def sloe : [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") where   [latinName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___latinName "Definition of example") := "Prunus spinosa"   [commonNames](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___commonNames "Definition of example") := ["sloe", "blackthorn"]`
All three syntaxes are equivalent.
Similarly, type class instances can be defined using all three syntaxes:
`class Display (α : Type) where   displayName : α → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")  instance : [Display](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Display "Definition of example") [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") :=   ⟨[Tree.latinName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___latinName "Definition of example")⟩  instance : [Display](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Display "Definition of example") [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") :=   { [displayName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Display___displayName "Definition of example") := [Tree.latinName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___latinName "Definition of example") }  instance : [Display](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Display "Definition of example") [Tree](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree "Definition of example") where   [displayName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Display___displayName "Definition of example") t := t.[latinName](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#Tree___latinName "Definition of example")`
The `where` syntax is typically used for instances, while structures use either the curly-brace syntax or the `where` syntax. The `⟨...⟩` syntax can be useful when emphasizing that a structure type is very much like a tuple in which the fields happen to be named, but the names are not important at the moment. However, there are situations where it can make sense to use other alternatives. In particular, a library might provide a function that constructs an instance value. Placing a call to this function after `:=` in an instance declaration is the easiest way to use such a function.
##  3.7.2. Examples[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=example-command "Permalink")
When experimenting with Lean code, definitions can be more convenient to use than `#eval` or `#check` commands. First off, definitions don't produce any output, which can help keep the reader's focus on the most interesting output. Secondly, it's easiest to write most Lean programs by starting with a type signature, allowing Lean to provide more assistance and better error messages while writing the program itself. On the other hand, `#eval` and `#check` are easiest to use in contexts where Lean is able to determine the type from the provided expression. Thirdly, `#eval` cannot be used with expressions whose types don't have `ToString` or `[Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference")` instances, such as functions. Finally, multi-step `do` blocks, `let`-expressions, and other syntactic forms that take multiple lines are particularly difficult to write with a type annotation in `#eval` or `#check`, simply because the required parenthesization can be difficult to predict.
To work around these issues, Lean supports the explicit indication of examples in a source file. An example is like a definition without a name. For instance, a non-empty list of birds commonly found in Copenhagen's green spaces can be written:
`example : NonEmptyList [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := "Sparrow",     [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := ["Duck", "Swan", "Magpie", "Eurasian coot", "Crow"]   }`
Examples may define functions by accepting arguments:
`example (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (k : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   n + k == k + n`
While this creates a function behind the scenes, this function has no name and cannot be called. Nonetheless, this is useful for demonstrating how a library can be used with arbitrary or unknown values of some given type. In source files, `example` declarations are best paired with comments that explain how the example illustrates the concepts of the library.
[←3.6. Coercions](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#coercions "3.6. Coercions")[3.8. Summary→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Summary/#type-classes-summary "3.8. Summary")
