title: Arrays and Indexing
markdown:
[←3.3. Controlling Instance Search](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#out-params "3.3. Controlling Instance Search")[3.5. Standard Classes→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#standard-classes "3.5. Standard Classes")
#  3.4. Arrays and Indexing
The [Interlude](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#props-proofs-indexing) describes how to use indexing notation in order to look up entries in a list by their position. This syntax is also governed by a type class, and it can be used for a variety of different types.
##  3.4.1. Arrays[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=array-indexing "Permalink")
For instance, Lean arrays are much more efficient than linked lists for most purposes. In Lean, the type `[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α` is a dynamically-sized array holding values of type `α`, much like a Java `ArrayList`, a C++ `std::vector`, or a Rust `Vec`. Unlike `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`, which has a pointer indirection on each use of the `[cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")` constructor, arrays occupy a contiguous region of memory, which is much better for processor caches. Also, looking up a value in an array takes constant time, while lookup in a linked list takes time proportional to the index being accessed.
In pure functional languages like Lean, it is not possible to mutate a given position in a data structure. Instead, a copy is made that has the desired modifications. However, copying is not always necessary: the Lean compiler and runtime contain an optimization that can allow modifications to be implemented as mutations behind the scenes when there is only a single unique reference to an array.
Arrays are written similarly to lists, but with a leading `#`:
`def northernTrees : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   #["sloe", "birch", "elm", "oak"]`
The number of values in an array can be found using `[Array.size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")`. For instance, `[northernTrees](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#northernTrees "Definition of example").[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")` evaluates to `4`. For indices that are smaller than an array's size, indexing notation can be used to find the corresponding value, just as with lists. That is, `[northernTrees](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#northernTrees "Definition of example")[2]` evaluates to `"elm"`. Similarly, the compiler requires a proof that an index is in bounds, and attempting to look up a value outside the bounds of the array results in a compile-time error, just as with lists. For instance, `[northernTrees](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#northernTrees "Definition of example")[8]` results in:

```
failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:
  - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid
  - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid
  - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type
  - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid
⊢ 8 [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") [northernTrees](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#northernTrees "Definition of example").[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")
```

##  3.4.2. Non-Empty Lists[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=non-empty-list-indexing "Permalink")
A datatype that represents non-empty lists can be defined as a structure with a field for the head of the list and a field for the tail, which is an ordinary, potentially empty list:
`structure NonEmptyList (α : Type) : Type where   head : α   tail : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`
For example, the non-empty list `idahoSpiders` (which contains some spider species native to the US state of Idaho) consists of `"Banded Garden Spider"` followed by four other spiders, for a total of five spiders:
`def idahoSpiders : NonEmptyList [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := {   [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := "Banded Garden Spider",   [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := [     "Long-legged Sac Spider",     "Wolf Spider",     "Hobo Spider",     "Cat-faced Spider"   ] }`
Looking up the value at a specific index in this list with a recursive function should consider three possibilities:
  1. The index is `0`, in which case the head of the list should be returned.
  2. The index is `n + 1` and the tail is empty, in which case the index is out of bounds.
  3. The index is `n + 1` and the tail is non-empty, in which case the function can be called recursively on the tail and `n`.


For example, a lookup function that returns an `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` can be written as follows:
`def NonEmptyList.get? : NonEmptyList α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | xs, 0 => [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") xs.[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example")   | {[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := _, [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := []}, _ + 1 => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | {[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := _, [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := h :: t}, n + 1 => [get?](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___get___ "Definition of example") {[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := h, [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := t} n`
Each case in the pattern match corresponds to one of the possibilities above. The recursive call to `[get?](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___get___ "Definition of example")` does not require a `NonEmptyList` namespace qualifier because the body of the definition is implicitly in the definition's namespace. Another way to write this function uses a list lookup `xs.[tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example")[n]?` when the index is greater than zero:
`def NonEmptyList.get? : NonEmptyList α → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | xs, 0 => [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") xs.[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example")   | xs, n + 1 => xs.[tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example")[n]?`
If the list contains one entry, then only `0` is a valid index. If it contains two entries, then both `0` and `1` are valid indices. If it contains three entries, then `0`, `1`, and `2` are valid indices. In other words, the valid indices into a non-empty list are natural numbers that are strictly less than the length of the list, which are less than or equal to the length of the tail.
The definition of what it means for an index to be in bounds should be written as an `abbrev` because the tactics used to find evidence that indices are acceptable are able to solve inequalities of numbers, but they don't know anything about the name `NonEmptyList.inBounds`:
`abbrev NonEmptyList.inBounds (xs : NonEmptyList α) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Prop :=   i ≤ xs.[tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")`
This function returns a proposition that might be true or false. For instance, `2` is in bounds for `idahoSpiders`, while `5` is not:
`theorem atLeastThreeSpiders : idahoSpiders.inBounds 2 := by⊢ idahoSpiders.inBounds 2 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙  theorem notSixSpiders : ¬idahoSpiders.inBounds 5 := by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")idahoSpiders.inBounds 5 [decide](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#decide "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
The logical negation operator has a very low precedence, which means that `¬idahoSpiders.inBounds 5` is equivalent to `¬(idahoSpiders.inBounds 5)`.
This fact can be used to write a lookup function that requires evidence that the index is valid, and thus need not return `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")`, by delegating to the version for lists that checks the evidence at compile time:
`def NonEmptyList.get (xs : NonEmptyList α)     (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (ok : xs.inBounds i) : α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") i [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | 0 => xs.[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example")   | n + 1 => xs.[tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example")[n]`
It is, of course, possible to write this function to use the evidence directly, rather than delegating to a standard library function that happens to be able to use the same evidence. This requires techniques for working with proofs and propositions that are described later in this book.
##  3.4.3. Overloading Indexing[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=overloading-indexing "Permalink")
Indexing notation for a collection type can be overloaded by defining an instance of the `GetElem` type class. For the sake of flexibility, `GetElem` has four parameters:
  * The type of the collection
  * The type of the index
  * The type of elements that are extracted from the collection
  * A function that determines what counts as evidence that the index is in bounds


The element type and the evidence function are both output parameters. `GetElem` has a single method, `getElem`, which takes a collection value, an index value, and evidence that the index is in bounds as arguments, and returns an element:
`class GetElem     (coll : Type)     (idx : Type)     (item : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") Type)     (inBounds : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (coll → idx → Prop)) where   getElem : (c : coll) → (i : idx) → inBounds c i → item`
In the case of `NonEmptyList α`, these parameters are:
  * The collection is `NonEmptyList α`
  * Indices have type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
  * The type of elements is `α`
  * An index is in bounds if it is less than or equal to the length of the tail


In fact, the `[GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference")` instance can delegate directly to `[NonEmptyList.get](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___get "Definition of example")`:
`instance : [GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference") (NonEmptyList α) [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") α NonEmptyList.inBounds where   [getElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem.getElem in Lean Language Reference") := [NonEmptyList.get](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___get "Definition of example")`
With this instance, `NonEmptyList` becomes just as convenient to use as `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`. Evaluating `idahoSpiders.[head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example")` yields `"Banded Garden Spider"`, while `idahoSpiders[9]` leads to the compile-time error:

```
failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:
  - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid
  - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid
  - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type
  - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid
⊢ idahoSpiders.inBounds 9
```

Because both the collection type and the index type are input parameters to the `[GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference")` type class, new types can be used to index into existing collections. The positive number type `Pos` is a perfectly reasonable index into a `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`, with the caveat that it cannot point at the first entry. The following instance of `[GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference")` allows `Pos` to be used just as conveniently as `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to find a list entry:
`instance : [GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference") ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) Pos α     (fun list n => list.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") > n.toNat) where   [getElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem.getElem in Lean Language Reference") (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (i : Pos) ok := xs[i.toNat]`
Indexing can also make sense for non-numeric indices. For example, `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` can be used to select between the fields in a point, with `[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")` corresponding to `x` and `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")` corresponding to `y`:
`instance : [GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference") (PPoint α) [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") α (fun _ _ => [True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")) where   [getElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem.getElem in Lean Language Reference") (p : PPoint α) (i : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) _ :=     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") i [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.x [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p.y`
In this case, both Booleans are valid indices. Because every possible `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` is in bounds, the evidence is simply the true proposition `[True](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#True___intro "Documentation for True in Lean Language Reference")`. 
[←3.3. Controlling Instance Search](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#out-params "3.3. Controlling Instance Search")[3.5. Standard Classes→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#standard-classes "3.5. Standard Classes")

title: Coercions
markdown:
[←3.5. Standard Classes](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#standard-classes "3.5. Standard Classes")[3.7. Additional Conveniences→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Additional-Conveniences/#type-class-conveniences "3.7. Additional Conveniences")
#  3.6. Coercions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=coercions "Permalink")
In mathematics, it is common to use the same symbol to stand for different aspects of some object in different contexts. For example, if a ring is referred to in a context where a set is expected, then it is understood that the ring's underlying set is what's intended. In programming languages, it is common to have rules to automatically translate values of one type into values of another type. Java allows a `byte` to be automatically promoted to an `int`, and Kotlin allows a non-nullable type to be used in a context that expects a nullable version of the type.
In Lean, both purposes are served by a mechanism called _coercions_. When Lean encounters an expression of one type in a context that expects a different type, it will attempt to coerce the expression before reporting a type error. Unlike Java, C, and Kotlin, the coercions are extensible by defining instances of type classes.
##  3.6.1. Strings and Paths[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=string-path-coercion "Permalink")
In the [source code to `feline`](Hello___-World___/Worked-Example___--cat/#handling-input), a `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` is converted to a `[FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")` using the anonymous constructor syntax. In fact, this was not necessary: Lean defines a coercion from `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` to `[FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")`, so a string can be used in an position where a path is expected. Even though the function `[IO.FS.readFile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___readFile "Documentation for IO.FS.readFile in Lean Language Reference")` has type `[System.FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference") → [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`, the following code is accepted by Lean:
`def fileDumper : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")   let stdout ← [IO.getStdout](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdout "Documentation for IO.getStdout in Lean Language Reference")   stdout.[putStr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStr in Lean Language Reference") "Which file? "   stdout.[flush](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.flush in Lean Language Reference")   let f := (← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")).[trim](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___trim "Documentation for String.trim in Lean Language Reference")   stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") s!"'The file {f}' contains:"   stdout.[putStrLn](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___putStrLn "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.putStrLn in Lean Language Reference") (← [IO.FS.readFile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___readFile "Documentation for IO.FS.readFile in Lean Language Reference") f)`
`[String.trim](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___trim "Documentation for String.trim in Lean Language Reference")` removes leading and trailing whitespace from a string. On the last line of `fileDumper`, the coercion from `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` to `[FilePath](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#System___FilePath___mk "Documentation for System.FilePath in Lean Language Reference")` automatically converts `f`, so it is not necessary to write `IO.FS.readFile ⟨f⟩`.
##  3.6.2. Positive Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=positive-number-coercion "Permalink")
Every positive number corresponds to a natural number. The function `Pos.toNat` that was defined earlier converts a `Pos` to the corresponding `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def Pos.toNat : Pos → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   | Pos.one => 1   | [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") n => n.toNat + 1`
The function `[List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference")`, with type `{α : Type} → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α`, removes a prefix of a list. Applying `[List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference")` to a `Pos`, however, leads to a type error:
`[1, 2, 3, 4].[drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") (2 : Pos)`
```
Application type mismatch: The argument
  2
has type
  Pos
but is expected to have type
  [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
in the application
  [List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") 2
```

Because the author of `[List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference")` did not make it a method of a type class, it can't be overridden by defining a new instance.
The type class `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference")` describes overloaded ways of coercing from one type to another:
`class Coe (α : Type) (β : Type) where   coe : α → β`
An instance of `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is enough to allow the prior code to work:
`instance : [Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe.coe in Lean Language Reference") x := x.toNat```[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 4[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [1, 2, 3, 4].[drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") (2 : Pos)`
```
[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 4[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")
```

Using `#check` shows the result of the instance search that was used behind the scenes:
``[List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") (Pos.toNat 2) [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")1[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 2[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 4[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [1, 2, 3, 4].[drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") (2 : Pos)`
```
[List.drop](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___drop "Documentation for List.drop in Lean Language Reference") (Pos.toNat 2) [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")1[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 2[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 4[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```

##  3.6.3. Chaining Coercions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=chaining-coercions "Permalink")
When searching for coercions, Lean will attempt to assemble a coercion out of a chain of smaller coercions. For example, there is already a coercion from `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")`. Because of that instance, combined with the `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` instance, the following code is accepted:
`def oneInt : [Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference") := Pos.one`
This definition uses two coercions: from `Pos` to `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, and then from `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")`.
The Lean compiler does not get stuck in the presence of circular coercions. For example, even if two types `A` and `B` can be coerced to one another, their mutual coercions can be used to find a path:
`inductive A where   | a  inductive B where   | b  instance : [Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") A B where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe.coe in Lean Language Reference") _ := [B.b](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#B___b "Definition of example")  instance : [Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") B A where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe.coe in Lean Language Reference") _ := [A.a](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#A___a "Definition of example")  instance : [Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") A where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe.coe in Lean Language Reference") _ := [A.a](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#A___a "Definition of example")  def coercedToB : B := ()`
Remember: the double parentheses `()` is short for the constructor `[Unit.unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit___unit "Documentation for Unit.unit in Lean Language Reference")`. After deriving a `[Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference") B` instance with `[deriving](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Deriving-Instances/#Lean___Parser___Command___deriving-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [instance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Deriving-Instances/#Lean___Parser___Command___deriving-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Repr](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Repr___mk "Documentation for Repr in Lean Language Reference") [for](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Deriving-Instances/#Lean___Parser___Command___deriving-next "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") B`,
``B.b`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [coercedToB](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#coercedToB "Definition of example")`
results in:

```
B.b
```

The `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` type can be used similarly to nullable types in C# and Kotlin: the `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` constructor represents the absence of a value. The Lean standard library defines a coercion from any type `α` to `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α` that wraps the value in `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")`. This allows option types to be used in a manner even more similar to nullable types, because `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` can be omitted. For instance, the function `List.last?` that finds the last entry in a list can be written without a `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` around the return value `x`:
`def List.last? : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") α   | [] => [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")   | [x] => x   | _ :: x :: xs => last? (x :: xs)`
Instance search finds the coercion, and inserts a call to `coe`, which wraps the argument in `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")`. These coercions can be chained, so that nested uses of `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference")` don't require nested `[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference")` constructors:
`def perhapsPerhapsPerhaps : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference"))) :=   "Please don't tell me"`
Coercions are only activated automatically when Lean encounters a mismatch between an inferred type and a type that is imposed from the rest of the program. In cases with other errors, coercions are not activated. For example, if the error is that an instance is missing, coercions will not be used:
`def [perhapsPerhapsPerhapsNat](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#perhapsPerhapsPerhapsNat "Definition of example") : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") This can be worked around by manually indicating the desired type to be used for `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")`:
`def perhapsPerhapsPerhapsNat : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))) :=   (392 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
Additionally, coercions can be manually inserted using an up arrow:
`def perhapsPerhapsPerhapsNat : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))) :=   ↑(392 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
In some cases, this can be used to ensure that Lean finds the right instances. It can also make the programmer's intentions more clear.
##  3.6.4. Non-Empty Lists and Dependent Coercions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=CoeDep "Permalink")
An instance of `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") α β` makes sense when the type `β` has a value that can represent each value from the type `α`. Coercing from `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")` makes sense, because the type `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")` contains all the natural numbers, but a coercion from `[Int](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Integers/#Int___ofNat "Documentation for Int in Lean Language Reference")` to `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is a poor idea because `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` does not contain the negative numbers. Similarly, a coercion from non-empty lists to ordinary lists makes sense because the `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` type can represent every non-empty list:
`instance : [Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") (NonEmptyList α) ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe.coe in Lean Language Reference")     | { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := x, [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := xs } => x :: xs`
This allows non-empty lists to be used with the entire `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` API.
On the other hand, it is impossible to write an instance of `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference") ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (NonEmptyList α)`, because there's no non-empty list that can represent the empty list. This limitation can be worked around by using another version of coercions, which are called _dependent coercions_. Dependent coercions can be used when the ability to coerce from one type to another depends on which particular value is being coerced. Just as the `OfNat` type class takes the particular `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` being overloaded as a parameter, dependent coercion takes the value being coerced as a parameter:
`class CoeDep (α : Type) (x : α) (β : Type) where   coe : β`
This is a chance to select only certain values, either by imposing further type class constraints on the value or by writing certain constructors directly. For example, any `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` that is not actually empty can be coerced to a `NonEmptyList`:
`instance : [CoeDep](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-Between-Types/#CoeDep___mk "Documentation for CoeDep in Lean Language Reference") ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (x :: xs) (NonEmptyList α) where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-Between-Types/#CoeDep___mk "Documentation for CoeDep.coe in Lean Language Reference") := { [head](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___head "Definition of example") := x, [tail](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#NonEmptyList___tail "Definition of example") := xs }`
##  3.6.5. Coercing to Types[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=CoeSort "Permalink")
In mathematics, it is common to have a concept that consists of a set equipped with additional structure. For example, a monoid is some set `SSS`, an element `sss` of `SSS`, and an associative binary operator on `SSS`, such that `sss` is neutral on the left and right of the operator. `SSS` is referred to as the “carrier set” of the monoid. The natural numbers with zero and addition form a monoid, because addition is associative and adding zero to any number is the identity. Similarly, the natural numbers with one and multiplication also form a monoid. Monoids are also widely used in functional programming: lists, the empty list, and the append operator form a monoid, as do strings, the empty string, and string append:
`structure Monoid where   Carrier : Type   neutral : Carrier   op : Carrier → Carrier → Carrier  def natMulMonoid : Monoid :=   { [Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example") := [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), [neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") := 1, [op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") := (· * ·) }  def natAddMonoid : Monoid :=   { [Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example") := [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"), [neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") := 0, [op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") := (· + ·) }  def stringMonoid : Monoid :=   { [Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example") := [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference"), [neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") := "", [op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") := [String.append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___append "Documentation for String.append in Lean Language Reference") }  def listMonoid (α : Type) : Monoid :=   { [Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example") := [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α, [neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") := [], [op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") := [List.append](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___append "Documentation for List.append in Lean Language Reference") }`
Given a monoid, it is possible to write the `foldMap` function that, in a single pass, transforms the entries in a list into a monoid's carrier set and then combines them using the monoid's operator. Because monoids have a neutral element, there is a natural result to return when the list is empty, and because the operator is associative, clients of the function don't have to care whether the recursive function combines elements from left to right or from right to left.
`def foldMap (M : Monoid) (f : α → M.[Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example")) (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : M.[Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example") :=   let rec go (soFar : M.[Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example")) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → M.[Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example")     | [] => soFar     | y :: ys => [go](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#MMM___foldMap___go "Definition of example") (M.[op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") soFar (f y)) ys   [go](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#MMM___foldMap___go "Definition of example") M.[neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") xs`
Even though a monoid consists of three separate pieces of information, it is common to just refer to the monoid's name in order to refer to its set. Instead of saying “Let A be a monoid and let _x_ and _y_ be elements of its carrier set”, it is common to say “Let _A_ be a monoid and let _x_ and _y_ be elements of _A_ ”. This practice can be encoded in Lean by defining a new kind of coercion, from the monoid to its carrier set.
The `[CoeSort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort in Lean Language Reference")` class is just like the `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference")` class, with the exception that the target of the coercion must be a _sort_ , namely `Type` or `Prop`. The term _sort_ in Lean refers to these types that classify other types—`Type` classifies types that themselves classify data, and `Prop` classifies propositions that themselves classify evidence of their truth. Just as `[Coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/#Coe___mk "Documentation for Coe in Lean Language Reference")` is checked when a type mismatch occurs, `[CoeSort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort in Lean Language Reference")` is used when something other than a sort is provided in a context where a sort would be expected.
The coercion from a monoid into its carrier set extracts the carrier:
`instance : [CoeSort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort in Lean Language Reference") Monoid Type where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort.coe in Lean Language Reference") m := m.[Carrier](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___Carrier "Definition of example")`
With this coercion, the type signatures become less bureaucratic:
`def foldMap (M : Monoid) (f : α → M) (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : M :=   let rec go (soFar : M) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α → M     | [] => soFar     | y :: ys => [go](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#foldMap___go "Definition of example") (M.[op](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___op "Definition of example") soFar (f y)) ys   [go](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#foldMap___go "Definition of example") M.[neutral](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Monoid___neutral "Definition of example") xs`
Another useful example of `[CoeSort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort in Lean Language Reference")` is used to bridge the gap between `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` and `Prop`. As discussed in [the section on ordering and equality](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#equality-and-ordering), Lean's `if` expression expects the condition to be a decidable proposition rather than a `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`. Programs typically need to be able to branch based on Boolean values, however. Rather than have two kinds of `if` expression, the Lean standard library defines a coercion from `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` to the proposition that the `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` in question is equal to `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`:
`instance : [CoeSort](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") Prop where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Sorts/#CoeSort___mk "Documentation for CoeSort.coe in Lean Language Reference") b := b = [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`
In this case, the sort in question is `Prop` rather than `Type`.
##  3.6.6. Coercing to Functions[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=CoeFun "Permalink")
Many datatypes that occur regularly in programming consist of a function along with some extra information about it. For example, a function might be accompanied by a name to show in logs or by some configuration data. Additionally, putting a type in a field of a structure, similarly to the `Monoid` example, can make sense in contexts where there is more than one way to implement an operation and more manual control is needed than type classes would allow. For example, the specific details of values emitted by a JSON serializer may be important because another application expects a particular format. Sometimes, the function itself may be derivable from just the configuration data.
A type class called `CoeFun` can transform values from non-function types to function types. `CoeFun` has two parameters: the first is the type whose values should be transformed into functions, and the second is an output parameter that determines exactly which function type is being targeted.
`class CoeFun (α : Type) (makeFunctionType : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") (α → Type)) where   coe : (x : α) → makeFunctionType x`
The second parameter is itself a function that computes a type. In Lean, types are first-class and can be passed to functions or returned from them, just like anything else.
For example, a function that adds a constant amount to its argument can be represented as a wrapper around the amount to add, rather than by defining an actual function:
`structure Adder where   howMuch : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
A function that adds five to its argument has a `5` in the `howMuch` field:
`def add5 : Adder := ⟨5⟩`
This `Adder` type is not a function, and applying it to an argument results in an error:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `Function expected at   [add5](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#add5 "Definition of example") but this term has type   Adder  Note: Expected a function because this term is being applied to the argument   3`[add5](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#add5 "Definition of example") 3`
```
Function expected at
  [add5](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#add5 "Definition of example")
but this term has type
  Adder

Note: Expected a function because this term is being applied to the argument
  3
```

Defining a `[CoeFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun in Lean Language Reference")` instance causes Lean to transform the adder into a function with type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`:
`instance : [CoeFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun in Lean Language Reference") Adder (fun _ => [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun.coe in Lean Language Reference") a := (· + a.howMuch)```8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [add5](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#add5 "Definition of example") 3`
```
8
```

Because all `Adder`s should be transformed into `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` functions, the argument to `[CoeFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun in Lean Language Reference")`'s second parameter was ignored.
When the value itself is needed to determine the right function type, then `[CoeFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun in Lean Language Reference")`'s second parameter is no longer ignored. For example, given the following representation of JSON values:
`inductive JSON where   | true : JSON   | false : JSON   | null : JSON   | string : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → JSON   | number : [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference") → JSON   | object : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ([String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") × JSON) → JSON   | array : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") JSON → JSON`
a JSON serializer is a structure that tracks the type it knows how to serialize along with the serialization code itself:
`structure Serializer where   Contents : Type   serialize : Contents → JSON`
A serializer for strings need only wrap the provided string in the `[JSON.string](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___string "Definition of example")` constructor:
`def Str : [Serializer](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer "Definition of example") :=   { [Contents](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer___Contents "Definition of example") := [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference"),     [serialize](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer___serialize "Definition of example") := [JSON.string](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___string "Definition of example")   }`
Viewing JSON serializers as functions that serialize their argument requires extracting the inner type of serializable data:
`instance : [CoeFun](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun in Lean Language Reference") [Serializer](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer "Definition of example") (fun s => s.[Contents](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer___Contents "Definition of example") → JSON) where   [coe](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Coercions/Coercing-to-Function-Types/#CoeFun___mk "Documentation for CoeFun.coe in Lean Language Reference") s := s.[serialize](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer___serialize "Definition of example")`
Given this instance, a serializer can be applied directly to an argument:
`def buildResponse (title : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (R : [Serializer](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer "Definition of example"))     (record : R.[Contents](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Serializer___Contents "Definition of example")) : JSON :=   [JSON.object](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___object "Definition of example") [     ("title", [JSON.string](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___string "Definition of example") title),     ("status", [JSON.number](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___number "Definition of example") 200),     ("record", R record)   ]`
The serializer can be passed directly to `[buildResponse](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___buildResponse "Definition of example")`:
``JSON.object   [("title", JSON.string "Functional Programming in Lean"),    ("status", JSON.number 200.000000),    ("record", JSON.string "Programming is fun!")]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [buildResponse](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___buildResponse "Definition of example") "Functional Programming in Lean" [Str](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Str "Definition of example") "Programming is fun!"`
```
JSON.object
  [("title", JSON.string "Functional Programming in Lean"),
   ("status", JSON.number 200.000000),
   ("record", JSON.string "Programming is fun!")]
```

###  3.6.6.1. Aside: JSON as a String[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=json-string "Permalink")
It can be a bit difficult to understand JSON when encoded as Lean objects. To help make sure that the serialized response was what was expected, it can be convenient to write a simple converter from `JSON` to `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")`. The first step is to simplify the display of numbers. `JSON` doesn't distinguish between integers and floating point numbers, and the type `[Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")` is used to represent both. In Lean, `[Float.toString](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___toString "Documentation for Float.toString in Lean Language Reference")` includes a number of trailing zeros:
``"5.000000"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (5 : [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")).[toString](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___toString "Documentation for Float.toString in Lean Language Reference")`
```
"5.000000"
```

The solution is to write a little function that cleans up the presentation by dropping all trailing zeros, followed by a trailing decimal point:
`def dropDecimals (numString : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") numString.[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___contains "Documentation for String.contains in Lean Language Reference") '.' [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let noTrailingZeros := numString.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") (· == '0')     noTrailingZeros.[dropRightWhile](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRightWhile "Documentation for String.dropRightWhile in Lean Language Reference") (· == '.')   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") numString`
With this definition, `[dropDecimals](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#dropDecimals "Definition of example") (5 : [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")).[toString](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___toString "Documentation for Float.toString in Lean Language Reference")` yields `5`, and `[dropDecimals](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#dropDecimals "Definition of example") (5.2 : [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")).[toString](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___toString "Documentation for Float.toString in Lean Language Reference")` yields `5.2`.
The next step is to define a helper function to append a list of strings with a separator in between them:
`def String.separate (sep : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (strings : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") strings [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => ""   | x :: xs => [String.join](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___join "Documentation for String.join in Lean Language Reference") (x :: xs.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") (sep ++ ·))`
This function is useful to account for comma-separated elements in JSON arrays and objects. `", ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") ["1", "2"]` yields `"1, 2"`, `", ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") ["1"]` yields `"1"`, and `", ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") []` yields `""`. In the Lean standard library, this function is called `[String.intercalate](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___intercalate "Documentation for String.intercalate in Lean Language Reference")`.
Finally, a string escaping procedure is needed for JSON strings, so that the Lean string containing `"Hello!"` can be output as `"\"Hello!\""`. Fortunately, the Lean compiler contains an internal function for escaping JSON strings already, called `Lean.Json.escape`. To access this function, add `import Lean` to the beginning of your file.
The function that emits a string from a `JSON` value is declared `partial` because Lean cannot see that it terminates. This is because recursive calls to `[asString](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___asString "Definition of example")` occur in functions that are being applied by `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")`, and this pattern of recursion is complicated enough that Lean cannot see that the recursive calls are actually being performed on smaller values. In an application that just needs to produce JSON strings and doesn't need to mathematically reason about the process, having the function be `partial` is not likely to cause problems.
`partial def JSON.asString (val : JSON) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") val [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [true](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___true "Definition of example") => "true"   | [false](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___false "Definition of example") => "false"   | [null](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___null "Definition of example") => "null"   | [string](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___string "Definition of example") s => "\"" ++ Lean.Json.escape s ++ "\""   | [number](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___number "Definition of example") n => [dropDecimals](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#dropDecimals "Definition of example") n.[toString](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float___toString "Documentation for Float.toString in Lean Language Reference")   | [object](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___object "Definition of example") members =>     let memberToString mem :=       "\"" ++ Lean.Json.escape mem.[fst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.fst in Lean Language Reference") ++ "\": " ++ [asString](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___asString "Definition of example") mem.[snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference")     "{" ++ ", ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") (members.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") memberToString) ++ "}"   | [array](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___array "Definition of example") elements =>     "[" ++ ", ".[separate](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#String___separate "Definition of example") (elements.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") [asString](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___asString "Definition of example")) ++ "]"`
With this definition, the output of serialization is easier to read:
``"{\"title\": \"Functional Programming in Lean\", \"status\": 200, \"record\": \"Programming is fun!\"}"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([buildResponse](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___buildResponse "Definition of example") "Functional Programming in Lean" [Str](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#Ser___Str "Definition of example") "Programming is fun!").[asString](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Coercions/#JSON___asString "Definition of example")`
```
"{\"title\": \"Functional Programming in Lean\", \"status\": 200, \"record\": \"Programming is fun!\"}"
```

##  3.6.7. Messages You May Meet[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=coercion-messages "Permalink")
Natural number literals are overloaded with the `OfNat` type class. Because coercions fire in cases where types don't match, rather than in cases of missing instances, a missing `OfNat` instance for a type does not cause a coercion from `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to be applied:
