##  3.6.8. Design Considerations[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=coercion-design-considerations "Permalink")
Coercions are a powerful tool that should be used responsibly. On the one hand, they can allow an API to naturally follow the everyday rules of the domain being modeled. This can be the difference between a bureaucratic mess of manual conversion functions and a clear program. As Abelson and Sussman wrote in the preface to _Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs_ (MIT Press, 1996),
> Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
Coercions, used wisely, are a valuable means of achieving readable code that can serve as the basis for communication with domain experts. APIs that rely heavily on coercions have a number of important limitations, however. Think carefully about these limitations before using coercions in your own libraries.
First off, coercions are only applied in contexts where enough type information is available for Lean to know all of the types involved, because there are no output parameters in the coercion type classes. This means that a return type annotation on a function can be the difference between a type error and a successfully applied coercion. For example, the coercion from non-empty lists to lists makes the following program work:
`def lastSpider : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   [List.getLast?](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___getLast___ "Documentation for List.getLast? in Lean Language Reference") idahoSpiders`
On the other hand, if the type annotation is omitted, then the result type is unknown, so Lean is unable to find the coercion:
`def lastSpider :=   [List.getLast?](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___getLast___ "Documentation for List.getLast? in Lean Language Reference") `Application type mismatch: The argument   idahoSpiders has type   NonEmptyList [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") but is expected to have type   [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ?m.3 in the application   [List.getLast?](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___getLast___ "Documentation for List.getLast? in Lean Language Reference") idahoSpiders`idahoSpiders`
```
Application type mismatch: The argument
  idahoSpiders
has type
  NonEmptyList [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")
but is expected to have type
  [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") ?m.3
in the application
  [List.getLast?](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___getLast___ "Documentation for List.getLast? in Lean Language Reference") idahoSpiders
```

More generally, when a coercion is not applied for some reason, the user receives the original type error, which can make it difficult to debug chains of coercions.
Finally, coercions are not applied in the context of field accessor notation. This means that there is still an important difference between expressions that need to be coerced and those that don't, and this difference is visible to users of your API.

title: Controlling Instance Search
markdown:
[←3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Type-Classes-and-Polymorphism/#tc-polymorphism "3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism")[3.4. Arrays and Indexing→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Overloading-and-Type-Classes--Arrays-and-Indexing "3.4. Arrays and Indexing")
#  3.3. Controlling Instance Search[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=out-params "Permalink")
An instance of the `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` class is sufficient to allow two expressions with type `Pos` to be conveniently added, producing another `Pos`. However, in many cases, it can be useful to be more flexible and allow _heterogeneous_ operator overloading, where the arguments may have different types. For example, adding a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to a `Pos` or a `Pos` to a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` will always yield a `Pos`:
`def addNatPos : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Pos → Pos   | 0, p => p   | n + 1, p => [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([addNatPos](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addNatPos "Definition of example") n p)  def addPosNat : Pos → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Pos   | p, 0 => p   | p, n + 1 => [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([addPosNat](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addPosNat "Definition of example") p n)`
These functions allow natural numbers to be added to positive numbers, but they cannot be used with the `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` type class, which expects both arguments to `[add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference")` to have the same type.
##  3.3.1. Heterogeneous Overloadings[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=heterogeneous-operators "Permalink")
As mentioned in the section on [overloaded addition](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#overloaded-addition), Lean provides a type class called `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` for overloading addition heterogeneously. The `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` class takes three type parameters: the two argument types and the return type. Instances of `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos` and `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos` allow ordinary addition notation to be used to mix the types:
`instance : [HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos where   [hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") := [addNatPos](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addNatPos "Definition of example")  instance : [HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos where   [hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") := [addPosNat](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addPosNat "Definition of example")`
Given the above two instances, the following examples work:
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (3 : Pos) + (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
8
```
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (3 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) + (5 : Pos)`
```
8
```

The definition of the `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` type class is very much like the following definition of `HPlus` with the corresponding instances:
`class HPlus (α : Type) (β : Type) (γ : Type) where   hPlus : α → β → γ``instance : HPlus [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos where   [hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#ProblematicHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") := [addNatPos](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addNatPos "Definition of example")  instance : HPlus Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos where   [hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#ProblematicHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") := [addPosNat](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#addPosNat "Definition of example")`
However, instances of `HPlus` are significantly less useful than instances of `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")`. When attempting to use these instances with `#eval`, an error occurs:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") toString (`typeclass instance problem is stuck   HPlus Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ?m.6  Note: Lean will not try to resolve this typeclass instance problem because the third type argument to `HPlus` is a metavariable. This argument must be fully determined before Lean will try to resolve the typeclass.  Hint: Adding type annotations and supplying implicit arguments to functions can give Lean more information for typeclass resolution. For example, if you have a variable `x` that you intend to be a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, but Lean reports it as having an unresolved type like `?m`, replacing `x` with `(x : Nat)` can get typeclass resolution un-stuck.`[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#ProblematicHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (3 : Pos) (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")))`
```
typeclass instance problem is stuck
  HPlus Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") ?m.6

Note: Lean will not try to resolve this typeclass instance problem because the third type argument to `HPlus` is a metavariable. This argument must be fully determined before Lean will try to resolve the typeclass.

Hint: Adding type annotations and supplying implicit arguments to functions can give Lean more information for typeclass resolution. For example, if you have a variable `x` that you intend to be a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, but Lean reports it as having an unresolved type like `?m`, replacing `x` with `(x : Nat)` can get typeclass resolution un-stuck.
```

The message indicates that this happens because there is a metavariable in the type, and Lean has no way to solve it.
As discussed in [the initial description of polymorphism](Getting-to-Know-Lean/Polymorphism/#polymorphism), metavariables represent unknown parts of a program that could not be inferred. When an expression is written following `#eval`, Lean attempts to determine its type automatically. In this case, it could not. Because the third type parameter for `HPlus` was unknown, Lean couldn't carry out type class instance search, but instance search is the only way that Lean could determine the expression's type. That is, the `HPlus Pos [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") Pos` instance can only apply if the expression should have type `Pos`, but there's nothing in the program other than the instance itself to indicate that it should have this type.
One solution to the problem is to ensure that all three types are available by adding a type annotation to the whole expression:
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") ([HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#ProblematicHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (3 : Pos) (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Pos)`
```
8
```

However, this solution is not very convenient for users of the positive number library.
##  3.3.2. Output Parameters[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=output-parameters "Permalink")
This problem can also be solved by declaring `γ` to be an _output parameter_. Most type class parameters are inputs to the search algorithm: they are used to select an instance. For example, in an `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` instance, both the type and the natural number are used to select a particular interpretation of a natural number literal. However, in some cases, it can be convenient to start the search process even when some of the type parameters are not yet known, and use the instances that are discovered in the search to determine values for metavariables. The parameters that aren't needed to start instance search are outputs of the process, which is declared with the `[outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference")` modifier:
`class HPlus (α : Type) (β : Type) (γ : [outParam](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Synthesis/#outParam "Documentation for outParam in Lean Language Reference") Type) where   hPlus : α → β → γ`
With this output parameter, type class instance search is able to select an instance without knowing `γ` in advance. For instance:
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (3 : Pos) (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
8
```

It might be helpful to think of output parameters as defining a kind of function. Any given instance of a type class that has one or more output parameters provides Lean with instructions for determining the outputs from the inputs. The process of searching for an instance, possibly recursively, ends up being more powerful than mere overloading. Output parameters can determine other types in the program, and instance search can assemble a collection of underlying instances into a program that has this type.
##  3.3.3. Default Instances[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=default-instances "Permalink")
Deciding whether a parameter is an input or an output controls the circumstances under which Lean will initiate type class search. In particular, type class search does not occur until all inputs are known. However, in some cases, output parameters are not enough, and instance search should also occur when some inputs are unknown. This is a bit like default values for optional function arguments in Python or Kotlin, except default _types_ are being selected.
_Default instances_ are instances that are available for instance search _even when not all their inputs are known_. When one of these instances can be used, it will be used. This can cause programs to successfully type check, rather than failing with errors related to unknown types and metavariables. On the other hand, default instances can make instance selection less predictable. In particular, if an undesired default instance is selected, then an expression may have a different type than expected, which can cause confusing type errors to occur elsewhere in the program. Be selective about where default instances are used!
One example of where default instances can be useful is an instance of `HPlus` that can be derived from an `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` instance. In other words, ordinary addition is a special case of heterogeneous addition in which all three types happen to be the same. This can be implemented using the following instance:
`instance [[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") α] : HPlus α α α where   [hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") := [Add.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference")`
With this instance, `[hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example")` can be used for any addable type, like `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`:
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (3 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
```
8
```

However, this instance will only be used in situations where the types of both arguments are known. For example,
``[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 3 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) (3 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
yields the type

```
[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 3 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```

as expected, but
``[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 : ?m.2 → ?m.3`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
yields a type that contains two metavariables, one for the remaining argument and one for the return type:

```
[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 : ?m.2 → ?m.3
```

In the vast majority of cases, when someone supplies one argument to addition, the other argument will have the same type. To make this instance into a default instance, apply the `[default_instance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Declarations/#Lean___Parser___Attr___default_instance "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` attribute:
`@[[default_instance](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Instance-Declarations/#Lean___Parser___Attr___default_instance "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")] instance [[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") α] : HPlus α α α where   [hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") := [Add.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference")`
With this default instance, the example has a more useful type:
``[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") (5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference"))`
yields

```
[HPlus.hPlus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#BetterHPlus___HPlus___hPlus "Definition of example") 5 : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")
```

Each operator that exists in overloadable heterogeneous and homogeneous versions follows the pattern of a default instance that allows the homogeneous version to be used in contexts where the heterogeneous is expected. The infix operator is replaced with a call to the heterogeneous version, and the homogeneous default instance is selected when possible.
Similarly, simply writing `5` gives a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` rather than a type with a metavariable that is waiting for more information in order to select an `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` instance. This is because the `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is a default instance.
Default instances can also be assigned _priorities_ that affect which will be chosen in situations where more than one might apply. For more information on default instance priorities, please consult the Lean manual.
##  3.3.4. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=out-params-exercises "Permalink")
Define an instance of `[HMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul in Lean Language Reference") (PPoint α) α (PPoint α)` that multiplies both projections by the scalar. It should work for any type `α` for which there is a `[Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference") α` instance. For example,
``{ x := 5.000000, y := 7.400000 }`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") {x := 2.5, y := 3.7 : PPoint [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")} * 2.0`
should yield

```
{ x := 5.000000, y := 7.400000 }
```
[←3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Type-Classes-and-Polymorphism/#tc-polymorphism "3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism")[3.4. Arrays and Indexing→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Arrays-and-Indexing/#Functional-Programming-in-Lean--Overloading-and-Type-Classes--Arrays-and-Indexing "3.4. Arrays and Indexing")

title: Positive Numbers
markdown:
[←3. Overloading and Type Classes](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/#type-classes "3. Overloading and Type Classes")[3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism→](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Type-Classes-and-Polymorphism/#tc-polymorphism "3.2. Type Classes and Polymorphism")
#  3.1. Positive Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=positive-numbers "Permalink")
In some applications, only positive numbers make sense. For example, compilers and interpreters typically use one-indexed line and column numbers for source positions, and a datatype that represents only non-empty lists will never report a length of zero. Rather than relying on natural numbers, and littering the code with assertions that the number is not zero, it can be useful to design a datatype that represents only positive numbers.
One way to represent positive numbers is very similar to `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, except with `one` as the base case instead of `[zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")`:
`inductive Pos : Type where   | one : Pos   | succ : Pos → Pos`
This datatype represents exactly the intended set of values, but it is not very convenient to use. For example, numeric literals are rejected:
`def [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") : Pos := `failed to synthesize   [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") Pos 7 numerals are polymorphic in Lean, but the numeral `7` cannot be used in a context where the expected type is   Pos due to the absence of the instance above  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`7`
```
failed to synthesize
  [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") Pos 7
numerals are polymorphic in Lean, but the numeral `7` cannot be used in a context where the expected type is
  Pos
due to the absence of the instance above

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```

Instead, the constructors must be used directly:
`def seven : Pos :=   [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") ([Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") Pos.one)))))`
Similarly, addition and multiplication are not easy to use:
`def [fourteen](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#fourteen "Definition of example") : Pos := `failed to synthesize   [HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos ?m.3  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`[seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") + [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")`
```
failed to synthesize
  [HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos ?m.3

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```
`def fortyNine : Pos := `failed to synthesize   [HMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos ?m.3  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`[seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") * [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")`
```
failed to synthesize
  [HMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul in Lean Language Reference") Pos Pos ?m.3

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```

Each of these error messages begins with `failed to synthesize`. This indicates that the error is due to an overloaded operation that has not been implemented, and it describes the type class that must be implemented.
##  3.1.1. Classes and Instances[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=classes-and-instances "Permalink")
A type class consists of a name, some parameters, and a collection of _methods_. The parameters describe the types for which overloadable operations are being defined, and the methods are the names and type signatures of the overloadable operations. Once again, there is a terminology clash with object-oriented languages. In object-oriented programming, a method is essentially a function that is connected to a particular object in memory, with special access to the object's private state. Objects are interacted with via their methods. In Lean, the term “method” refers to an operation that has been declared to be overloadable, with no special connection to objects or values or private fields.
One way to overload addition is to define a type class named `Plus`, with an addition method named `plus`. Once an instance of `Plus` for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` has been defined, it becomes possible to add two `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`s using `Plus.plus`:
``8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Plus.plus 5 3`
```
8
```

Adding more instances allows `Plus.plus` to take more types of arguments.
In the following type class declaration, `Plus` is the name of the class, `α : Type` is the only argument, and `plus : α → α → α` is the only method:
`class Plus (α : Type) where   plus : α → α → α`
This declaration says that there is a type class `Plus` that overloads operations with respect to a type `α`. In particular, there is one overloaded operation called `plus` that takes two `α`s and returns an `α`.
Type classes are first class, just as types are first class. In particular, a type class is another kind of type. The type of `Plus` is `Type → Type`, because it takes a type as an argument (`α`) and results in a new type that describes the overloading of `Plus`'s operation for `α`.
To overload `plus` for a particular type, write an instance:
`instance : Plus [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   plus := [Nat.add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___add "Documentation for Nat.add in Lean Language Reference")`
The colon after `instance` indicates that `Plus [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is indeed a type. Each method of class `Plus` should be assigned a value using `:=`. In this case, there is only one method: `plus`.
By default, type class methods are defined in a namespace with the same name as the type class. It can be convenient to `[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")` the namespace so that users don't need to type the name of the class first. Parentheses in an `open` command indicate that only the indicated names from the namespace are to be made accessible:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Plus (plus)```8`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") plus 5 3`
```
8
```

Defining an addition function for `Pos` and an instance of `Plus Pos` allows `[plus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___plus "Definition of example")` to be used to add both `Pos` and `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` values:
`def Pos.plus : Pos → Pos → Pos   | Pos.one, k => [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") k   | [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") n, k => [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") (n.[plus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___plus "Definition of example") k)  instance : Plus Pos where   plus := [Pos.plus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___plus "Definition of example")  def fourteen : Pos := plus [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")`
Because there is not yet an instance of `Plus [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")`, attempting to add two floating-point numbers with `plus` fails with a familiar message:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") `failed to synthesize   Plus [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`plus 5.2 917.25861`
```
failed to synthesize
  Plus [Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")

Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.
```

These errors mean that Lean was unable to find an instance for a given type class.
##  3.1.2. Overloaded Addition[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=overloaded-addition "Permalink")
Lean's built-in addition operator is syntactic sugar for a type class called `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")`, which flexibly allows the arguments to addition to have different types. `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` is short for _heterogeneous addition_. For example, an `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` instance can be written to allow a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to be added to a `[Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")`, resulting in a new `[Float](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Floating-Point-Numbers/#Float-next "Documentation for Float in Lean Language Reference")`. When a programmer writes `x + y`, it is interpreted as meaning `[HAdd.hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x y`.
While an understanding of the full generality of `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` relies on features that are discussed in [another section in this chapter](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Controlling-Instance-Search/#out-params), there is a simpler type class called `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` that does not allow the types of the arguments to be mixed. The Lean libraries are set up so that an instance of `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")` will be found when searching for an instance of `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")` in which both arguments have the same type.
Defining an instance of `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") Pos` allows `Pos` values to use ordinary addition syntax:
`instance : [Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference") Pos where   [add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add.add in Lean Language Reference") := [Pos.plus](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___plus "Definition of example")``def fourteen : Pos := [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") + [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")`
##  3.1.3. Conversion to Strings[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=conversion-to-strings "Permalink")
Another useful built-in class is called `ToString`. Instances of `ToString` provide a standard way of converting values from a given type into strings. For example, a `ToString` instance is used when a value occurs in an interpolated string, and it determines how the `[IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference")` function used at the [beginning of the description of ``](Hello___-World___/Running-a-Program/#running-a-program)`[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` will display a value.
For example, one way to convert a `Pos` into a `[String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")` is to reveal its inner structure. The function `posToString` takes a `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` that determines whether to parenthesize uses of `[Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example")`, which should be `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")` in the initial call to the function and `[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")` in all recursive calls.
`def posToString (atTop : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) (p : Pos) : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") :=   let paren s := [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") atTop [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "(" ++ s ++ ")"   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | Pos.one => "Pos.one"   | [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") n => paren s!"Pos.succ {posToString [false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference") n}"`
Using this function for a `ToString` instance:
`instance : ToString Pos where   toString := posToString [true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`
results in informative, yet overwhelming, output:
``"There are Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ Pos.one)))))"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s!"There are {[seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")}"`
```
"There are Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ (Pos.succ Pos.one)))))"
```

On the other hand, every positive number has a corresponding `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. Converting it to a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` and then using the `ToString [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` instance (that is, the overloading of `ToString` for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`) is a quick way to generate much shorter output:
`def Pos.toNat : Pos → [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   | Pos.one => 1   | [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") n => n.toNat + 1``instance : ToString Pos where   toString x := toString (x.toNat)```"There are 7"`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s!"There are {[seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")}"`
```
"There are 7"
```

When more than one instance is defined, the most recent takes precedence. Additionally, if a type has a `ToString` instance, then it can be used to display the result of `#eval` so `[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")` outputs `7`.
##  3.1.4. Overloaded Multiplication[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=overloaded-multiplication "Permalink")
For multiplication, there is a type class called `[HMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul in Lean Language Reference")` that allows mixed argument types, just like `[HAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd in Lean Language Reference")`. Just as `x + y` is interpreted as `[HAdd.hAdd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HAdd___mk "Documentation for HAdd.hAdd in Lean Language Reference") x y`, `x * y` is interpreted as `[HMul.hMul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#HMul___mk "Documentation for HMul.hMul in Lean Language Reference") x y`. For the common case of multiplication of two arguments with the same type, a `[Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference")` instance suffices.
An instance of `[Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference")` allows ordinary multiplication syntax to be used with `Pos`:
`def Pos.mul : Pos → Pos → Pos   | Pos.one, k => k   | [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") n, k => n.[mul](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___mul "Definition of example") k + k  instance : [Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference") Pos where   [mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul.mul in Lean Language Reference") := [Pos.mul](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___mul "Definition of example")`
With this instance, multiplication works as expected:
``[7, 49, 14]`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [[seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") * Pos.one, [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example") * [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example"), [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") Pos.one * [seven](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#seven "Definition of example")]`
```
[7, 49, 14]
```

##  3.1.5. Literal Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=literal-numbers "Permalink")
It is quite inconvenient to write out a sequence of constructors for positive numbers. One way to work around the problem would be to provide a function to convert a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` into a `Pos`. However, this approach has downsides. First off, because `Pos` cannot represent `0`, the resulting function would either convert a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` to a bigger number, or it would return `[Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") Pos`. Neither is particularly convenient for users. Secondly, the need to call the function explicitly would make programs that use positive numbers much less convenient to write than programs that use `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. Having a trade-off between precise types and convenient APIs means that the precise types become less useful.
There are three type classes that are used to overload numeric literals: `Zero`, `One`, and `OfNat`. Because many types have values that are naturally written with `0`, the `Zero` class allow these specific values to be overridden. It is defined as follows:
`class Zero (α : Type) where   zero : α`
Because `0` is not a positive number, there should be no instance of `[Zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Zero___mk "Documentation for Zero in Lean Language Reference") Pos`.
Similarly, many types have values that are naturally written with `1`. The `One` class allows these to be overridden:
`class One (α : Type) where   one : α`
An instance of `One Pos` makes perfect sense:
`instance : One Pos where   one := Pos.one`
With this instance, `1` can be used for `Pos.one`:
``1`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (1 : Pos)`
```
1
```

In Lean, natural number literals are interpreted using a type class called `OfNat`:
`class OfNat (α : Type) (_ : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) where   ofNat : α`
This type class takes two arguments: `α` is the type for which a natural number is overloaded, and the unnamed `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` argument is the actual literal number that was encountered in the program. The method `ofNat` is then used as the value of the numeric literal. Because the class contains the `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` argument, it becomes possible to define only instances for those values where the number makes sense.
`OfNat` demonstrates that the arguments to type classes do not need to be types. Because types in Lean are first-class participants in the language that can be passed as arguments to functions and given definitions with `def` and `abbrev`, there is no barrier that prevents non-type arguments in positions where a less-flexible language could not permit them. This flexibility allows overloaded operations to be provided for particular values as well as particular types. Additionally, it allows the Lean standard library to arrange for there to be a `[Zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Zero___mk "Documentation for Zero in Lean Language Reference") α` instance whenever there's an `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") α 0` instance, and vice versa. Similarly, an instance of `One α` implies an instance of `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") α 1`, just as an instance of `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") α 1` implies an instance of `One α`.
A sum type that represents natural numbers less than four can be defined as follows:
`inductive LT4 where   | zero   | one   | two   | three`
While it would not make sense to allow _any_ literal number to be used for this type, numbers less than four clearly make sense:
`instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") 0 where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := [LT4.zero](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4___zero "Definition of example")  instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") 1 where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := [LT4.one](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4___one "Definition of example")  instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") 2 where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := [LT4.two](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4___two "Definition of example")  instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") 3 where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") := [LT4.three](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4___three "Definition of example")`
With these instances, the following examples work:
``LT4.three`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (3 : [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example"))`
```
LT4.three
```
``LT4.zero`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (0 : [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example"))`
```
LT4.zero
```

On the other hand, out-of-bounds literals are still not allowed:
`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (`failed to synthesize   [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") 4 numerals are polymorphic in Lean, but the numeral `4` cannot be used in a context where the expected type is   [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example") due to the absence of the instance above  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`4 : [LT4](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#LT4 "Definition of example"))`
```

For `Pos`, the `OfNat` instance should work for _any_ `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` other than `[Nat.zero](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.zero in Lean Language Reference")`. Another way to phrase this is to say that for all natural numbers `n`, the instance should work for `n + 1`. Just as names like `α` automatically become implicit arguments to functions that Lean fills out on its own, instances can take automatic implicit arguments. In this instance, the argument `n` stands for any `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, and the instance is defined for a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` that's one greater:
`instance : [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") Pos (n + 1) where   [ofNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat.ofNat in Lean Language Reference") :=     let rec natPlusOne : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Pos       | 0 => Pos.one       | k + 1 => [Pos.succ](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Positive-Numbers/#Pos___succ "Definition of example") (natPlusOne k)     natPlusOne n`
Because `n` stands for a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` that's one less than what the user wrote, the helper function `natPlusOne` returns a `Pos` that's one greater than its argument. This makes it possible to use natural number literals for positive numbers, but not for zero:
`def eight : Pos := 8``def zero : Pos := `failed to synthesize   [OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference") Pos 0 numerals are polymorphic in Lean, but the numeral `0` cannot be used in a context where the expected type is   Pos due to the absence of the instance above  Hint: Additional diagnostic information may be available using the `set_option diagnostics true` command.`0`

##  3.1.6. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=positive-numbers-exercises "Permalink")
###  3.1.6.1. Another Representation[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=positive-numbers-another-representation "Permalink")
An alternative way to represent a positive number is as the successor of some `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`. Replace the definition of `Pos` with a structure whose constructor is named `succ` that contains a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`:
`structure Pos where   succ ::   pred : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
Define instances of `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference")`, `ToString`, and `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` that allow this version of `Pos` to be used conveniently.
###  3.1.6.2. Even Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=even-numbers-ex "Permalink")
Define a datatype that represents only even numbers. Define instances of `[Add](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Add___mk "Documentation for Add in Lean Language Reference")`, `[Mul](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Mul___mk "Documentation for Mul in Lean Language Reference")`, and `ToString` that allow it to be used conveniently. `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` requires a feature that is introduced in [the next section](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Type-Classes-and-Polymorphism/#tc-polymorphism).
###  3.1.6.3. HTTP Requests[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=http-request-ex "Permalink")
An HTTP request begins with an identification of a HTTP method, such as `GET` or `POST`, along with a URI and an HTTP version. Define an inductive type that represents an interesting subset of the HTTP methods, and a structure that represents HTTP responses. Responses should have a `ToString` instance that makes it possible to debug them. Use a type class to associate different `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` actions with each HTTP method, and write a test harness as an `[IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference")` action that calls each method and prints the result.
