Errors that begin with `unsolved goals` describe tactics that failed to completely build the expressions that they were supposed to. In Lean's tactic language, a _goal_ is a type that a tactic is to fulfill by constructing an appropriate expression behind the scenes. In this case, `constructor` caused `Subschema.cons` to be applied, and the two goals represent the two arguments expected by `cons`. Adding another instance of `constructor` causes the first goal (`HasCol peak "location" [DBType.string](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___string "Definition of example")`) to be addressed with `HasCol.there`, because `peak`'s first column is not `"location"`:

Indeed, a version written without the use of tactics has four constructors:
`example : Subschema [⟨"location", [.string](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___string "Definition of example")⟩] peak :=   .cons (.there .here) .nil`
Instead of experimenting to find the right number of times to write `constructor`, the `repeat` tactic can be used to ask Lean to just keep trying `constructor` as long as it keeps making progress:
`example : Subschema [⟨"location", [.string](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___string "Definition of example")⟩] peak := by⊢ Subschema [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference"){ [name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example") := "location", [contains](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___contains "Definition of example") := [DBType.string](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___string "Definition of example") }[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") peak [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
This more flexible version also works for more interesting `Subschema` problems:
`example : Subschema travelDiary peak := by⊢ Subschema travelDiary peak [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙  example : Subschema travelDiary waterfall := by⊢ Subschema travelDiary waterfall [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
The approach of blindly trying constructors until something works is not very useful for types like `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` or `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`. Just because an expression has type `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` doesn't mean that it's the _correct_ `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`, after all. But types like `HasCol` and `Subschema` are sufficiently constrained by their indices that only one constructor will ever be applicable, which means that the contents of the program itself are less interesting, and a computer can pick the correct one.
If one schema is a subschema of another, then it is also a subschema of the larger schema extended with an additional column. This fact can be captured as a function definition. `Subschema.addColumn` takes evidence that `smaller` is a subschema of `bigger`, and then returns evidence that `smaller` is a subschema of `c :: bigger`, that is, `bigger` with one additional column:
`def Subschema.addColumn :     Subschema smaller bigger →     Subschema smaller (c :: bigger)   | .nil  => .nil   | .cons col sub' => .cons (.there col) sub'.addColumn`
A subschema describes where to find each column from the smaller schema in the larger schema. `Subschema.addColumn` must translate these descriptions from the original larger schema into the extended larger schema. In the `nil` case, the smaller schema is `[]`, and `nil` is also evidence that `[]` is a subschema of `c :: bigger`. In the `cons` case, which describes how to place one column from `smaller` into `bigger`, the placement of the column needs to be adjusted with `there` to account for the new column `c`, and a recursive call adjusts the rest of the columns.
Another way to think about `Subschema` is that it defines a _relation_ between two schemas—the existence of an expression with type `Subschema smaller bigger` means that `(smaller, bigger)` is in the relation. This relation is reflexive, meaning that every schema is a subschema of itself:
`def Subschema.reflexive : (s : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")) → Subschema s s   | [] => .nil   | _ :: cs => .cons .here ([reflexive](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Subschema___reflexive "Definition of example") cs).addColumn`
###  7.3.2.4. Projecting Rows[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=projecting-rows "Permalink")
Given evidence that `s'` is a subschema of `s`, a row in `s` can be projected into a row in `s'`. This is done using the evidence that `s'` is a subschema of `s`, which explains where each column of `s'` is found in `s`. The new row in `s'` is built up one column at a time by retrieving the value from the appropriate place in the old row.
The function that performs this projection, `Row.project`, has three cases, one for each case of `Row` itself. It uses `Row.get` together with each `HasCol` in the `Subschema` argument to construct the projected row:
`def Row.project (row : Row s) : (s' : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")) → Subschema s' s → Row s'   | [], .nil => ()   | [_], .cons c .nil => row.get c   | _::_::_, .cons c cs => (row.get c, row.project _ cs)`
##  7.3.3. Conditions and Selection[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=conditions-and-selection "Permalink")
Projection removes unwanted columns from a table, but queries must also be able to remove unwanted rows. This operation is called _selection_. Selection relies on having a means of expressing which rows are desired.
The example query language contains expressions, which are analogous to what can be written in a `WHERE` clause in SQL. Expressions are represented by the indexed family `DBExpr`. Because expressions can refer to columns from the database, but different sub-expressions all have the same schema, `DBExpr` takes the database schema as a parameter. Additionally, each expression has a type, and these vary, making it an index:
`inductive DBExpr (s : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")) : DBType → Type where   | col (n : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (loc : HasCol s n t) : DBExpr s t   | eq (e1 e2 : DBExpr s t) : DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example")   | lt (e1 e2 : DBExpr s [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")) : DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example")   | and (e1 e2 : DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example")) : DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example")   | const : t.asType → DBExpr s t`
The `col` constructor represents a reference to a column in the database. The `eq` constructor compares two expressions for equality, `lt` checks whether one is less than the other, `and` is Boolean conjunction, and `const` is a constant value of some type.
For example, an expression in `peak` that checks whether the `elevation` column is greater than 1000 and the location is `"Denmark"` can be written:
`def tallInDenmark : DBExpr peak [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example") :=   .and (.lt (.const 1000) (.col "elevation" (by⊢ HasCol peak "elevation" [DBType.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example") [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙)))        (.eq (.col "location" (by⊢ HasCol peak "location" ?m.16 [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙)) (.const "Denmark"))`
This is somewhat noisy. In particular, references to columns contain boilerplate calls to `by [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`. A Lean feature called _macros_ can help make expressions easier to read by eliminating this boilerplate:
`[macro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Notations-and-Macros/Macros/#Lean___Parser___Command___macro "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") "c!" n:term : term => `(DBExpr.col $n (by [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")))`
This declaration adds the `c!` keyword to Lean, and instructs Lean to replace any instance of `c!` followed by an expression with the corresponding `DBExpr.col` construction. Here, `term` stands for Lean expressions, rather than commands, tactics, or some other part of the language. Lean macros are a bit like C preprocessor macros, except they are better integrated into the language and they automatically avoid some of the pitfalls of CPP. In fact, they are very closely related to macros in Scheme and Racket.
With this macro, the expression can be much easier to read:
`def tallInDenmark : DBExpr peak [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example") :=   .and (.lt (.const 1000) (c! "elevation"))        (.eq (c! "location") (.const "Denmark"))`
Finding the value of an expression with respect to a given row uses `Row.get` to extract column references, and it delegates to Lean's operations on values for every other expression:
`def DBExpr.evaluate (row : Row s) : DBExpr s t → t.asType   | .col _ loc => row.get loc   | .eq e1 e2  => evaluate row e1 == evaluate row e2   | .lt e1 e2  => evaluate row e1 < evaluate row e2   | .and e1 e2 => evaluate row e1 && evaluate row e2   | .const v => v`
Evaluating the expression for Valby Bakke, the tallest hill in the Copenhagen area, yields `[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")` because Valby Bakke is much less than 1 km over sea level:
``[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [tallInDenmark](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#tallInDenmark "Definition of example").evaluate ("Valby Bakke", "Denmark", 31, 2023)`
```
[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")
```

Evaluating it for a fictional mountain of 1230m elevation yields `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`:
``[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [tallInDenmark](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#tallInDenmark "Definition of example").evaluate ("Fictional mountain", "Denmark", 1230, 2023)`
```
[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")
```

Evaluating it for the highest peak in the US state of Idaho yields `[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`, as Idaho is not part of Denmark:
``[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [tallInDenmark](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#tallInDenmark "Definition of example").evaluate ("Mount Borah", "USA", 3859, 1996)`
```
[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")
```

##  7.3.4. Queries[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=typed-query-language "Permalink")
The query language is based on relational algebra. In addition to tables, it includes the following operators:
  1. The union of two expressions that have the same schema combines the rows that result from two queries
  2. The difference of two expressions that have the same schema removes rows found in the second result from the rows in the first result
  3. Selection by some criterion filters the result of a query according to an expression
  4. Projection into a subschema, removing columns from the result of a query
  5. Cartesian product, combining every row from one query with every row from another
  6. Renaming a column in the result of a query, which modifies its schema
  7. Prefixing all columns in a query with a name


The last operator is not strictly necessary, but it makes the language more convenient to use.
Once again, queries are represented by an indexed family:
`inductive Query : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example") → Type where   | table : Table s → Query s   | union : Query s → Query s → Query s   | diff : Query s → Query s → Query s   | select : Query s → DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example") → Query s   | project :     Query s → (s' : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")) →     Subschema s' s →     Query s'   | product :     Query s1 → Query s2 →     [disjoint](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#disjoint "Definition of example") (s1.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") [Column.name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example")) (s2.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") [Column.name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example")) →     Query (s1 ++ s2)   | renameColumn :     Query s → (c : HasCol s n t) → (n' : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) →     !((s.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") [Column.name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example")).[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___contains "Documentation for List.contains in Lean Language Reference") n') →     Query (s.renameColumn c n')   | prefixWith :     (n : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) → Query s →     Query (s.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") fun c => {c with [name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example") := n ++ "." ++ c.[name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example")})`
The `select` constructor requires that the expression used for selection return a Boolean. The `product` constructor's type contains a call to `[disjoint](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#disjoint "Definition of example")`, which ensures that the two schemas don't share any names:
`def disjoint [[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference") α] (xs ys : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference") :=   [not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___not "Documentation for Bool.not in Lean Language Reference") (xs.[any](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___any "Documentation for List.any in Lean Language Reference") ys.[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___contains "Documentation for List.contains in Lean Language Reference") || ys.[any](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___any "Documentation for List.any in Lean Language Reference") xs.[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___contains "Documentation for List.contains in Lean Language Reference"))`
The use of an expression of type `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` where a type is expected triggers a coercion from `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` to `Prop`. Just as decidable propositions can be considered to be Booleans, where evidence for the proposition is coerced to `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")` and refutations of the proposition are coerced to `[false](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.false in Lean Language Reference")`, Booleans are coerced into the proposition that states that the expression is equal to `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`. Because all uses of the library are expected to occur in contexts where the schemas are known ahead of time, this proposition can be proved with `by simp`. Similarly, the `renameColumn` constructor checks that the new name does not already exist in the schema. It uses the helper `Schema.renameColumn` to change the name of the column pointed to by `HasCol`:
`def Schema.renameColumn : (s : [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")) → HasCol s n t → [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") → [Schema](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Schema "Definition of example")   | c :: cs, .here, n' => {c with [name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example") := n'} :: cs   | c :: cs, .there next, n' => c :: renameColumn cs next n'`
##  7.3.5. Executing Queries[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=executing-queries "Permalink")
Executing queries requires a number of helper functions. The result of a query is a table; this means that each operation in the query language requires a corresponding implementation that works with tables.
###  7.3.5.1. Cartesian Product[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=executing-cartesian-product "Permalink")
Taking the Cartesian product of two tables is done by appending each row from the first table to each row from the second. First off, due to the structure of `Row`, adding a single column to a row requires pattern matching on its schema in order to determine whether the result will be a bare value or a tuple. Because this is a common operation, factoring the pattern matching out into a helper is convenient:
`def addVal (v : c.[contains](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___contains "Definition of example").asType) (row : Row s) : Row (c :: s) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s, row [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [], () => v   | c' :: cs, v' => (v, v')`
Appending two rows is recursive on the structure of both the first schema and the first row, because the structure of the row proceeds in lock-step with the structure of the schema. When the first row is empty, appending returns the second row. When the first row is a singleton, the value is added to the second row. When the first row contains multiple columns, the first column's value is added to the result of recursion on the remainder of the row.
`def Row.append (r1 : Row s1) (r2 : Row s2) : Row (s1 ++ s2) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s1, r1 [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [], () => r2   | [_], v => [addVal](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#addVal "Definition of example") v r2   | _::_::_, (v, r') => (v, r'.[append](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Row___append "Definition of example") r2)`
`List.flatMap`, found in the standard library, applies a function that itself returns a list to every entry in an input list, returning the result of appending the resulting lists in order:
`def List.flatMap (f : α → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β) : (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) → [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") β   | [] => []   | x :: xs => f x ++ xs.[flatMap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___flatMap "Documentation for List.flatMap in Lean Language Reference") f`
The type signature suggests that `List.flatMap` could be used to implement a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference") [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` instance. Indeed, together with `[pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") x := [x]`, `List.flatMap` does implement a monad. However, it's not a very useful `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance. The `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")` monad is basically a version of `Many` that explores _every_ possible path through the search space in advance, before users have the chance to request some number of values. Because of this performance trap, it's usually not a good idea to define a `[Monad](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Monad___mk "Documentation for Monad in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`. Here, however, the query language has no operator for restricting the number of results to be returned, so combining all possibilities is exactly what is desired:
`def Table.cartesianProduct (table1 : Table s1) (table2 : Table s2) :     Table (s1 ++ s2) :=   table1.[flatMap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___flatMap "Documentation for List.flatMap in Lean Language Reference") fun r1 => table2.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") r1.[append](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Row___append "Definition of example")`
Just as with `List.product`, a loop with mutation in the identity monad can be used as an alternative implementation technique:
`def Table.cartesianProduct (table1 : Table s1) (table2 : Table s2) :     Table (s1 ++ s2) := [Id.run](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Varieties-of-Monads/#Id___run "Documentation for Id.run in Lean Language Reference") [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let mut out : Table (s1 ++ s2) := []   for r1 in table1 do     for r2 in table2 do       out := (r1.[append](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Row___append "Definition of example") r2) :: out   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") out.[reverse](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___reverse "Documentation for List.reverse in Lean Language Reference")`
###  7.3.5.2. Difference[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=executing-difference "Permalink")
Removing undesired rows from a table can be done using `[List.filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference")`, which takes a list and a function that returns a `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`. A new list is returned that contains only the entries for which the function returns `[true](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool.true in Lean Language Reference")`. For instance,
`["Willamette", "Columbia", "Sandy", "Deschutes"].[filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference") (·.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___length "Documentation for String.length in Lean Language Reference") > 8)`
evaluates to
`["Willamette", "Deschutes"]`
because `"Columbia"` and `"Sandy"` have lengths less than or equal to `8`. Removing the entries of a table can be done using the helper `List.without`:
`def List.without [[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference") α] (source banned : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α :=   source.[filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference") fun r => !(banned.[contains](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___contains "Documentation for List.contains in Lean Language Reference") r)`
This will be used with the `[BEq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#BEq___mk "Documentation for BEq in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `Row` when interpreting queries.
###  7.3.5.3. Renaming Columns[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=executing-renaming-columns "Permalink")
Renaming a column in a row is done with a recursive function that traverses the row until the column in question is found, at which point the column with the new name gets the same value as the column with the old name:
`def Row.rename (c : HasCol s n t) (row : Row s) :     Row (s.renameColumn c n') :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s, row, c [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [_], v, .here => v   | _::_::_, (v, r), .here => (v, r)   | _::_::_, (v, r), .there next => [addVal](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#addVal "Definition of example") v (r.rename next)`
While this function changes the _type_ of its argument, the actual return value contains precisely the same data as the original argument. From a run-time perspective, `Row.rename` is nothing but a slow identity function. One difficulty in programming with indexed families is that when performance matters, this kind of operation can get in the way. It takes a very careful, often brittle, design to eliminate these kinds of “re-indexing” functions.
###  7.3.5.4. Prefixing Column Names[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=executing-prefixing-column-names "Permalink")
Adding a prefix to column names is very similar to renaming a column. Instead of proceeding to a desired column and then returning, `prefixRow` must process all columns:
`def prefixRow (row : Row s) :     Row (s.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") fun c => {c with [name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example") := n ++ "." ++ c.[name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example")}) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") s, row [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [], _ => ()   | [_], v => v   | _::_::_, (v, r) => (v, prefixRow r)`
This can be used with `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")` in order to add a prefix to all rows in a table. Once again, this function only exists to change the type of a value.
###  7.3.5.5. Putting the Pieces Together[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=query-exec-runner "Permalink")
With all of these helpers defined, executing a query requires only a short recursive function:
`def Query.exec : Query s → Table s   | [.table](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___table "Definition of example") t => t   | [.union](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___union "Definition of example") q1 q2 => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q1 ++ [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q2   | [.diff](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___diff "Definition of example") q1 q2 => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q1 |>.without ([exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q2)   | .select q e => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q |>.[filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference") e.evaluate   | .project q _ sub => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q |>.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") (·.project _ sub)   | .product q1 q2 _ => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q1 |>.cartesianProduct ([exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q2)   | [.renameColumn](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___renameColumn "Definition of example") q c _ _ => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q |>.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") (·.rename c)   | [.prefixWith](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___prefixWith "Definition of example") _ q => [exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example") q |>.[map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference") prefixRow`
Some arguments to the constructors are not used during execution. In particular, both the constructor `project` and the function `Row.project` take the smaller schema as explicit arguments, but the type of the _evidence_ that this schema is a subschema of the larger schema contains enough information for Lean to fill out the argument automatically. Similarly, the fact that the two tables have disjoint column names that is required by the `product` constructor is not needed by `Table.cartesianProduct`. Generally speaking, dependent types provide many opportunities to have Lean fill out arguments on behalf of the programmer.
Dot notation is used with the results of queries to call functions defined both in the `Table` and `List` namespaces, such `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")`, `[List.filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference")`, and `Table.cartesianProduct`. This works because `Table` is defined using `abbrev`. Just like type class search, dot notation can see through definitions created with `abbrev`.
The implementation of `select` is also quite concise. After executing the query `q`, `[List.filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference")` is used to remove the rows that do not satisfy the expression. `[List.filter](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___filter "Documentation for List.filter in Lean Language Reference")` expects a function from `Row s` to `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`, but `DBExpr.evaluate` has type `Row s → DBExpr s t → t.asType`. Because the type of the `select` constructor requires that the expression have type `DBExpr s [.bool](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___bool "Definition of example")`, `t.asType` is actually `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")` in this context.
A query that finds the heights of all mountain peaks with an elevation greater than 500 meters can be written:
`[open](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___open "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") Query [in](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Namespaces-and-Sections/#Lean___Parser___Command___in "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") def example1 :=   [table](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___table "Definition of example") [mountainDiary](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#mountainDiary "Definition of example") |>.select   (.lt (.const 500) (c! "elevation")) |>.project   [⟨"elevation", [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")⟩] (by⊢ Subschema [[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference"){ [name](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___name "Definition of example") := "elevation", [contains](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Column___contains "Definition of example") := [DBType.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example") }[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") peak [repeat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/The-Tactic-Language/#repeat "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙)`
Executing it returns the expected list of integers:
``[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")3637[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 1519[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 2549[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [example1](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#example1 "Definition of example").[exec](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#Query___exec "Definition of example")`
```
[[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")3637[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 1519[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 2549[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")
```

To plan a sightseeing tour, it may be relevant to match all pairs mountains and waterfalls in the same location. This can be done by taking the Cartesian product of both tables, selecting only the rows in which they are equal, and then projecting out the names:

###  7.3.5.6. Errors You May Meet[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=typed-queries-error-messages "Permalink")

```

Lean's macro system contains everything needed not only to provide a convenient syntax for queries, but also to arrange for the error messages to be helpful. Unfortunately, it is beyond the scope of this book to provide a description of implementing languages with Lean macros. An indexed family such as `Query` is probably best as the core of a typed database interaction library, rather than its user interface.
##  7.3.6. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=typed-query-exercises "Permalink")
###  7.3.6.1. Dates
Define a structure to represent dates. Add it to the `DBType` universe and update the rest of the code accordingly. Provide the extra `DBExpr` constructors that seem to be necessary.
###  7.3.6.2. Nullable Types
Add support for nullable columns to the query language by representing database types with the following structure:
`structure NDBType where   underlying : DBType   nullable : [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")  abbrev NDBType.asType (t : [NDBType](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#NDBType "Definition of example")) : Type :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") t.[nullable](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#NDBType___nullable "Definition of example") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") t.[underlying](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#NDBType___underlying "Definition of example").asType   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     t.[underlying](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#NDBType___underlying "Definition of example").asType`
Use this type in place of `DBType` in `Column` and `DBExpr`, and look up SQL's rules for `NULL` and comparison operators to determine the types of `DBExpr`'s constructors.
###  7.3.6.3. Experimenting with Tactics
What is the result of asking Lean to find values of the following types using `by repeat constructor`? Explain why each gives the result that it does.
  * `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
  * `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`
  * `Vect [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") 4`
  * `Row []`
  * `Row [⟨"price", [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")⟩]`
  * `Row peak`
  * `HasCol [⟨"price", [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")⟩, ⟨"price", [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")⟩] "price" [.int](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#DBType___int "Definition of example")`

title: Programming, Proving, and Performance
markdown:
[←Interlude: Tactics, Induction, and Proofs](Interlude___-Tactics___-Induction___-and-Proofs/#tactics-induction-proofs "Interlude: Tactics, Induction, and Proofs")[8.1. Tail Recursion→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Tail-Recursion/#tail-recursion "8.1. Tail Recursion")
#  8. Programming, Proving, and Performance
This chapter is about programming. Programs need to compute the correct result, but they also need to do so efficiently. To write efficient functional programs, it's important to know both how to use data structures appropriately and how to think about the time and space needed to run a program.
This chapter is also about proofs. One of the most important data structures for efficient programming in Lean is the array, but safe use of arrays requires proving that array indices are in bounds. Furthermore, most interesting algorithms on arrays do not follow the pattern of structural recursion—instead, they iterate over the array. While these algorithms terminate, Lean will not necessarily be able to automatically check this. Proofs can be used to demonstrate why a program terminates.
Rewriting programs to make them faster often results in code that is more difficult to understand. Proofs can also show that two programs always compute the same answers, even if they do so with different algorithms or implementation techniques. In this way, the slow, straightforward program can serve as a specification for the fast, complicated version.
Combining proofs and programming allows programs to be both safe and efficient. Proofs allow elision of run-time bounds checks, they render many tests unnecessary, and they provide an extremely high level of confidence in a program without introducing any runtime performance overhead. However, proving theorems about programs can be time consuming and expensive, so other tools are often more economical.
Interactive theorem proving is a deep topic. This chapter provides only a taste, oriented towards the proofs that come up in practice while programming in Lean. Most interesting theorems are not closely related to programming. Please refer to [Next Steps](Next-Steps/#next-steps) for a list of resources for learning more. Just as when learning programming, however, there's no substitute for hands-on experience when learning to write proofs—it's time to get started!
  1. [8.1. Tail Recursion](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Tail-Recursion/#tail-recursion)
  2. [8.2. Proving Equivalence](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Proving-Equivalence/#proving-tail-rec-equiv)
  3. [8.3. Arrays and Termination](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#array-termination)
  4. [8.4. More Inequalities](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#more-inequalities)
  5. [8.5. Bounded Numbers](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Bounded-Numbers/#Fin)
  6. [8.6. Insertion Sort and Array Mutation](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Insertion-Sort-and-Array-Mutation/#insertion-sort-mutation)
  7. [8.7. Special Types](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Special-Types/#runtime-special-types)
  8. [8.8. Summary](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Summary/#programs-proofs-summary)

title: Arrays and Termination
markdown:
[←8.2. Proving Equivalence](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Proving-Equivalence/#proving-tail-rec-equiv "8.2. Proving Equivalence")[8.4. More Inequalities→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#more-inequalities "8.4. More Inequalities")
#  8.3. Arrays and Termination[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=array-termination "Permalink")
To write efficient code, it is important to select appropriate data structures. Linked lists have their place: in some applications, the ability to share the tails of lists is very important. However, most use cases for a variable-length sequential collection of data are better served by arrays, which have both less memory overhead and better locality.
Arrays, however, have two drawbacks relative to lists:
  1. Arrays are accessed through indexing, rather than by pattern matching, which imposes [proof obligations](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#props-proofs-indexing) in order to maintain safety.
  2. A loop that processes an entire array from left to right is a tail-recursive function, but it does not have an argument that decreases on each call.


Making effective use of arrays requires knowing how to prove to Lean that an array index is in bounds, and how to prove that an array index that approaches the size of the array also causes the program to terminate. Both of these are expressed using an inequality proposition, rather than propositional equality.
##  8.3.1. Inequality[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inequality "Permalink")
Because different types have different notions of ordering, inequality is governed by two type classes, called `[LE](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE in Lean Language Reference")` and `[LT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT in Lean Language Reference")`. The table in the section on [standard type classes](Overloading-and-Type-Classes/Standard-Classes/#equality-and-ordering) describes how these classes relate to the syntax:  
|  Expression  |  Desugaring  |  Class Name  |  
| --- | --- | --- |  
|  `x < y`  |  `[LT.lt](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") x y`  |  `[LT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
|  `x ≤ y`  |  `[LE.le](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") x y`  |  `[LE](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
|  `x > y`  |  `[LT.lt](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") y x`  |  `[LT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
|  `x ≥ y`  |  `[LE.le](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE.le in Lean Language Reference") y x`  |  `[LE](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE in Lean Language Reference")`  |  
In other words, a type may customize the meaning of the `<` and `≤` operators, while `>` and `≥` derive their meanings from `<` and `≤`. The classes `[LT](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT in Lean Language Reference")` and `[LE](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LE___mk "Documentation for LE in Lean Language Reference")` have methods that return propositions rather than `[Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")`s:
`class LE (α : Type u) where   le : α → α → Prop  class LT (α : Type u) where   lt : α → α → Prop`
The instance of `[LE](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___LE "Definition of example")` for `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` delegates to `Nat.le`:
`instance : [LE](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___LE "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   [le](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___LE___le "Definition of example") := Nat.le`
Defining `Nat.le` requires a feature of Lean that has not yet been presented: it is an inductively-defined relation.
###  8.3.1.1. Inductively-Defined Propositions, Predicates, and Relations[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inductive-props "Permalink")
`Nat.le` is an _inductively-defined relation_. Just as `inductive` can be used to create new datatypes, it can be used to create new propositions. When a proposition takes an argument, it is referred to as a _predicate_ that may be true for some, but not all, potential arguments. Propositions that take multiple arguments are called _relations_.
Each constructor of an inductively defined proposition is a way to prove it. In other words, the declaration of the proposition describes the different forms of evidence that it is true. A proposition with no arguments that has a single constructor can be quite easy to prove:
`inductive EasyToProve : Prop where   | heresTheProof : EasyToProve`
The proof consists of using its constructor:
`theorem fairlyEasy : EasyToProve := by⊢ EasyToProve   [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
In fact, the proposition `True`, which should always be easy to prove, is defined just like `EasyToProve`:
`inductive True : Prop where   | intro : True`
Inductively-defined propositions that don't take arguments are not nearly as interesting as inductively-defined datatypes. This is because data is interesting in its own right—the natural number `3` is different from the number `35`, and someone who has ordered 3 pizzas will be upset if 35 arrive at their door 30 minutes later. The constructors of a proposition describe ways in which the proposition can be true, but once a proposition has been proved, there is no need to know _which_ underlying constructors were used. This is why most interesting inductively-defined types in the `Prop` universe take arguments.
The inductively-defined predicate `IsThree` states that its argument is three:
`inductive IsThree : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Prop where   | isThree : IsThree 3`
The mechanism used here is just like [indexed families such as `HasCol`](Programming-with-Dependent-Types/Worked-Example___-Typed-Queries/#column-pointers), except the resulting type is a proposition that can be proved rather than data that can be used.
Using this predicate, it is possible to prove that three is indeed three:
`theorem three_is_three : IsThree 3 := by⊢ IsThree 3   [constructor](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#constructor "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")All goals completed! 🐙`
Similarly, `IsFive` is a predicate that states that its argument is `5`:
`inductive IsFive : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Prop where   | isFive : IsFive 5`
If a number is three, then the result of adding two to it should be five. This can be expressed as a theorem statement:
`theorem three_plus_two_five : IsThree n → IsFive (n + 2) := `unsolved goals n:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean
