The standard false proposition `[False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")` has no constructors, making it impossible to provide direct evidence for. The only way to provide evidence for `[False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")` is if an assumption is itself impossible, similarly to how `nomatch` can be used to mark code that the type system can see is unreachable. As described in [the initial Interlude on proofs](Interlude___-Propositions___-Proofs___-and-Indexing/#connectives), the negation `[Not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference") A` is short for `A → [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`. `[Not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference") A` can also be written `¬A`.
It is not the case that four is three:
`theorem four_is_not_three : ¬ IsThree 4 := `unsolved goals ⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4`by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4 [skip](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#skip "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4`
The initial proof goal contains `[Not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")`:

```
unsolved goals
⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4
```

The fact that it's actually a function type can be exposed using `[unfold](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#unfold "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`:
`theorem four_is_not_three : ¬ IsThree 4 := `unsolved goals ⊢ IsThree 4 → [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4 [unfold](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#unfold "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") [Not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")⊢ IsThree 4 → [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`
```
unsolved goals
⊢ IsThree 4 → [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")
```

Because the goal is a function type, `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can be used to convert the argument into an assumption. There is no need to keep `[unfold](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#unfold "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")`, as `[intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` can unfold the definition of `[Not](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")` itself:
`theorem four_is_not_three : ¬ IsThree 4 := `unsolved goals h:IsThree 4⊢ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4 [intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hh:IsThree 4⊢ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")`
```
unsolved goals
h:IsThree 4⊢ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")
```

In this proof, the `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic solves the goal immediately:
`theorem four_is_not_three : ¬ IsThree 4 := by⊢ [¬](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#Not "Documentation for Not in Lean Language Reference")IsThree 4   [intro](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#intro "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hh:IsThree 4⊢ [False](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Truth/#False "Documentation for False in Lean Language Reference")   [cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") hAll goals completed! 🐙`
Just as a pattern match on a `Vect [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") 2` doesn't need to include a case for `Vect.nil`, a proof by cases over `IsThree 4` doesn't need to include a case for `isThree`.
###  8.3.1.2. Inequality of Natural Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inequality-of-natural-numbers "Permalink")
The definition of `Nat.le` has a parameter and an index:
`inductive Nat.le (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") → Prop   | refl : Nat.le n n   | step : Nat.le n m → Nat.le n (m + 1)`
The parameter `n` is the number that should be smaller, while the index is the number that should be greater than or equal to `n`. The `refl` constructor is used when both numbers are equal, while the `step` constructor is used when the index is greater than `n`.
From the perspective of evidence, a proof that `n≤kn \leq kn≤k` consists of finding some number `ddd` such that `n+d=mn + d = mn+d=m`. In Lean, the proof then consists of a `Nat.le.refl` constructor wrapped by `ddd` instances of `Nat.le.step`. Each `step` constructor adds one to its index argument, so `ddd` `step` constructors adds `ddd` to the larger number. For example, evidence that four is less than or equal to seven consists of three `step`s around a `refl`:
`theorem four_le_seven : 4 ≤ 7 :=   open Nat.le in   [step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference") ([step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference") ([step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference") [refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.refl in Lean Language Reference")))`
The strict less-than relation is defined by adding one to the number on the left:
`def Nat.lt (n m : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : Prop :=   Nat.le (n + 1) m  instance : [LT](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___LT "Definition of example") [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") where   [lt](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___LT___lt "Definition of example") := [Nat.lt](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#Orders___Nat___lt "Definition of example")`
Evidence that four is strictly less than seven consists of two `[step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference")`'s around a `[refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.refl in Lean Language Reference")`:
`theorem four_lt_seven : 4 < 7 :=   open Nat.le in   [step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference") ([step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference") [refl](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.refl in Lean Language Reference"))`
This is because `4 < 7` is equivalent to `5 ≤ 7`.
##  8.3.2. Proving Termination[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=proving-termination "Permalink")
The function `Array.map` transforms an array with a function, returning a new array that contains the result of applying the function to each element of the input array. Writing it as a tail-recursive function follows the usual pattern of delegating to a function that passes the output array in an accumulator. The accumulator is initialized with an empty array. The accumulator-passing helper function also takes an argument that tracks the current index into the array, which starts at `0`:
`def Array.map (f : α → β) (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β :=   arrayMapHelper f arr [Array.empty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___empty "Documentation for Array.empty in Lean Language Reference") 0`
The helper should, at each iteration, check whether the index is still in bounds. If so, it should loop again with the transformed element added to the end of the accumulator and the index incremented by `1`. If not, then it should terminate and return the accumulator. An initial implementation of this code fails because Lean is unable to prove that the array index is valid:
`def arrayMapHelper (f : α → β) (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α)     (soFar : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     arrayMapHelper f arr (soFar.[push](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___push "Documentation for Array.push in Lean Language Reference") (f `failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:   - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid   - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid   - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type   - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid α:Type ?u.3051β:Type ?u.3054f:α → βarr:[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") αsoFar:[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") βi:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ i [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")`arr[i])) (i + 1) [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") soFar`
```
failed to prove index is valid, possible solutions:
  - Use `have`-expressions to prove the index is valid
  - Use `a[i]!` notation instead, runtime check is performed, and 'Panic' error message is produced if index is not valid
  - Use `a[i]?` notation instead, result is an `Option` type
  - Use `a[i]'h` notation instead, where `h` is a proof that index is valid
α:Type ?u.3051β:Type ?u.3054f:α → βarr:[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") αsoFar:[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") βi:[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")⊢ i [<](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")
```

However, the conditional expression already checks the precise condition that the array index's validity demands (namely, `i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")`). Adding a name to the `if` resolves the issue, because it adds an assumption that the array indexing tactic can use:
`def arrayMapHelper (f : α → β) (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α)     (soFar : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") inBounds : i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     arrayMapHelper f arr (soFar.[push](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___push "Documentation for Array.push in Lean Language Reference") (f arr[i])) (i + 1)   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") soFar`
Lean accepts the modified program, even though the recursive call is not made on an argument to one of the input constructors. In fact, both the accumulator and the index grow, rather than shrinking.
Behind the scenes, Lean's proof automation constructs a termination proof. Reconstructing this proof can make it easier to understand the cases that Lean cannot automatically recognize.
Why does `arrayMapHelper` terminate? Each iteration checks whether the index `i` is still in bounds for the array `arr`. If so, `i` is incremented and the loop repeats. If not, the program terminates. Because `arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")` is a finite number, `i` can be incremented only a finite number of times. Even though no argument to the function decreases on each call, `arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") - i` decreases toward zero.
The value that decreases at each recursive call is called a _measure_. Lean can be instructed to use a specific expression as the measure of termination by providing a `termination_by` clause at the end of a definition. For `arrayMapHelper`, the explicit measure looks like this:
`def arrayMapHelper (f : α → β) (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α)     (soFar : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") β :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") inBounds : i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     arrayMapHelper f arr (soFar.[push](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___push "Documentation for Array.push in Lean Language Reference") (f arr[i])) (i + 1)   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") soFar termination_by arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") - i`
A similar termination proof can be used to write `Array.find`, a function that finds the first element in an array that satisfies a Boolean function and returns both the element and its index:
`def Array.find (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) :     [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   findHelper arr p 0`
Once again, the helper function terminates because `arr.size - i` decreases as `i` increases:
`def findHelper (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"))     (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h : i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let x := arr[i]     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p x [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")       [some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") (i, x)     [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") findHelper arr p (i + 1)   [else](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`
Adding a question mark to `termination_by` (that is, using `termination_by?`) causes Lean to explicitly suggest the measure that it chose. Clicking `[apply]` replaces `termination_by?` with the suggested measure:
`def findHelper (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference"))     (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") h : i < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termDepIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     let x := arr[i]     [if](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") p x [then](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Conditionals/#termIfThenElse "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") i`termination_by?`
```
Try this:
  [apply] termination_by arr.size - i
```

Not all termination arguments are as quite as simple as this one. However, the basic structure of identifying some expression based on the function's arguments that will decrease in each call occurs in all termination proofs. Sometimes, creativity can be required in order to figure out just why a function terminates, and sometimes Lean requires additional proofs in order to accept that the measure in fact decreases.
##  8.3.3. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=array-termination-exercises "Permalink")
  * Implement a `[ForM](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#ForM___mk "Documentation for ForM in Lean Language Reference") m ([Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α)` instance on arrays using a tail-recursive accumulator-passing function and a `termination_by` clause.
  * Reimplement `Array.map`, `Array.find`, and the `[ForM](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#ForM___mk "Documentation for ForM in Lean Language Reference")` instance using `for`` ... ``in`` ...` loops in the identity monad and compare the resulting code.
  * Reimplement array reversal using a `for`` ... ``in`` ...` loop in the identity monad. Compare it to the tail-recursive function. 

[←8.2. Proving Equivalence](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Proving-Equivalence/#proving-tail-rec-equiv "8.2. Proving Equivalence")[8.4. More Inequalities→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#more-inequalities "8.4. More Inequalities")

title: Bounded Numbers
markdown:
[←8.4. More Inequalities](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#more-inequalities "8.4. More Inequalities")[8.6. Insertion Sort and Array Mutation→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Insertion-Sort-and-Array-Mutation/#insertion-sort-mutation "8.6. Insertion Sort and Array Mutation")
#  8.5. Bounded Numbers[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=Fin "Permalink")
The `[GetElem](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#GetElem___mk "Documentation for GetElem in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `[Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` requires a proof that the provided `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` is smaller than the array. In practice, these proofs often end up being passed to functions along with the indices. Rather than passing an index and a proof separately, a type called `Fin` can be used to bundle up the index and the proof into a single value. This can make code easier to read.
The type `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") n` represents numbers that are strictly less than `n`. In other words, `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 3` describes `0`, `1`, and `2`, while `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 0` has no values at all. The definition of `Fin` resembles `[Subtype](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Subtypes/#Subtype___mk "Documentation for Subtype in Lean Language Reference")`, as a `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") n` is a structure that contains a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` and a proof that it is less than `n`:
`structure Fin (n : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) where   val  : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")   isLt : [LT.lt](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#LT___mk "Documentation for LT.lt in Lean Language Reference") val n`
Lean includes instances of `ToString` and `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` that allow `Fin` values to be conveniently used as numbers. In other words, the output of `[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (5 : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 8)` is `5`, rather than something like `{val := 5, isLt := _}`.
Instead of failing when the provided number is larger than the bound, the `[OfNat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Literals/#OfNat___mk "Documentation for OfNat in Lean Language Reference")` instance for `Fin` returns a value modulo the bound. This means that `[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (45 : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 10)` results in `5` rather than a compile-time error.
In a return type, a `Fin` returned as a found index makes its connection to the data structure in which it was found more clear. The `Array.find` in the [previous section](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#proving-termination) returns an index that the caller cannot immediately use to perform lookups into the array, because the information about its validity has been lost. A more specific type results in a value that can be used without making the program significantly more complicated:
`def findHelper (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) (p : α → [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) (i : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")) :     [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") × α) := [Bool](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Booleans/#Bool___false "Documentation for Bool in Lean Language Reference")) : [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference") × α) :=   [findHelper](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Bounded-Numbers/#Finny___findHelper "Definition of example") arr p 0`
##  8.5.1. Exercise[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=Fin-exercises "Permalink")
Write a function `Fin.next? : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") n → [Option](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option in Lean Language Reference") ([Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") n)` that returns the next largest `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference")` when it would be in bounds, or `[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")` if not. Check that
``[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") 4`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (3 : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 8).next?`
outputs

```
[some](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.some in Lean Language Reference") 4
```

and that
``[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") (7 : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") 8).next?`
outputs

```
[none](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Optional-Values/#Option___none "Documentation for Option.none in Lean Language Reference")
```

title: Insertion Sort and Array Mutation
markdown:
[←8.5. Bounded Numbers](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Bounded-Numbers/#Fin "8.5. Bounded Numbers")[8.7. Special Types→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Special-Types/#runtime-special-types "8.7. Special Types")
#  8.6. Insertion Sort and Array Mutation[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=insertion-sort-mutation "Permalink")
While insertion sort does not have the optimal worst-case time complexity for a sorting algorithm, it still has a number of useful properties:
  * It is simple and straightforward to implement and understand
  * It is an in-place algorithm, requiring no additional space to run
  * It is a stable sort
  * It is fast when the input is already almost sorted


In-place algorithms are particularly useful in Lean due to the way it manages memory. In some cases, operations that would normally copy an array can be optimized into mutation. This includes swapping elements in an array.
Most languages and run-time systems with automatic memory management, including JavaScript, the JVM, and .NET, use tracing garbage collection. When memory needs to be reclaimed, the system starts at a number of _roots_ (such as the call stack and global values) and then determines which values can be reached by recursively chasing pointers. Any values that can't be reached are deallocated, freeing memory.
Reference counting is an alternative to tracing garbage collection that is used by a number of languages, including Python, Swift, and Lean. In a system with reference counting, each object in memory has a field that tracks how many references there are to it. When a new reference is established, the counter is incremented. When a reference ceases to exist, the counter is decremented. When the counter reaches zero, the object is immediately deallocated.
Reference counting has one major disadvantage compared to a tracing garbage collector: circular references can lead to memory leaks. If object `AAA` references object `BBB` , and object `BBB` references object `AAA`, they will never be deallocated, even if nothing else in the program references either `AAA` or `BBB`. Circular references result either from uncontrolled recursion or from mutable references. Because Lean supports neither, it is impossible to construct circular references.
Reference counting means that the Lean runtime system's primitives for allocating and deallocating data structures can check whether a reference count is about to fall to zero, and re-use an existing object instead of allocating a new one. This is particularly important when working with large arrays.
An implementation of insertion sort for Lean arrays should satisfy the following criteria:
  1. Lean should accept the function without a `partial` annotation
  2. If passed an array to which there are no other references, it should modify the array in-place rather than allocating a new one


The first criterion is easy to check: if Lean accepts the definition, then it is satisfied. The second, however, requires a means of testing it. Lean provides a built-in function called `[dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference")` with the following signature:
``[dbgTraceIfShared.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference") {α : Type u} (s : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (a : α) : α`[#check](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___check "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference")`
```
[dbgTraceIfShared.{u}](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference") {α : Type u} (s : [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) (a : α) : α
```

It takes a string and a value as arguments, and prints a message that uses the string to standard error if the value has more than one reference, returning the value. This is not, strictly speaking, a pure function. However, it is intended to be used only during development to check that a function is in fact able to re-use memory rather than allocating and copying.
When learning to use `[dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference")`, it's important to know that `#eval` will report that many more values are shared than in compiled code. This can be confusing. It's important to build an executable with `lake` rather than experimenting in an editor.
Insertion sort consists of two loops. The outer loop moves a pointer from left to right across the array to be sorted. After each iteration, the region of the array to the left of the pointer is sorted, while the region to the right may not yet be sorted. The inner loop takes the element pointed to by the pointer and moves it to the left until the appropriate location has been found and the loop invariant has been restored. In other words, each iteration inserts the next element of the array into the appropriate location in the sorted region.
##  8.6.1. The Inner Loop[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=inner-insertion-sort-loop "Permalink")
The inner loop of insertion sort can be implemented as a tail-recursive function that takes the array and the index of the element being inserted as arguments. The element being inserted is repeatedly swapped with the element to its left until either the element to the left is smaller or the beginning of the array is reached. The inner loop is structurally recursive on the `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` that is inside the `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference")` used to index into the array:
`def insertSorted [[Ord](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ord___mk "Documentation for Ord in Lean Language Reference") α] (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) (i : [Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") If the index `i` is `0`, then the element being inserted into the sorted region has reached the beginning of the region and is the smallest. If the index is `i' + 1`, then the element at `i'` should be compared to the element at `i`. Note that while `i` is a `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference") arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")`, `i'` is just a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` because it results from the `[val](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin.val in Lean Language Reference")` field of `i`. Nonetheless, the proof automation used for checking array index notation includes a solver for linear integer arithmetic, so `i'` is automatically usable as an index.
The two elements are looked up and compared. If the element to the left is less than or equal to the element being inserted, then the loop is finished and the invariant has been restored. If the element to the left is greater than the element being inserted, then the elements are swapped and the inner loop begins again. `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")` takes both of its indices as `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")`s, using the same tactics as array indexing behind the scenes to ensure that they are in bounds.
Nonetheless, the `[Fin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Finite-Natural-Numbers/#Fin___mk "Documentation for Fin in Lean Language Reference")` used for the recursive call needs a proof that `i'` is in bounds for the result of swapping two elements. The `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic's database contains the fact that swapping two elements of an array doesn't change its size, and the `[*]` argument instructs it to additionally use the assumption introduced by `have`. Omitting the `have`-expression with the proof that `i' < arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")` reveals the following goal:

```

##  8.6.2. The Outer Loop[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=outer-insertion-sort-loop "Permalink")
The outer loop of insertion sort moves the pointer from left to right, invoking `insertSorted` at each iteration to insert the element at the pointer into the correct position in the array. The basic form of the loop resembles the implementation of `Array.map`:

While Lean can prove that a `[Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")` that increases towards a constant bound at each iteration leads to a terminating function, this function has no constant bound because the array is replaced with the result of calling `insertSorted` at each iteration.

###  8.6.2.1. Termination[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=insertionSortLoop-termination "Permalink")

The problem is that Lean has no way to know that `insertSorted` returns an array that's the same size as the one it is passed. In order to prove that `insertionSortLoop` terminates, it is necessary to first prove that `insertSorted` doesn't change the size of the array. Copying the unproved termination condition from the error message to the function and “proving” it with `[sorry](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#sorry "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` allows the function to be temporarily accepted:

Because `insertSorted` is structurally recursive on the index of the element being inserted, the proof should be by induction on the index. In the base case, the array is returned unchanged, so its length certainly does not change. For the inductive step, the induction hypothesis is that a recursive call on the next smaller index will not change the length of the array. There are two cases two consider: either the element has been fully inserted into the sorted region and the array is returned unchanged, in which case the length is also unchanged, or the element is swapped with the next one before the recursive call. However, swapping two elements in an array doesn't change the size of it, and the induction hypothesis states that the recursive call with the next index returns an array that's the same size as its argument. Thus, the size remains unchanged.
Translating this English-language theorem statement to Lean and proceeding using the techniques from this chapter is enough to prove the base case and make progress in the inductive step:

Unfortunately, the induction hypothesis is not strong enough to prove this goal. The induction hypothesis states that calling `insertSorted` on `arr` leaves the size unchanged, but the proof goal is to show that the result of the recursive call with the result of swapping leaves the size unchanged. Successfully completing the proof requires an induction hypothesis that works for _any_ array that is passed to `insertSorted` together with the smaller index as an argument
It is possible to get a strong induction hypothesis by using the `generalizing` option to the `[induction](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#induction "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` tactic. This option brings additional assumptions from the context into the statement that's used to generate the base case, the induction hypothesis, and the goal to be shown in the inductive step. However, this whole proof is beginning to get unmanageable. The next step would be to introduce a variable standing for the length of the result of swapping, show that it is equal to `arr.[size](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___size "Documentation for Array.size in Lean Language Reference")`, and then show that this variable is also equal to the length of the array that results from the recursive call. The Lean library includes the theorem `Array.size_swap`, which states that swapping two elements of an array doesn't change its size. By default, `[grind](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#grind "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` doesn't use this fact, but once instructed to do so, it can take care of all four cases:

##  8.6.3. The Driver Function[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=insertion-sort-driver-function "Permalink")
Insertion sort itself calls `insertionSortLoop`, initializing the index that demarcates the sorted region of the array from the unsorted region to `0`:
`def insertionSort [[Ord](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ord___mk "Documentation for Ord in Lean Language Reference") α] (arr : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α) : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") α :=    insertionSortLoop arr 0`
A few quick tests show the function is at least not blatantly wrong:
``[#[](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___toArray "Documentation for List.toArray in Lean Language Reference")1[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 3[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 4[,](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference") 7[]](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___toArray "Documentation for List.toArray in Lean Language Reference")`[#eval](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Interacting-with-Lean/#Lean___Parser___Command___eval "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") insertionSort #[3, 1, 7, 4]`

##  8.6.4. Is This Really Insertion Sort?[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=insertion-sort-in-place "Permalink")
Insertion sort is _defined_ to be an in-place sorting algorithm. What makes it useful, despite its quadratic worst-case run time, is that it is a stable sorting algorithm that doesn't allocate extra space and that handles almost-sorted data efficiently. If each iteration of the inner loop allocated a new array, then the algorithm wouldn't _really_ be insertion sort.
Lean's array operations, such as `[Array.set](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___set "Documentation for Array.set in Lean Language Reference")` and `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")`, check whether the array in question has a reference count that is greater than one. If so, then the array is visible to multiple parts of the code, which means that it must be copied. Otherwise, Lean would no longer be a pure functional language. However, when the reference count is exactly one, there are no other potential observers of the value. In these cases, the array primitives mutate the array in place. What other parts of the program don't know can't hurt them.
Lean's proof logic works at the level of pure functional programs, not the underlying implementation. This means that the best way to discover whether a program unnecessarily copies data is to test it. Adding calls to `[dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference")` at each point where mutation is desired causes the provided message to be printed to `stderr` when the value in question has more than one reference.
Insertion sort has precisely one place that is at risk of copying rather than mutating: the call to `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")`. Replacing `arr.[swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference") i' i` with `([dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference") "array to swap" arr).[swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference") i' i` causes the program to emit `shared RC array to swap` whenever it is unable to mutate the array. However, this change to the program changes the proofs as well, because now there's a call to an additional function. Adding a local assumption that `[dbgTraceIfShared](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Run-Time-Code/Reference-Counting/#dbgTraceIfShared "Documentation for dbgTraceIfShared in Lean Language Reference")` preserves the length of its argument and adding it to some calls to `[simp](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#simp "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference")` is enough to fix the program and proofs.
The complete instrumented code for insertion sort is:

A bit of cleverness is required to check whether the instrumentation actually works. First off, the Lean compiler aggressively optimizes function calls away when all their arguments are known at compile time. Simply writing a program that applies `insertionSort` to a large array is not sufficient, because the resulting compiled code may contain only the sorted array as a constant. The easiest way to ensure that the compiler doesn't optimize away the sorting routine is to read the array from `stdin`. Secondly, the compiler performs dead code elimination. Adding extra `let`s to the program won't necessarily result in more references in running code if the `let`-bound variables are never used. To ensure that the extra reference is not eliminated entirely, it's important to ensure that the extra reference is somehow used.
The first step in testing the instrumentation is to write `getLines`, which reads an array of lines from standard input:
`def getLines : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") ([Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let stdin ← [IO.getStdin](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___getStdin "Documentation for IO.getStdin in Lean Language Reference")   let mut lines : [Array](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___mk "Documentation for Array in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference") := #[]   let mut currLine ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")   while !currLine.[isEmpty](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___isEmpty "Documentation for String.isEmpty in Lean Language Reference") do      -- Drop trailing newline:     lines := lines.[push](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___push "Documentation for Array.push in Lean Language Reference") (currLine.[dropRight](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___dropRight "Documentation for String.dropRight in Lean Language Reference") 1)     currLine ← stdin.[getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")   [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") lines`
`[IO.FS.Stream.getLine](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Files___-File-Handles___-and-Streams/#IO___FS___Stream___mk "Documentation for IO.FS.Stream.getLine in Lean Language Reference")` returns a complete line of text, including the trailing newline. It returns `""` when the end-of-file marker has been reached.
Next, two separate `main` routines are needed. Both read the array to be sorted from standard input, ensuring that the calls to `insertionSort` won't be replaced by their return values at compile time. Both then print to the console, ensuring that the calls to `insertionSort` won't be optimized away entirely. One of them prints only the sorted array, while the other prints both the sorted array and the original array. The second function should trigger a warning that `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")` had to allocate a new array:
`def mainUnique : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let lines ← getLines   for line in insertionSort lines do     [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") line  def mainShared : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [Unit](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/The-Unit-Type/#Unit "Documentation for Unit in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   let lines ← getLines   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "--- Sorted lines: ---"   for line in insertionSort lines do     [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") line    [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") ""   [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "--- Original data: ---"   for line in lines do     [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") line`
The actual `main` simply selects one of the two main actions based on the provided command-line arguments:
`def main (args : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") [String](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Strings/#String___mk "Documentation for String in Lean Language Reference")) : [IO](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Logical-Model/#IO "Documentation for IO in Lean Language Reference") [UInt32](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Fixed-Precision-Integers/#UInt32___ofBitVec "Documentation for UInt32 in Lean Language Reference") := [do](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/Syntax/#Lean___Parser___Term___do "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   match args with   | ["--shared"] => [mainShared](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Insertion-Sort-and-Array-Mutation/#mainShared "Definition of example"); [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 0   | ["--unique"] => [mainUnique](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Insertion-Sort-and-Array-Mutation/#mainUnique "Definition of example"); [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 0   | _ =>     [IO.println](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/IO/Console-Output/#IO___println "Documentation for IO.println in Lean Language Reference") "Expected single argument, either \"--shared\" or \"--unique\""     [pure](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Functors___-Monads-and--do--Notation/#Pure___mk "Documentation for Pure.pure in Lean Language Reference") 1`
Running it with no arguments produces the expected usage information:
`sort``Expected single argument, either "--shared" or "--unique" `
The file `test-data` contains the following rocks:
File: `test-data``schist``feldspar``diorite``pumice``obsidian``shale``gneiss``marble``flint`
Using the instrumented insertion sort on these rocks results them being printed in alphabetical order:
`sort --unique < test-data``diorite feldspar flint gneiss marble obsidian pumice schist shale `
However, the version in which a reference is retained to the original array results in a notification on `stderr` (namely, `shared RC array to swap`) from the first call to `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")`:
`sort --shared < test-data``--- Sorted lines: --- diorite feldspar flint gneiss marble obsidian pumice schist shale  --- Original data: --- schist feldspar diorite pumice obsidian shale gneiss marble flint ``shared RC array to swap `
The fact that only a single `shared RC` notification appears means that the array is copied only once. This is because the copy that results from the call to `[Array.swap](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Arrays/#Array___swap "Documentation for Array.swap in Lean Language Reference")` is itself unique, so no further copies need to be made. In an imperative language, subtle bugs can result from forgetting to explicitly copy an array before passing it by reference. When running `sort --shared`, the array is copied as needed to preserve the pure functional meaning of Lean programs, but no more.
##  8.6.5. Other Opportunities for Mutation
The use of mutation instead of copying when references are unique is not limited to array update operators. Lean also attempts to “recycle” constructors whose reference counts are about to fall to zero, reusing them instead of allocating new data. This means, for instance, that `[List.map](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___map "Documentation for List.map in Lean Language Reference")` will mutate a linked list in place, at least in cases when nobody could possibly notice. One of the most important steps in optimizing hot loops in Lean code is making sure that the data being modified is not referred to from multiple locations.
##  8.6.6. Exercises[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=insertion-sort-exercises "Permalink")
  * Write a function that reverses arrays. Test that if the input array has a reference count of one, then your function does not allocate a new array.
  * Implement either merge sort or quicksort for arrays. Prove that your implementation terminates, and test that it doesn't allocate more arrays than expected. This is a challenging exercise! 

[←8.5. Bounded Numbers](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Bounded-Numbers/#Fin "8.5. Bounded Numbers")[8.7. Special Types→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Special-Types/#runtime-special-types "8.7. Special Types")

title: More Inequalities
markdown:
[←8.3. Arrays and Termination](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Arrays-and-Termination/#array-termination "8.3. Arrays and Termination")[8.5. Bounded Numbers→](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/Bounded-Numbers/#Fin "8.5. Bounded Numbers")
#  8.4. More Inequalities[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=more-inequalities "Permalink")
Lean's built-in proof automation is sufficient to check that `arrayMapHelper` and `findHelper` terminate. All that was needed was to provide an expression whose value decreases with each recursive call. However, Lean's built-in automation is not magic, and it often needs some help.
##  8.4.1. Merge Sort[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=merge-sort "Permalink")
One example of a function whose termination proof is non-trivial is merge sort on `[List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference")`. Merge sort consists of two phases: first, a list is split in half. Each half is sorted using merge sort, and then the results are merged using a function that combines two sorted lists into a larger sorted list. The base cases are the empty list and the singleton list, both of which are already considered to be sorted.
To merge two sorted lists, there are two basic cases to consider:
  1. If one of the input lists is empty, then the result is the other list.
  2. If both lists are non-empty, then their heads should be compared. The result of the function is the smaller of the two heads, followed by the result of merging the remaining entries of both lists.


This is not structurally recursive on either list. The recursion terminates because an entry is removed from one of the two lists in each recursive call, but it could be either list. Behind the scenes, Lean uses this fact to prove that it terminates:
`def merge [[Ord](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ord___mk "Documentation for Ord in Lean Language Reference") α] (xs : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) (ys : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") xs, ys [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [], _ => ys   | _, [] => xs   | x'::xs', y'::ys' =>     [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") [Ord.compare](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ord___mk "Documentation for Ord.compare in Lean Language Reference") x' y' [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")     | [.lt](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ordering___lt "Documentation for Ordering.lt in Lean Language Reference") | [.eq](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ordering___lt "Documentation for Ordering.eq in Lean Language Reference") => x' :: [merge](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#merge "Definition of example") xs' (y' :: ys')     | [.gt](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ordering___lt "Documentation for Ordering.gt in Lean Language Reference") => y' :: [merge](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#merge "Definition of example") (x'::xs') ys'`
A simple way to split a list is to add each entry in the input list to two alternating output lists:
`def splitList (lst : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) : ([List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α × [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α) :=   [match](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference") lst [with](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Terms/Pattern-Matching/#Lean___Parser___Term___match "Documentation for syntax in Lean Language Reference")   | [] => ([], [])   | x :: xs =>     let (a, b) := splitList xs     (x :: b, a)`
This splitting function is structurally recursive.
Merge sort checks whether a base case has been reached. If so, it returns the input list. If not, it splits the input, and merges the result of sorting each half:
`def `fail to show termination for   mergeSort with errors failed to infer structural recursion: Not considering parameter α of mergeSort:   it is unchanged in the recursive calls Not considering parameter #2 of mergeSort:   it is unchanged in the recursive calls Cannot use parameter xs:   failed to eliminate recursive application     mergeSort halves.[fst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.fst in Lean Language Reference")   Could not find a decreasing measure. The basic measures relate at each recursive call as follows: (<, ≤, =: relation proved, ? all proofs failed, _: no proof attempted)             xs #1 1) 70:11-31  ?  ? 2) 70:34-54  _  _  #1: xs.length  Please use `termination_by` to specify a decreasing measure.`mergeSort [[Ord](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Type-Classes/Basic-Classes/#Ord___mk "Documentation for Ord in Lean Language Lean's pattern match compiler is able to tell that the assumption `h` introduced by the `if` that tests whether `xs.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") < 2` rules out lists longer than one entry, so there is no “missing cases” error. However, even though this program always terminates, it is not structurally recursive, and Lean is unable to automatically discover a decreasing measure:

```

2) 70:34-54  _  _

#1: xs.length

The reason it terminates is that `splitList` always returns lists that are shorter than its input, at least when applied to lists that contain at least two elements. Thus, the length of `halves.[fst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.fst in Lean Language Reference")` and `halves.[snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference")` are less than the length of `xs`. ##  8.4.2. Splitting a List Makes it Shorter[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=splitting-shortens "Permalink")
It will also be necessary to prove that `(splitList xs).snd.length < xs.length`. Because `splitList` alternates between adding entries to the two lists, it is easiest to prove both statements at once, so the structure of the proof can follow the algorithm used to implement `splitList`. In other words, it is easiest to prove that `∀(lst : [List](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List in Lean Language Reference") α), (splitList lst).[fst](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.fst in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") < lst.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") ∧ (splitList lst).[snd](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Tuples/#Prod___mk "Documentation for Prod.snd in Lean Language Reference").[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference") < lst.[length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")`.
Unfortunately, the statement is false. In particular, `splitList []` is `([], [])`. Both output lists have length `0`, which is not less than `0`, the length of the input list. Similarly, `splitList ["basalt"]` evaluates to `(["basalt"], [])`, and `["basalt"]` is not shorter than `["basalt"]`. However, `splitList ["basalt", "granite"]` evaluates to `(["basalt"], ["granite"])`, and both of these output lists are shorter than the input list.
It turns out that the lengths of the output lists are always less than or equal to the length of the input list, but they are only strictly shorter when the input list contains at least two entries. It turns out to be easiest to prove the former statement, then extend it to the latter statement. Begin with a theorem statement:

Because `splitList` is structurally recursive on the list, the proof should use induction. The structural recursion in `splitList` fits a proof by induction perfectly: the base case of the induction matches the base case of the recursion, and the inductive step matches the recursive call. The goal for the `[nil](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.nil in Lean Language Reference")` case can be proved by invoking the simplifier and instructing it to unfold the definition of `splitList`, because the length of the empty list is less than or equal to the length of the empty list. Similarly, simplifying with `splitList` in the `[cons](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___nil "Documentation for List.cons in Lean Language Reference")` case places `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")` around the lengths in the goal:

This is because the call to `[List.length](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Linked-Lists/#List___length "Documentation for List.length in Lean Language Reference")` consumes the head of the list `x :: xs`, converting it to a `[Nat.succ](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat.succ in Lean Language Reference")`, in both the length of the input list and the length of the first output list.
Writing `A ∧ B` in Lean is short for `[And](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Propositions/Logical-Connectives/#And___intro "Documentation for And in Lean Language Reference") A B`. `And` is a structure type in the `Prop` universe:
`structure And (a b : Prop) : Prop where   intro ::   left : a   right : b`
In other words, a proof of `A ∧ B` consists of the `[And.intro](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#AndDef___And___intro "Definition of example")` constructor applied to a proof of `A` in the `left` field and a proof of `B` in the `right` field.
The `cases` tactic allows a proof to consider each constructor of a datatype or each potential proof of a proposition in turn. It corresponds to a `match` expression without recursion. Using `cases` on a structure results in the structure being broken apart, with an assumption added for each field of the structure, just as a pattern match expression extracts the field of a structure for use in a program. Because structures have only one constructor, using `cases` on a structure does not result in additional goals.
Because `ih` is a proof of `List.length (splitList xs).fst ≤ List.length xs ∧ List.length (splitList xs).snd ≤ List.length xs`, using `[cases](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Tactic-Proofs/Tactic-Reference/#cases "Documentation for tactic in Lean Language Reference") ih` results in an assumption that `List.length (splitList xs).fst ≤ List.length xs` and an assumption that `List.length (splitList xs).snd ≤ List.length xs`:

The `right` goal resembles the `right✝` assumption, except the goal adds a `+ 1` only to the length of the input list. It's time to prove that the inequality holds.
###  8.4.2.1. Adding One to the Greater Side[🔗](find/?domain=Verso.Genre.Manual.section&name=le-succ-of-le "Permalink")
The inequality needed to prove `[splitList_shorter_le](Programming___-Proving___-and-Performance/More-Inequalities/#splitList_shorter_le "Definition of example")` is `∀(n m : [Nat](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___zero "Documentation for Nat in Lean Language Reference")), n ≤ m → n ≤ m + 1`. The incoming assumption that `n ≤ m` essentially tracks the difference between `n` and `m` in the number of `[Nat.le.step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference")` constructors. Thus, the proof should add an extra `[Nat.le.step](https://lean-lang.org/doc/reference/4.26.0/Basic-Types/Natural-Numbers/#Nat___le___refl "Documentation for Nat.le.step in Lean Language Reference")` in the base case.

To reveal what's going on behind the scenes, the `apply` and `exact` tactics can be used to indicate exactly which constructor is being applied.
